settings.welcome: Hi! I'm TranscriptBot. Ask me anything about the videos.

introduction.name: Welcome to your learning transformation.txt
introduction.content: Welcome to the I Can Study program. I'm Dr. Justin Sung, the co-founder and head of learning here. In these first few videos, I want to quickly help you to understand what to expect from this program and how you can get the most out of it to become a better, more efficient learner. Whether that means getting better grades or entering or navigating through your dream career pathway, or even just being more confident with your current learning goals. And I know that right now, if you're anything like I used to be, you probably want to get straight into learning about the techniques. And I will teach you the techniques, but there's something even more important than the techniques that I wish I had known when I was first starting off on my journey. The truth is... There is no such thing as the best studying technique. Back when I was trying to enter into medical school, I thought about learning as just a series of techniques. And so being a good learner just meant having a better technique. So what I would end up doing is just picking up these new methods of writing notes or doing flashcards. And the good news is that I did manage to enter into medical school. The bad news is that I had to sacrifice a lot. I was studying 20 hours a day, seven days a week, extremely sleep deprived. I was mentally shattered, emotionally just very fragile. I had, I entered into a severe depression. which actually took me over three years to recover from. And needless to say, I was completely, completely burnt out, beyond burnt out. But some of you might be thinking that you will gladly go through that sacrifice if it means that you're going to achieve your goals. And I can admire if you are that motivated. But here's what I wish someone had told me, which is that if that's how you're thinking, You shouldn't think that way. I did not successfully enter medical school because I worked so hard and because of what I did. I... got lucky. I have friends that did just as much. I got in, they didn't. They sacrificed just as much, more even. Some of them damaged the relationship that they had with their family in that process. And for those people, they didn't end up with a success story ending. And the reason I'm sharing this story with you is because I want you to realize that Even though I had a successful outcome, my story is not a success story. I'm someone that did a lot of the wrong things and I was lucky enough that for me it worked. And yes, I did work hard, really hard. But if just working hard is all that mattered, then it would be easy. Working hard is not that hard, ironically. And this became really clear to me when I entered into medical school. And I remember being in medical school and I'm just surrounded by like incredibly intelligent people. And I was looking down at... five years of medical school, realizing after the first week of lectures in medical school, that this was way harder than the previous year. Like if I was studying 20 hours a day beforehand, there's no way I can do like, what, 40 hours a day now in medical school. So I had to find a way to get through that. And also, have a life, not just study. I didn't want to spend the best years of my life just locked away in a library. And that's actually what it felt like sometimes. I would be sitting there in the library studying and trying to figure things out. And I'd look out the window into the main building. And then I'd see all these other students, my classmates. They're just socializing. They're having fun. They're just chatting. They're talking about where they're going to go out. And for me, I'm just thinking, how do you manage to do so well when I am here? I feel like barely hanging on doing my best. So here's what I realized after that interview. process of trying to figure things out and learning what it means to really be a great learner that has confidence, that performs consistently, and is able to tackle these, you know, competitive, difficult learning goals, but can do so without risk and sacrificing everything. A great learner is not someone that has the best techniques. It's actually the other way around. A great learner is someone who can succeed even without the best techniques. A great learner doesn't need to study as much. They don't need. every new fancy app on learning because they have the skills to use their brain effectively. But learning is a complicated skill. It's one of the most complicated skills that you can ever learn. So just to make sure that you have your expectations clear, I want to be very transparent with you. This is not something that happens overnight. It takes time. If you are planning on improving your learning over the next one week so that you can ace that next exam coming up and right now you're struggling, that's not realistic. If you've got 10 years of bad habits around learning, you're not going to unlearn and overlearn new habits in 10 days. And so if that's what you're looking for, this program is not going to be right for you. For me, without anyone guiding me. It took me easily over 10,000 hours spread across five years of constant trial and error and experimentation and reading through research to figure this out. often very confusing research. Which brings me to another point, which is that learning to become a really efficient learner is not this roadmap, the secret that has kind of been fully researched and figured out and like, it's just here. Even if you look at the learning science research that's being published today, and you look at what they say are like the most effective techniques, and you look at the stats, They say it's like 50 to 60% of the people found it pretty effective. It's like, okay, so you're telling me that the most effective technique there still is not working for 40% of people. And it's not to say that the technique doesn't work, but it's to say that there are so many things that influence whether something is successful or not. As a very easy example, let's say you teach someone a really, really good technique, but they procrastinate like crazy. They don't get the benefit of that technique. Or even if they're not procrastinating, maybe they just don't apply the technique in quite the right way. It's just so nuanced. And this nuance is the reason why it took me not only five years personal experience, but then seven years coaching people, reading through that research to try to figure out how to teach people these skills in a way that's more than 50, 60% consistency. And so all this to say, I can promise you three things. First of all, you will not be able to improve your learning ability overnight. If that's what you're expecting, this isn't for you. Number two, however, you will be able to improve. I can promise you that. I am confident to say every week you should be making improvements that realistically get you closer to your goal. And every single week you will become a better and better learner. And the final promise is that that process of getting better will be much faster than it took me or it would take you without this guidance. If you follow our advice, you will be able to improve at 10, 20 times the speed that I improved at. There are a lot of these complicated nuances I had to figure out. I figured that amount already for you and packaged them. But in order for that improvement to happen and for us to be in a position that we can really help you, We have to have a deeper relationship. We need to be able to trust that you will do your best to learn what we teach you because there's no way for us to watch over your shoulder. This is an online delivery that's happening right now. You could go through, watch every single video, click next, next, next, next, next, next, next, and think, Why am I not improving? Of course you're not going to improve. We can't stop you from doing that. So we're going to give you these lessons and tell you to do certain things and give you activities and things to practice. And we have to trust that you will do that, that you will follow that advice and those instructions. But in order for that to happen, you need to trust that what we have to say is legitimate, that it's valuable and that it will help you. Because sometimes the advice that we give and the instructions that we give you, they're not gonna be what you expect. The way I think about learning now is completely different to how I used to think about learning before. And the things that I think are important now 10 years ago, I wouldn't have known why this is so important. So if someone told me to do this thing, I would have been like, why? I don't get it. It seems less important than learning this other technique. And to think and feel that way is completely normal. And I'm sure you're going to have that feeling as well. But in those situations, you need to be able to trust that we've got your back. And it's a two-way relationship. I think it's very important. And I want you to feel that that on this journey of learning to learn, that you feel safe, that you feel supported, that you can ask us for help, and that we will be there to help you without judgment. I want you to know that you are now part of a community that has got your back. We are all on the same journey. We're just at different stages of it. So to help you get a little bit more of an understanding of who we are, I actually want to spend just a couple of minutes actually telling you about who we are as like the people behind the program and also like why we made the program in the first place. So who are we? At the end of the day, Our entire team, the iConsider team, we are first and foremost educators. And we treat that word educator with a lot of responsibility. For me, I left my job as a medical doctor to do this kind of work full time. After spending, after sacrificing so much to enter into medical school and to get through medical school and the sacrifice continually, why did I leave that? to pursue this, being a learning coach. A learning coach is not even a profession that existed. I'll be honest, the first, the reason I initially got into coaching was just because I wanted to make some money while I was going through medical school. Like, you know, I got into medical school, I did a bit of tutoring, I liked teaching, I was passionate about it. And what I quickly realized was that the students that are doing well keep doing well and the students that are struggling are still struggling. It's just now they're wasting money paying me to tutor them on things. But the real issue is why did they even need me to tutor them in the first place? During this time, I was exploring the learning science a lot. I was starting to really get into that idea of like, what does it mean to be an efficient learner? And so I was learning a lot about myself and how to learn effectively. And I started teaching this to my students that I was tutoring at the time. And then a couple of years in, I decided to create a nonprofit for trying to teach these skills without the subject tutoring. I thought, well, I really feel like 99% of the difference is made through just having the skills. So maybe I can just teach the skills directly. So I created this free mentoring program that we would run in high school. for students from lower socioeconomic backgrounds. And this is actually how I ended up gaining so much experience with learning coaching because I was working with so many students from high school through to university, through to graduation, just teaching them these skills and seeing how they found it and how it affects their life and their ability to succeed. And the powerful thing I've realized, which is what helped convince me to move away from medicine, was that I realized that this thing of learning to learn is not just like, it's not just a skill. It's not just like, hey, here's how you can study. Our ability to learn defines what we believe we can achieve in life. There is so much of ourselves and our identity that we place into how smart we think we are. And a lot of that is based on just our academic performance. And I would work with these incredible, intelligent, like... really like inspirational young students who had so much potential, but they thought of themselves as someone that just can't do this. I can't be a doctor. I can't, you know, be an engineer. I can't do this because my school grades have said that I can't do that. But you are more than your exam result. Your exam performance is, does not determine your worth as a person. Whether you do well or badly in an exam, it doesn't mean you are a better or a worse person. Intelligence is not something that is fixed. It isn't something that's just assigned to you at birth and then you're like bound to it by destiny. Intelligence is... just a series of thinking habits that can be unlearned and relearned and then trained. And because your intelligence is basically just a skill that can be trained, a series of habits, it means that your exams and your past performance are not a reflection of who you are or what you're capable of. They're a reflection of what you did at the time. And helping people realize this and make that transformation, for me at least, gave me more fulfillment and satisfaction than I had treating a debilitating disease as a medical doctor, for me at least. But that is just my story. The iCanStudy team is more than just myself. But we are bound by the common thread that we all resonate with the message that I've just said. We are all educators with decades of experience under our belts. And while each one of us has our own individual story, we were all brought together by the common realization that people need to learn these skills and that the way that it is often taught in the mainstream is not enough. So that's why this program exists, because we wanted to give you these skills that can help you feel this way and have this transformation and not just give you the confidence that you can walk into an exam and do well, but that you can achieve whatever goal that you want and that you have the confidence to go out and really try for that goal. We want you to feel like you can learn anything and do anything because we know you can. And don't get me wrong, hard work is still necessary. Whatever goal you've got set for yourself, I'm sure it's not an easy one. But hard work only leads to results when it's put in the right places. So that's where we are coming from. Whatever your goal is, whatever your previous experience is, whatever your learning ability is, whatever stage of learner you are right now, this program is for you. We will teach you the tools that we know work through experience and have been shown through a deep examination of the research that will help you to carve your way towards your goals.

introduction.name: Golden rule of learning.txt
introduction.content: To start you off, I want to teach you something that I call the golden rule of learning. And I call it the golden rule because it's something that I've seen consistently in our top performing learners. And it's also repeated countless times in the research. But despite that, it's also something that I've noticed most people don't seem to never really think about ever, which directly makes it harder and more stressful to reach your goals. I'm going to teach this golden rule to you now upfront, because I want you to remember this for the rest of your life. For everything you learn, for everything we teach you in every lesson that follows this, I want this to be there in your mind. The golden rule is to ruthlessly focus on your locus of control. The locus of control is a term commonly used in psychology research. And in short, it means the stuff that you can control to get the outcome that you want. And when it comes to learning to learn, this is crucial. Let's say you've got this really difficult exam. that you wanna do well in. There are so many factors that influence your chance of successfully doing well in this exam. It isn't just as simple as study more and you'll do well. Yes, effort is one of those factors, but if you study lots using bad techniques, then you probably not gonna do as well as someone studying lots and using good techniques. And even outside of studying, things like your level of focus, how much you procrastinate or how easily you get distracted. All of these things influence our ability to reach our goals. And here's the unfortunate reality. We can't control all of those things. All we can do is put ourselves in the best position to succeed, to give ourselves the best chance possible. So focusing on the locus of control means that we're thinking about what are the things that we can actually do that we can control that puts us in that best position. When we don't focus on this locus of control, then we spend a lot of time trying to figure out how to achieve the goal. We spend a lot of time getting distracted, trying all sorts of different things. We're very busy, we're very worried, and we waste valuable time and effort. And so this goal we're trying to reach stays difficult and far away. Trying to reach it is risky. And so you can see here, our focus is very scattered. Whereas when we are focusing on the locus of control, it means that for the same amount of effort that we put in, we can make more productive, in fact, a surprising amount of progress towards our goals. So here's how I want you to think about achieving your goals from now on. Think about that goal that you want to achieve. It might be an exam score, entering into a degree, navigating through a job, your dream outcome. Think about that. You cannot control that. Recognize that outcome is not within your locus of control. Outcomes cannot be controlled. Just because you really want to succeed doesn't mean you will. Otherwise, The more you worry, the more you win. But I'm sure you've had situations in the past where you did really want an outcome and you were worried about it and you didn't get the outcome. Outcomes are a symptom of the process. And it sounds scary. I know. but we have to feel it and we have to accept it. Because only when we've realized that that's not something we can control, do we free ourselves up to then turn our attention to the things that we can control, AKA the processes that we use. And when the processes are right, and we will teach you those processes, then it means you've got the best chance to succeed. You're in that best position. And it may not mean that your chance of success is 100%. It probably won't be, but it's as good as it gets. And that rest, the uncertainty, the what ifs, That's just life. And we have to learn to be able to deal with that and be okay with that. So with that being said, in the next video, I'll go over how we should start thinking about these processes themselves. What is the right process? And then how can we start engaging in the right processes to upgrade the way that we learn?
chunks: To start you off, I want to teach you something that I call the golden rule of learning. And I call it the golden rule because it's something that I've seen consistently in our top performing learners. And it's also repeated countless times in the research. But despite that, it's also something that I've noticed most people don't seem to never really think about ever, which directly makes it harder and more stressful to reach your goals. I'm going to teach this golden rule to you now upfront, because I want you to remember this for the rest of your life. For everything you learn, for everything we teach you in every lesson that follows this, I want this to be there in your mind. The golden rule is to ruthlessly focus on your locus of control. The locus of control is a term commonly used in psychology research. And in short, it means the stuff that you can control to get the outcome that you want. And when it comes to learning to learn, this is crucial. Let's say you've got this really difficult exam. that you wanna do well in. There are so many factors that influence your chance of successfully doing well in this exam. It isn't just as simple as study more and you'll do well. Yes, effort is one of those factors, but if you study lots using bad techniques, then you probably not gonna do as well as someone studying lots and using good techniques. And even outside of studying, things like your level of focus, how much you procrastinate or how easily you get distracted. All of these things influence our ability to reach our goals. And here's the unfortunate reality. We can't control all of those things. All we can do is put ourselves in the best position to succeed, to give ourselves the best chance possible. So focusing on the locus of control means that we're thinking about what are the things that we can actually do that we can control that puts us in that best position. When we don't focus on this locus of control, then we spend a lot of time trying to figure out how to achieve the goal. We spend a lot of time getting distracted, trying all sorts of different things. We're very busy, we're very worried, and we waste valuable time and effort. And

And so this goal we're trying to reach stays difficult and far away. Trying to reach it is risky. And so you can see here, our focus is very scattered. Whereas when we are focusing on the locus of control, it means that for the same amount of effort that we put in, we can make more productive, in fact, a surprising amount of progress towards our goals. So here's how I want you to think about achieving your goals from now on. Think about that goal that you want to achieve. Outcomes cannot be controlled. Just because you really want to succeed doesn't mean you will. Otherwise, The more you worry, the more you win. introduction.name: The perspective of top performers.txt
introduction.content: So we want to get better grades, or we want to be better at using our knowledge for work, and we want to remember more of what we learned. These are the outcomes. And remember, outcomes are not the things we control. So what are the processes that lead to those outcomes? Well, there is a massive amount of research on this topic, and I've spent years going through all of this stuff so that you don't have to. And there are a couple of interesting things that I realized about this. The first thing, which was interesting to me, is that the actual processes themselves are actually not the hardest part. Yes, it's not straightforward always. Some of the techniques and processes do require diligent practice. But in my years of coaching and looking at who are the students that are successful and who are the ones that are not, remember how I said even in the latest research, even the most successful techniques and processes the success rate of those is only like 50 to 60%. And those are the most successful. Like your average typical learning effective technique that's been researched out there, most of the stats are showing like 20, 30% effect. And the thing that clicked for me and seeing it with my own eyes and the students that I was working with noticing what are the really successful students doing that all the other students are consistently not. I realized that there are two very fundamental perspectives about learning that you have to have before you even start learning any techniques or processes in the first place. And the first and what I think is actually the most fundamental perspective that you need to have is to think about learning in systems rather than techniques. As I said, there's no such thing as the best technique. Every technique has pros and cons and strengths and limitations. And the idea is that you use a technique that has a certain benefit in some angle for some topic and you use that and then you balance the weaknesses with a different technique. And it's about layering these techniques in the right way to create an entire system that produces an effective learner. If you just use one technique or like a couple techniques and you use them perfectly, if those two techniques have the same gap between them, you're actually not going to do very well. If you only use one thing and you use lots of it, doing more of the same thing doesn't reduce the gaps. Like the gaps are still there. And... having real confidence in your learning ability is actually about understanding what are the strengths and limitations and pros and cons of each of these techniques and how do I layer them together to create that system? Because it means that if something changes in your environment, the challenge, the difficulty of an exam, the amount of time availability that you have, your specific weakness with regards to that topic, You can think about these variables and then you can look at the techniques you're using and you can adapt it. You can mold it. You become more flexible. That's what gives you real confidence because you know, regardless of the challenge, you can make it work. So the analogy I often say is to think about learning like a toolkit, like you're building a house. It doesn't matter how good your hammer is or your screwdriver or your drill. It's not about having the best of each thing. It's about having a comprehensive toolkit where you know the right way to use each tool. So for learning, each of us already comes to the table with a toolkit, a series of learning habits that we've picked up. Some of the tools work and we keep them or we upgrade them and make them even better. Some of the tools are getting a little outdated. They need a bit of maintenance or refreshing. And some of the tools are just, we don't need them at all. And we should just get rid of them because it's just making our toolkit heavier. And that is in a nutshell, basically what we're going to be doing in this program. We're going to be going through your toolkit, showing you how to think about a toolkit, what you need in your toolkit, what the best tool for each part is in that toolkit, helping you examine what you've got and then helping you remove and replace it. So that's the first perspective. You have to think about learning in systems rather than in techniques. Otherwise, you're not going to make a lot of good progress with learning to learn. The second perspective is to open the black box. Think about this. If you are racing a car, and you want to be able to make that car go as fast as possible, you need to be able to open up that hood and know what you're looking at. The exact same thing is true for learning, except the engine of the car is our brain. Back years and years and years ago, when I was studying 20 hours a day trying to enter into medical school, I didn't know almost anything about how the brain works or how learning happens. For me, all I knew was... how to steer and how to push on the accelerator, like what to study, what topic to study, and then how much to study it, how hard to study, hours. Like that's all I knew. And unless like the engine of learning was like rampantly on fire, which I mean, you know what? It probably was given that I had to study 20 hours a day. I was entering into severe depression. I was even starting to hallucinate because of the sleep deprivation. It probably was on fire. So you know what? Even when it was on fire, I didn't even realize it was on fire. But as you can probably imagine, like thinking about driving a car that's on fire, the performance is not exactly going to be something that instills confidence in you or is particularly consistent or particularly sustainable. There was actually a test that I studied like hundreds of hours for, right? I was doing my crazy amounts of studying. I had two tests. One was on a Monday. The other one was on a Thursday. The test on the Monday, I got 93%. Pretty good. The test on Thursday, I got a 78%. And I will tell you, the version of me, 17 years old, that where that is like, I felt like my world was ending like 78%. How do I recover from that? And you know what? Like in the next exam, I actually did recover from it because I got a 97% in the next exam. And this is what I meant is like, that was honestly just luck because I studied just as hard. I don't think I changed how I studied pretty much at all because I just happened to get questions in the exam that lined up more with what I studied. Like I really feel like it was a fluke. In a parallel universe, I just bombed that exam as well. So those of you out there that say that, hey, what I teach doesn't work because I'm just a genius. I'm not a genius. A genius doesn't study 20 hours a day to get 78% in an exam. The thing that helped me turn myself around to get to a point where I was able to get through medical school, multiple distinctions throughout medical school, go into a master of education, finish it in a 40th of the time, get top of my class, 100% of my exams while working full-time. The thing that gave me the confidence to be able to do that and not be surprised about it was this, opening the black box of learning. The black box of learning is opening the hood and knowing what's inside. The black box refers to a situation where there is an input and there is an output, and there is something that happens in the middle, which is the black box. And this is the mysterious set of processes that happen. And a very common example is like using your phone. You know, you can pick up your phone and you, you know, you tap around it and do stuff with it all the time. But do you actually know how it works? The technology behind what makes a phone actually work. Like, I don't really know how a touchscreen actually works, but I use it all the time. That's a black box. For a phone, you don't need to really know how your phone works unless you're becoming a phone engineer. but for learning, we have to know how our brain works because we are learning engineers. That's what getting better at learning actually means. And when you don't think about learning about how the brain works and you're just focused on what's the technique, I don't care about this, Justin, you're talking too much about this stuff that feels irrelevant to me. Just tell me what the technique is. If that's what you're thinking, You need to open the black box because what's going to happen is that you'll learn a technique and you won't know why it works and you won't know why it doesn't work or when to use it. It's kind of like me just giving you a tool and then you just saying, cool, I've got the tool now, therefore all my problems are solved. A tool is useless in the hands of someone that doesn't know how to use it. A good learner is able to take a technique, apply it to a situation and then observe how it works and make adjustments to it. That's what's called self-regulated learning. And when we don't open the black box, this is what creates anxiety, stress, frustration, and demotivation. Because we're doing stuff. We're putting in time. We're putting in effort. There's lots of things going in, but what we're getting out of it is sometimes good, is sometimes bad. And when we do a lot and we have inconsistency or bad results, this is what leads us to think, what's going wrong? Where's the problem? Is the problem me? This is what creates anxiety. And so how do we compensate for that anxiety? Well, we don't know what goes inside of the box. We don't know what they're actually fixed. We don't know what the process is. We don't know why this is working one day and it's not working another day, or we don't know how to use it properly. So all we know is to just put in more time and put in more effort. And again, when we put in more time and more effort, again, well, the box is exactly the same. So the product is exactly the same. Inconsistent, sometimes bad. So it fuels into this more and more, and it creates this loop of frustration and anxiety and demotivation. This is the experience of learning for some people for their entire lives. And I resonate with this so strongly because I felt this every single day when I was studying so much and seeing the people around me do better than me, and I just didn't know why. The trick to escaping that cycle is opening the black box. Understand what's going on inside. Know the processes. Understand a little bit of the theory of the brain. You don't have to become a learning scientist, but know some of these basic principles. Understand how the techniques... And those principles align together. And now you are a learning engineer. Even if it doesn't work the first time, you know what you can do to change it, to make it work eventually. And it is these two perspectives and the behaviors and the decisions that arise from people that think about learning like this that creates a successful learner. And for the people that aren't thinking about learning in this way, I've seen it happen thousands of times. those people will struggle to develop their skills. They will struggle to be a sustainably successful learner. And maybe right now it's easy enough that they can get away with not having to think about it like this, but when they do need to increase their skills and they do need to meet the bar, which is higher than where it is right now, they will struggle. So the next lesson, we're going to be opening up that black box. I'll teach you what I think are the most important processes and then how you should start thinking about learning in systems so that we can start designing it.

introduction.name: The black box of learning.txt
introduction.content: In this lesson, I'm going to teach you how to think about learning and building a learning system and the key processes that you need to get down, no matter what type of learning you need to do. This is for the rest of your life. Now, I can't go into full detail on all of the key processes. This is more a distilled package of the last 10 years of what I've learned about learning and what the research says about learning. If I had known this 13 years ago when I first started, then this would have saved me probably two or three years of trial and error. Now, because as I mentioned, learning is very nuanced and very complicated, I can't go into everything. So what I'm choosing to include in this lesson are the things that I think the most important foundational piece of knowledge that you need to have that are going to help you to become the best most efficient learner as soon as possible and to become an efficient learner in the shortest possible time you need to have a sophisticated understanding of how learning works because the fastest path to becoming a great learner is actually not that intuitive because of how complicated learning is and so once you understand the this, the way the program is actually structured and why we teach certain things in the way that we teach them should make more sense to you. And it means that later we can just focus on going into the full detail on each individual specific process, trusting that you have an understanding of the big picture of where we're trying to get to and how you should sort of be thinking about these lessons and how you're building the system yourself. So let's get into this. The most basic and I think useful way of thinking about learning is to think about it in terms of information that comes in and it enters into our brain. This is a little brain there. And then based on this, we have certain outputs. And the outputs are memory and mastery. Memory is an obvious one. This is basically how much do you remember? We can also call this retention. Mastery is a little bit different because this actually refers to the quality of what you're learning. So for example, learning something at a low level of quality or a low level of mastery is something we often call lower order learning. It means that at the end of it, you're actually only able to solve very, very simple problems or or recite facts and regurgitate facts and figures. Or you can just explain individual concepts. Whereas having a higher level of mastery, which we call higher order learning. So I'll write those down as well. So we can have lower order learning, and then we can have higher order learning. And the higher orders of mastery means that you can see how ideas are connected together. You can see how things relate to each other. Things don't exist in isolation anymore. It's integrated. So the characteristic of a higher order piece of information is that the information is integrated, whereas lower order means that it's isolated. So integrated mastery would mean that you can solve a complicated problem that involves lots of different concepts relating to each other. You can answer problems that have a lot of conceptual nuance and like very, very contextual, very specific, very detailed. And it means that also like, for example, if you're writing an essay, you're able to write essays that link these ideas together in a flow and a logic that makes a lot of sense. Most of the time when we're talking about someone that is a genius, is able to just like think at such a great level. We're talking about higher order, right? So higher order mastery is where the value for most learning is at. Now, there's this other part, which is that the mastery of learning actually affects your retention of it. And that's because of the way that memory itself actually works. So things are easier to remember and they are naturally stored in your memory longer and more easily when they're actually connected and integrated. And this is because your brain sees that it's relevant. It says, hey, it's worth holding on to this because it's connected to so many other things. I better not get rid of it. catalyst.content: Now, the first law is to lock in learning assets. And this is something that even very experienced learners often get wrong. In finance, the word asset means something that you own or control that has value and that continues to give you value in the future. For example, if I buy a plot of land, inside a busy suburb. The value of this land will continue to rise over time because there's only so much land and lots of people want to live in this suburb. So if I then sell this 20 years later, I'm going to be able to sell it for much more than I paid for it. So it's an asset because I own this land. The land has value, AKA people want to live on this land, build houses, et cetera. and it generates me value in the future without really doing anything. Just the fact that I own it means that it's generating me value. So you also need to think about learning assets. Remember, a learning system is just a combination of processes and habits that we train. And so these skills and the processes and the habits are our assets. They're our learning assets. If I have the skill of reading and remembering 90% of everything that I read, for example. That skill provides me value right now And it continues to provide me value for everything I read in the future. That means every exam I sit, every subject I study is that much easier. I have that much less problems because I have this skill. Let's contrast this with something that is not an asset. So let's say, for example, there's an exam coming up, you're struggling with it, and so you get... tons and tons and tons of private tutoring to cover this. And that person just breaks down every single topic and teaches it to you. So you're able to pass this exam. That is something that you paid for to provide you value now, but it doesn't provide you value in the future because the reason you struggled with that and the reason you weren't able to break it down yourself and the reason that you needed to pay someone else to come in and do that for you, that reason is still there. The processes and the skills and the habits you formed led you to a point that meant that you weren't able to do it. And as a result, in the future, that problem will... occur again. And so we have solved the issue now, but that's not an asset. Likewise, let's say that we just study really, really hard and do a lot of hours. That's not an asset because the reason we had to do so many hours, that is still there. Our skills and our processes and our habits required us to compensate with enough hours. Now, is the skill or the characteristic of being a hard worker an asset? Definitely, because that means that when there is hard work to be done, we have the ability to do that. And that helps us and provides us value into the future. But it doesn't mean that we want to be in a position where everything we try to do requires us to provide the maximum amount of effort. It's going to be exhausting doing it that way, especially if it's not necessary. But here's the thing. A skill or a technique that you learn only becomes an asset when we own and control it. So what does it actually mean to own or control a skill or a technique? Well, there are four requirements that I think are necessary to say that you own or control a skill in order to turn it into an asset. The first requirement is that you have to understand the skill. The second requirement is that you have to be able to remember the skill. And third, you have to be able to apply the skill correctly. And so far, this is obvious. You definitely wouldn't own or control a skill if you don't even understand the skill, or if you understood it at the time of learning about it, but then you can't remember it to actually use it, or if you can remember it, but you can't even apply it correctly in the real world. But the fourth requirement is the one that a lot of people will miss. The fourth requirement is that you have to be able to perform the skill consistently and easily. And this last one is really important because I may have a skill that I can perform correctly when I'm really focused and I'm concentrating. But that means that on a bad day, when I'm feeling more tired, when I'm a little bit more stressed, I can't perform that skill anymore. And especially for something like learning to learn or productivity, time management, Those are the situations where we need this asset the most. And so the reason that so many learners get this part wrong and fail to lock in their learning assets is because if there are a hundred different techniques that you can learn, And you want to do really, really well. It's almost intuitive to want to learn as many of those as possible. And as soon as you do that, you've violated this first law of growth, which is locking and learning assets. And remember what I said about the laws is as soon as you break the law, it gets harder. And here's why it gets harder. Every single skill takes time to learn. It takes time to understand. It takes time to be able to remember. It takes time to be able to apply it correctly. That process takes a long time because at first you apply it, you make some mistakes. You figure out what mistakes you made. You fix those mistakes. That process repeats four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten times until you can apply it correctly. And only once you can apply it correctly can you then work on applying it more consistently and then more easily. And therefore, what happens is also more quickly. And so it's only after all of this time does this become an accurate, habit that's developed. And now you own this learning asset. And only at that point have you met all four of the requirements. And so here's what actually happens in terms of the value that we receive from the skill. So I've got this graph here of time on the X axis, and this is time spent on developing a skill. And then here we've got the value that that skill is providing us. And then in the middle, we actually have this zero line where above it is positive value and below it is negative value. And this is because in the early stages of learning a skill, it's not actually providing us value. We are spending time on learning about the skill until we get to a point where we can then apply it. So understanding the skill doesn't provide value. Remembering the skill doesn't provide value. And applying a skill incorrectly doesn't provide us value. So the time it takes to understand it and then remember it and then eventually apply it correctly up until that point, this is just an investment of time to get to a point where we can then start applying it correctly in the real world so that that actually starts providing us with value. Now, in reality, it's not such a straight line. It's kind of up and down, up and down, up and down. And even once it's starting to provide value, it can also be up and down, up and down, up and down, sometimes being negative. That's why we need the fourth requirement, the ability to do this consistently and easily. It means every time we do it, it's providing us value and it's something that we can do day in, day out. And so what happens if we start trying to learn, let's say, five skills at the same time? Well, we've only got a fixed amount of time available for learning theory and for practicing, which means we only have a fixed amount of time that we can spend on learning to understand it and remembering it and then applying it correctly. So let's say just to make the math easy on this, let's say that to get to this point, it took us 10 hours. Well, if we're trying to learn five different skills, we have to divide that time by five. We're assigning two hours per skill. And so our skill grows at one fifth of the speed. And so instead of it providing us value here after 10 hours, those five skills only provide value all the way over here. And this is 50 hours. And in reality, because trying to learn multiple skills at the same time is so much harder than just focusing on one, this 50 hours is very conservative. This could be easily 100 or 200 hours. And remember, all of this time, during this time, it's not providing you value. You are just putting in time and effort, learning a skill that is not helping you or solving any of your problems yet. It is just giving you extra things to worry about and more burden and problems to deal with. And the real question is, would you even stay motivated enough to keep grinding away till you even reach this point where you are starting to get that positive return? And so what we often see is that because we teach a lot of skills and because a lot of our learners are very enthusiastic, they will try to learn many of these skills at the same time. And they'll spend weeks and weeks on trying to learn multiple of these skills. And it's very demotivating and very frustrating because they have spent a long amount of time, but they don't seem to be making much progress. The problems they started with at the beginning of this journey are still there two months in. And it's very overwhelming because again, a lot of this just early phase of learning a skill is just about figuring out how to do it correctly. Work on getting it correct and consistent as quickly as possible so that it starts providing you value as early as possible. Once that's locked in as an asset, that continues to provide you value for every day following. And if you do that, After two or three weeks, you're eliminating problems. You have an issue with procrastination, two weeks later, you're procrastinating less. Terrible at following your schedule, two weeks later, you're better at following your schedule. Always forgetting everything that you read, two weeks later, you're remembering a little bit more. You see how life is getting easier. And if we improve in this way, following the law of locking in your learning assets, achieving our goals becomes much easier.

catalyst.name: Learning the second law of learning.txt
catalyst.content: The second law of growth is to learn from the right teacher. And this doesn't mean what you probably think it means. The thing about learning to learn is that many times there isn't someone to teach you. And yes, while programs like this, I'll teach you as much as I know, As we've already talked about, 10% of it is knowing the theory, knowing what to do. 90% of it is developing the skill, putting it in practice, being able to actually do it. And so when we think about learning a skill, there are three key things that need to happen, like prerequisites in order to be able to have mastered a skill. In really simple terms, we have to know what to do, how to do it, and when to do it. These are essentially the three components that make up that third step from before, which is to be able to apply a skill correctly. That correctly refers to understanding the what, how, and when to use that skill. And so if these are the three things that are required to master a skill, What I want you to recognize here is that only one of the three things can be taught to you by someone else, which is the first part, the what to do. In the research, these three, what, how, and when, actually have their own terms. The what is called declarative knowledge. The how is called procedural knowledge. knowledge and the when is called conditional knowledge. And it's actually these last two parts that are the hardest ones to learn. An easy example is something like playing tennis. If we're learning to play tennis, that's the skill we're trying to master. It's easy for me to tell you what to do. I can say, you need to hit the ball over the net and you have a variety of different ways that you can hit the ball. That's the what. This is the conceptual declarative knowledge. But just knowing what to do isn't enough. Next, you have to learn how to do it. That's the procedural knowledge. So in tennis, this means mastering your grip, your stance, how to swing for a forehand versus a backhand, how to serve. And yes, I could explain that to you. And if you understood that explanation, again, that would be this declarative knowledge again. but to actually pull it off and to put this declarative skill into practice so that you not only just know how to do it, but you have the muscle memory and the coordination and the practice to be able to, well, procedurally do it. That is the procedural knowledge. And then there's this conditional knowledge. This is almost like thinking about it as like wisdom of knowledge. For instance, When should you use a topspin versus a slice when you're hitting the ball? When is it better to move forward towards the net or stay at the baseline? Deciding in real time based on your opponent's moves and game situation is often the trickiest part and that comes with experience and intuition. That is the conditional knowledge. And the same is true for learning to learn. I will tell you what to do. I will give you the detailed declarative information, why it works, how it works. I'll give you instructions on how to execute correctly. I'll tell you how to fix the common mistakes, but you still need to practice it and learn to pull it off. And even once you have pulled it off, you need to learn when to use it to get the best effect. And for each of these three types of knowledge, there is a best teacher to teach this knowledge to you. The best teacher for the declarative knowledge, in this situation where we're learning how to learn, is me. I'm going to deliver the information to you distilled from my over a decade of coaching experience and understanding of the research and seeing what is the most valuable at helping people become an ultra efficient learner. But I'm unfortunately Not for a lack of effort. I'm not the best teacher for procedural and conditional knowledge. In fact, I can't be. Because, in fact, the best teacher for your procedural and conditional knowledge are your own experiences. So I can teach you the declarative, but these two things, that's on you. So in order to develop this procedure and conditional knowledge quickly, we have to have the experience, trying it, making mistakes and figuring out how we can improve that. And we want to engage in this very quickly and very early on. And so what I say is to fail fast, fail safe. This is going to be your life model. And so when we have these experiences that allow us to find the mistakes and fail fast and fail safe, we go from a position where initially we don't know how to do it correctly. And we also don't know what our mistakes are going to be. So we don't know the correct way of doing it, but we also don't know how we are going to do it incorrectly. And by having some of these early experiences and identifying these mistakes, we start to learn more about the habits and the tendencies and the barriers and the challenges and all the little ways of doing it wrong. And we enter into this better position where we're much closer to success because although we still don't know the correct way of doing it, we have much greater visibility on all the ways of doing it incorrectly. We understand much more deeply what our tendencies and our existing habits are. The things that used to be invisible to us, the barriers that used to hold us back, we can now see them for what they are, which means we can actually work on them. This is basically the mechanism for developing procedural knowledge. This is what developing procedural knowledge actually means. And of course, eventually, once we start targeting more of the ways that we're doing it incorrectly and remove more and more of those barriers, then over time, we eventually are able to do it correctly. And then we can work on the consistency and being able to do it more easily to really lock in that learning asset. So fail fast, fail safe really means that you want to find the mistakes. That's the fail part. and you want to find them quickly. You want to do it early because when you wait to make the mistakes until later, it means that you increase the consequence of those mistakes. Let's say, for example, I teach you a new way of studying and you don't really apply any of these methods until just before a big exam. Well, now, if you don't know how to do the technique correctly, you don't really have enough time to fix it. And so you've left the consequence, potentially not doing well in this big exam. All of that risk is now concentrated into you needing to get it right in a very short period of time. And that's a lot of stress and pressure. Instead, you want to spread that risk out so that it's so thin. And so each time you make a mistake, there's really no consequence in the grand scheme of things. If you're studying for an exam and then on one day for two hours, six months before the exam, you make a small mistake with the technique. It doesn't really matter. In other words, small mistakes early prevent big mistakes later. So why is this law called learn from the right teacher? It's because a lot of people learn the procedural and conditional knowledge from the wrong teacher. They mistakenly believe that the best way to learn this procedural and conditional knowledge is not through personal experience and trial and error. They believe it's by getting more declarative information. And so what this looks like is that instead of jumping into the experiences and figuring out these mistakes that are going to be so helpful for growth, what they do instead is they ask a lot of questions. a lot of what if questions. And what they're doing with these what if questions is that they're trying to reduce the risk of making a mistake by asking enough questions upfront. I remember years and years ago, I was trying to learn how to play basketball. By the way, I'm terrible at basketball. And I asked my friends about like what the technique is, like what I meant to do. I watched a video about it, like this is what you're meant to do. And so I was like feeling like pretty confident about, you know, like what I needed to do. In fact, I'd look at my friend's technique and be like, hmm, you know, I think you should do this differently. I'd never even played, but I just watched the video and I was like, cool, now I'm an expert. As you can imagine, as soon as I started playing, I realized that I was not very good at basketball. And in fact, I was making a bunch of mistakes that I hadn't even thought of. So I distinctly remember there was this one part where I had to jump up to shoot. And I would always jump sideways, always. And I wasn't even aware of the fact that I was doing that until my friend pointed it out to me. And the reason is that because years, for years leading up to that point, I had been training competitively in Taekwondo. um the martial art and with taekwondo a lot of the movement is side to side jumping and i trained that thousands of times and so every time i jumped i realized i had the habit of jumping sideways and so when i play basketball and it sounds stupid i actually struggled to just jump straight up and that's the thing about learning new skills and especially about learning learning skills is that There are 300, 400 possible mistakes that someone could make. And yes, you could try to think about all 300 things and ask a what if question about everything so that you declaratively know what to do to avoid all 300 mistakes, but you think you're going to be able to keep track of 300 different things to be aware of? No, in fact, it's so overwhelming that that's the reason that you would procrastinate it because it's like this huge, big task that takes so much effort because you know of all these things that you have to be thinking about. It's actually going to prevent you from practicing. But just because there are 300 mistakes that you could make doesn't mean you're going to make all 300 of them. You, for each individual, may only make five, six, seven of those. And it's figuring out what are your personalized tendencies and mistakes. That's where the value is. So when you avoid just trying things out, you avoid discovering these personalized mistakes, which means that you avoid discovering your personalized tendencies, which means you are avoiding personalized growth and improvement. I also remember, back when I was in medical school, trying to think about how to learn effectively reading through all of this research and just trying to craft this like perfect way of learning and, you know, just thinking about it for hours and hours and hours, like weeks spent on trying to think about how I should change my study. And I get to a point where I feel like, yeah, I figured it out. And then I try it. And 20 minutes into my study, I realized I just didn't think about an entire set of variables that makes my whole plan useless. And I think a very common feeling is that feeling of not being able to make a mistake because making a mistake means I'll fail to achieve my goal. But this is completely the wrong way around. What's obvious to me now, but was not obvious to me before, is that the path to success starts through the door of mistakes. And if I'm always afraid of taking a step onto a path that I've never walked down before, then the only progress you're going to make is in circles. And if you resonate with what I'm saying, if you realize this, this means you now have a competitive advantage because those powerful, impactful skills that help you to achieve the goal more confidently, more quickly, with more certainty, Those skills are rare. Most people don't have those skills because most people are afraid of the process of attaining those skills. So here's my takeaway practical guidelines on how to learn from the right teacher for each of these knowledge types. For declarative knowledge, understanding the what? Focus on understanding it, focus on making sense of it. Write notes, think deeply, connect it with what you already know. Make analogies, compare it to your existing understanding of how learning works. Think critically, think deeply about the technique and the theory itself. Aim to become that expert. Then move swiftly into the procedural part. as soon as you feel like you know enough about the technique to start discovering your first personalized mistakes and tendencies. Give it a go. You do not need, in fact, it's impossible to get to a point where you feel like you understand it enough that you're going to get it correct on the first go. And if you wait to feel certain by just asking questions, clarifying, getting more information, then you've broken this law and it's going to be harder and it's going to be more challenging and stressful to develop the skill. As soon as you feel like you can give it a go, you're ready to move on to the next teacher, personal experience. And then after you've done that, after you've had the experience, reflect on the experience, see how it went, see where you could improve it because you've got that great declarative understanding of it and you know how it works, why it works, how it's meant to work. When you have that experience and you think about how it went procedurally, and you notice that it didn't go quite as you expected, you now know how you might be able to problem solve it by using that declarative knowledge. Or at the very least, it means that when you ask for help, you have very targeted specific questions. You become very easy to help as opposed to someone just saying, I'm not sure how to do this technique. Make me be sure. Slowly, as you continue the cycle of trying things out, reflecting on it, thinking about how it could be better, trying it again, understanding more at a personalized level, your tendencies, what habits you have, how you can change them. Then you slowly start developing this conditional knowledge as well as you test the skill and you practice it in all sorts of different contexts for different types of challenges, for different types of topics and different types of time pressures. So if you can do this and if you can follow this law of learning from the right teacher and knowing when to move from one teacher to the next teacher, from getting information to getting personal experience, you will be in a much better position to succeed than I was when I was at your stage of this journey. And don't forget, we are here to support you. And you are in a community surrounded by people who are happy to support you. Sometimes, just like how I didn't realize I jumped sideways playing basketball, we need people around us to point it out, to let us know how we can improve, to point out the tendencies that we might not be able to see. And so if you get stuck, ask for help. Knowing when and how to ask for help is a skill in itself.

catalyst.content: Based on your results, it seems that you are currently unchained. And that's actually a good place to be. Unchained means that your main limitation is a fixed mindset. Specifically, this means that you're likely to be averse to failure and making mistakes, and you probably aren't clear how you can turn mistakes into learning opportunities. Now, ironically, I guess, Even though we say it's unchained, it means that currently you are chained by the self-limiting beliefs and the goal is to become unchained. However, what we found is that people that are unchained, once they make that mindset shift, can see some of the most rapid disproportionate growth and benefit out of any other learner archetype. What that means is your future is looking bright, especially now that you know that the thing that holds you back the most is your mindset. Let me explain how that works. Mindset is our ability to engage with experimentation, and that experimentation is a necessary ingredient for learning new skills and growing. If we have a fixed mindset, we are reluctant to invest our time and energy and sacrifice it for experimentation. It can make us feel uncertain, insecure. In some cases, we might find that the idea of failing even attacks us personally in our sense of identity and who we are. We can have our self-esteem tied to achieving certain outcomes. So ironically, it's almost like we care so much about the outcome that we stop ourselves from achieving the outcome. We're paralyzed by the fear. And so the solution to that is rather simple actually. We just need to understand how we can learn from mistakes and then have some experience learning from them. Once we learn from the mistakes and we know how we can tackle mistakes confidently, that anxiety and fear can slowly dissolve away. The important thing is to recognize the impact that our mindset has on us, which means that we can focus our attention on developing that. Once we develop that, we can become unchained. But when we fail to recognize that, then we can try to develop new techniques and methods and work harder and spend more time and sacrifice more. And at the end of the day, it just seems like it's not enough and our results are inconsistent and it's stressful and it's full of anxiety. And that's because we're not addressing the thing that's holding us back the most. I also want to point out that you're not alone. In fact, you're far from it. Most of the learners that we have worked with have been unchained. And probably the most phenomenal learners that we've ever worked with have also been unchained. At least to start with. So we've got a lot of experience working with unchained learners and there is a path that is paved there for you, that's been tested, that's been walked on by thousands of other learners before you. We know helps people achieve success. And you now armed with this information, you have taken the first step on that path and your future is looking bright, as bright as that light, which is really bright. So the next step, for you to walk on this path is to read the rest of the report, learn a little bit more about the different dimensions and follow through with our recommendations.

catalyst.name: Overview of deep processing.txt
catalyst.content: - What makes someone smart or intelligent? Many people would say it's deep processing. Deep processing is what we call our ability to think critically, analyze information, make sense of things, and learn meaningfully. It's a broad term that encompasses a huge range of processes that allow us to understand, retain, and use information in a meaningful way. Most people who have been top achievers and good learners, especially in formal education settings, have been good learners and top achievers since a young age. This is because most high achievers are able to do well because of their deep processing ability. In many cases, they can use horrible learning methods, but still do well because their deep processing is able to compensate for it. If people without high levels of deep processing would use the same methods, they would not be able to achieve the same level of success. This is why following or simply copying the methods of a top achiever rarely allows others to replicate that success unless they also have a high level of deep processing. Luckily, deep processing can be trained, and we have seen that across six to 12 months, learners are able to train and improve their ability to understand, retain, and use knowledge. and effect becoming smarter. Because in modern conventional education, we don't often talk about the processes and the methods around learning, especially at a cognitive level. Let me give you some examples so you can see what deep processing might look like. Someone with low deep processing could read a page, the same way as someone with high deep processing, but mentally what happens in their mind is that they are just focused on trying to remember and understand and repeat the information. The information comes in and the thoughts they have about that information are not conducive to having a strong memory. Someone with high deep processing could read the same page and when the information comes in, they think about it a little bit more critically. They think, how is this related to other things that I already know? How can I apply this? How can I simplify this? How is that related to another part of the same text? How is it similar? How is it different? How does it all fit together in a big picture? These are the types of thoughts that someone with high deep processing would have. And because of these thoughts, their brain will then process that information in a different way, which allows it to be stored in the memory to a higher degree and also a deeper understanding, which ultimately means we can use that information more as well. These are all cognitive habits and processes. It is not so much about what is the strategy and the technique you use physically, it is more about how do you think about information cognitively. There's also a reason why it's so hard to just copy someone else because you never really know what's happening inside their own head. This is why you have many people using the same method, but some people can get much more value from it because what's happening in their head is very different. So in this program, we're going to unpack what the effective cognitive processes are, what the less effective cognitive processes are, and then show you how you can start training and upgrading them no matter what level you are at now. You'll learn the theory about what a effective cognitive process that's conducive for deep processing looks like, and then you'll learn the steps and the tasks and the techniques that you can use to stimulate your brain to think that way and then eventually develop new habits.

If we go too low, we enter into a more ineffective, passive state of learning where your brain isn't really doing anything at all. The second word is metacognition. Metacognition is your awareness of your own thought process. So if you have good metacognition, it means that you know how you are thinking, you know what you are doing and why you're doing it. If you have very low metacognition, you don't have a lot of self-awareness over your own thinking processes. You just do things. Metacognition is very important for learning because it allows you to know what you're doing and improve on them. It's one of the most important factors for someone to improve their learning ability. The next word is spaced repetition. Spaced repetition is simply the idea of repeating something that you've already learned in some kind of regular interval to try to keep your memory topped up. This goes back to some science that says that at first when you learn something, you forget it very quickly, but if you repeat it, you will hold on to it for longer. And so spaced repetition is really a technique for extending your memory and improving your attention by repeating the same thing Maybe a day after you learned it, and then a few days after that, and then a week after that, so on and so forth. Neuroplasticity is the ability of your brain to mold and adapt itself based on different types of forces and pressures. This is the reason why people can actually get smarter or learn new skills or skills. unlearn or learn new habits. This is a reason why intelligence is not fixed because your brain actually has this ability to mold and change and adapt depending on what type of forces and pressures we put it under. Chunking is a method of learning that allows us to increase the amount we can remember by grouping information into related clusters and categories. So instead of remembering like 10 different things, if you break it up into three groups of three and then one other item, it becomes easier to remember. The next word is active learning. Active learning is any method of learning that involves our brain actively thinking about and putting effort into evaluating and processing the information. This is compared to passive learning where our brain is essentially off and we're just going through the motions. An example of active learning might be listening very carefully, thinking of questions to ask, really comparing and contrasting. You're using your brain to really think deeply about what you're consuming, whereas passive learning might be just reading and rereading something or just mindlessly writing down as many notes as possible about what you're hearing. Passive learning is essentially a waste of time and active learning is vastly more effective. The next word is schema formation. This is relating to the idea that your memory and your knowledge exists in these networks, which we call schemas. They're just interconnected kind of maps of knowledge. So when information fits into this network and it belongs and is related to other pieces of information, it forms a schema. catalyst.name: Overview of mindset.txt
catalyst.content: Mindset is all about our beliefs, our perspectives, and our habits of emotional self-regulation. It's about how we respond to stress and challenges or react to mistakes and setbacks. It's related strongly to the research by Carol Dweck on growth mindsets, as well as topics like grit, resilience, psychological buoyancy, and open-mindedness. Our mindset affects our ability to learn new skills, to confront challenges, to face adversity and to persist through difficulty. One of the crucial and unavoidable parts of learning to learn is making mistakes and then learning from them. Just like how a skier might fall down multiple times while they're skiing down a mountain for the first time, each Fall is an opportunity to learn. Only through the cycle of trying, making mistakes, and then learning from them to try again can we improve and learn more. Our mindset determines how willing we are to make mistakes and our ability to learn from them. As a result, limitations in mindset can completely oppose all forms of self-improvement and growth. Mindset limitations are also the most common type of limitation that we see in our learners. Many people think that since mindsets are usually established and set since early childhood and partly also genetics, that it cannot change over time. And while it is true that genetics and your early childhood experiences are a strong influencer, we know that mindsets can change over time. Unfortunately, many people don't know that and they don't know how to improve and so they will continue to struggle to improve for their entire lives. But we know from our experience working with so many people that this is unnecessary. Mindset too can be trained and investing your time to train it is one of the most life-changing things that you can do. This is the reason why a lot of CEOs and entrepreneurs and top executives will pay for coaching and psychologists purely to help them develop better mindsets. Like deep processing, training a mindset requires you to unlearn old habits of thinking and replace them with new habits of thinking. With our program, Being Able To Train, train a growth mindset in people who have potentially struggled with this for decades is actually personally one of the proudest things that we have achieved. And if you are limited by your mindset, then it is an incredibly liberating feeling to adopt and embody a growth mindset.

catalyst.content: We're now gonna start the process of upgrading your mindset towards a more growth mindset. And we're gonna do this with a very simple and easy activity. Anyone of a reasonable level of intelligence should be able to do this very easily, so don't stress out. I'm gonna give you this list of 12 words. They're unrelated to each other. And what I want you to do is just mentally Memorize and remember all 12 of these words. Don't write any notes. Don't use any aids. Just try to remember and memorize this list of 12 words. I'll give you a little bit of time to do that now. All right, I'm going to test you on those words in a little bit. And in the meantime, I'll tell you a little bit about what it means to actually develop a growth mindset. A key challenge of improving or learning any skill that helps us to achieve a goal. So whether it is improving our learning skills so that we can hit a learning goal, or whether it's improving an athletic skill so that we can hit an athletic goal, or we are constantly pushing up against our current limitations. We're pushing against these limitations constantly so that we can break through and then expand them. But there are some people that will push up against the limitation and not be very successful and actually growing and improving. They will hit against this limitation and they will just kind of bounce back like a sad rubber ball being thrown at a concrete wall. And for us, all of us here, where we want to improve and we want to grow, What's important is to understand that the way we think about the limitation actually has a very strong influence on whether we will break through it and expand it or whether we just bounce back. Back in high school, I used to do a lot of Taekwondo. And one of the things that we did for, I don't actually know whatever reason, I think it was just like showing off purposes, was board breaking. That's where you have this wooden board, someone holds this wooden board and you have to try to kick through it or punch through it. And I remember thinking, I was quite young at the time, I remember thinking, man, this is going to be impossible. I'm going to break my fist on this board. That doesn't seem possible for me. And so I was looking at it. I was psyching myself up. I was getting ready to do the punch. I was doing a lot of the practice swings. And I was saying, man, I'm going to hurt myself if I do this. And I punched it. And the strangest thing happened. You know what happened? I hurt myself. Yeah, I didn't break the board. I hurt my fist. It was bleeding a little bit. I thought, yep, I was pretty much correct. The board is very hard and that did really hurt. And so one of my seniors who was watching me, he said, "No, no, no, you're doing it wrong." Yeah, I know I'm doing it wrong because clearly the board didn't break. But the advice he gave to me was don't punch the board. Punch through the board. Your goal is not to end your fist where the board starts. Your goal lies beyond the board. So I thought, okay, why not? And I committed to it and I punched through aiming past the board. And this time the strangest thing happened. I actually did punch through the board. It snapped in half. In fact, very easily. And what I've realized is that after doing it once, these boards are actually not that hard. They're actually very easy to break through, but it's that I thought it was hard. And so I was holding myself back. I created my own limitation where the board started, because in my mind, that board is not something that can be broken.
