There's no problem with caring and feeling frustrated and feeling anxious. That's normal. But feeling frustrated or anxious or stressed isn't going to change the result. But what we can do is we can turn those emotions into a radar for self-improvement. The emotions tell you what you need to work on. And we can use the CPJ framework to be specific and find that targeted process that we can improve to give ourselves a chance that we deserve.

catalyst.name: Overview of self regulation.txt
catalyst.content: Self-regulation, or more specifically, self-regulated learning in our program, is your ability to monitor and adapt your learning methods to achieve your learning goals. Self-regulation is what creates consistency in learning success. If deep processing is someone's just raw physical strength, then self-regulation will be the technique and the form through which they apply that strength. Self-regulation is about the tools that you have in your toolkit and your mastery of using the right tool for the right occasion. In many cases, people with high self-regulation can do just as well as people with high deep processing or even better, especially when there are more challenges and more factors and variables to account for. People with high deep processing but low levels of self-regulation, they might do well if the learning task is fairly simple and there's not other things to worry about. But people with high self-regulation, they can make the learning work for them even when it's not ideal. Even when the teacher is not the best, a subject is not the most interesting, the resources aren't the best, things are scattered everywhere, there's time pressures. Self-regulation is your ability to overcome each of those barriers and give your deep processing a chance to shine. We saw this in full effect during the 2020 COVID lockdowns where a lot of students were disadvantaged because their normal patterns and routines were disrupted. However, what research found was that those with high levels of self-regulation were able to protect themselves against the damages and the effects of those lockdowns, and in some cases, even do better. What was a setback for others became an opportunity to shine for students with higher self-regulation. Self-regulation and self-regulated learning is one of the most influential long-term predictors of learning success, both in formal education settings as a student, as well as in the professional workforce. Self-regulation is also a skill that can be trained, just like deep processing. Over the coming weeks and months, you will learn a series of techniques and tools that you can apply to increase your ability to monitor and adapt your learning methods to overcome learning obstacles and achieve your learning goals more consistently and with more confidence.

catalyst.name: Self regulation training challenge (level 1).txt
catalyst.content: Let's improve our self-regulation using the self-regulation challenge. Self-regulation is in concept, a very easy dimension to understand. It's basically, do you know what you're doing and why you're doing it? And if what you're doing isn't achieving your goal, do you know how to change it so that it does achieve your goal? And so for most people, they don't have that great self-regulation. And the reason is not because they are incapable learners, it's because this is not something that's often taught or something that people are exposed to in normal education. And there is particularly two main parts that are often missing. And that is having enough metacognition and knowing which meaningful adjustments to make. Now, the second part, you're in good hands because as you go through this program, We're going to teach you the techniques and we're going to explain how it works and why it works. So you will know what meaningful adjustments to make. If you're using a technique and it's not quite going as planned and there are things that you need to change about it, if you know the theory of the technique and how it's meant to work and you understand the principles behind it, you can think about your own technique, you can understand what you're doing, and you can make your own little modifications. And often, you don't even need someone to point it out and say, you know, get feedback or a coach to tell you, this is what you need to do. A lot of the time, if you have enough self-awareness of your own process, and you know the principles you're trying to hit, the adjustments you make, like you can make all sorts of different adjustments. If it hits the principles, it's usually going to be fine. As a disclaimer, when you start getting into the more complicated techniques, like more complex systems of learning, yes, there are some quite deep technicalities and principles and nuances, so it's not as intuitive, but especially in these early stages. you can get very, very far with improving your learning skill just by knowing the basic principles and having an awareness of how close your current technique aligns to those basic principles. So that is less of an issue because you're going to learn that through the upcoming lessons. The bigger one is the metacognition, aka how much awareness do you have about your own thinking and learning process and trying to improve your learning ability, learning efficiency without having that metacognition is like trying to fix a car without understanding how a car works. Like someone can give you instructions on what to do and they say, hey, open up the hood and then, you know, take out this part and this component. And then when you do this thing, you know, look for if there's leaking over here and if it's leaking here, do this. And if it's not, then look at this. And you're thinking, I don't even understand like the instructions. I don't even, I'm not even aware. I can't even visualize and imagine what part of the car you're talking about. All I do is put the key and turn it on, like step on the gas. And all I know is that it's not going fast enough. So if that's you and that's the amount of knowledge you have about learning, It's very cumbersome and slow and awkward to try to actually improve your skills. We need to have enough awareness of our learning to be able to talk about it like a mechanic of our own brain. We need to go from, I'm studying a lot, but I'm doing badly in tests, to... I'm trying to encode this information at this order using this note-taking technique paired with this priming technique, but I'm finding that in this type of testing, I'm underperforming by 20% compared to this test thing. Both people may have the exact same problem. but the second person is in a position where they are one step away from solving it. Whereas the first person, they don't even know enough about the problem to even start guessing how they might solve it. So how do we go from being this first person to becoming this second person? 'Cause no one is born knowing that much about learning. This is where the self-regulation challenge comes in. What we're gonna do is create a learning flow diagram. We're gonna trace the path of a single piece of information. Almost like if you imagine tracing the path of someone eating a cookie. It's like, okay, you take a bite of a cookie, where does the food go? And you follow it through the body. We're doing the same thing, but we're following it through our thought. So when information first comes in, write down, what are we doing with this information? How are we thinking about it? Are we thinking in a certain way? Is there a certain objective we're trying to hit? Are we writing notes in a certain way? Are we priming that or pre-studying that beforehand? So there's actually like something that happens even before that information properly is being consumed. And then after that, what are we doing? Immediately after, is there something we're doing straight after our lecture? Is there something that we're doing on the same day? Is there something we're doing the next day? When we go back to review that material, how are we doing it? And don't just say, I sit down and study. Really force yourself to be as specific as is possible for you at your current level now. So that might be just saying, I sit down to study by opening my books and then reading through it. Okay, where do you start reading from? Is it from the beginning? Like how do you decide where you start? Imagine you're almost creating like an instruction manual, a series of steps for a robot. This robot is just gonna follow the instructions you give it. So if you don't say, hey, start reading here, Figure out where to start reading based on this. If you don't give those specific instructions, it's gonna start opening up a textbook into a random page and then start reading from a random word in a random order. The reason we do this is because learning is a series of micro decisions. Most of those decisions, especially as a beginner, we're not gonna know much about. We make these implicit habitual decisions. Okay, well, when I start reading, of course I'm gonna start reading from the headings first. But not everyone does that and you don't have to do that. You could start reading from the conclusion first. You could start with practice questions first. You could scan to find keywords first. You could try to find the heading that you feel resonates with you the most and then read the sentence underneath that until you find a different keyword and then skip over to that one. These are viable strategies. And so everyone does things a little differently when they're reading, when they're listening, when they're writing their notes, when they're reviewing and testing themselves. And everyone reacts differently to different information. So when you create your learning flow, you want to be as detailed and as specific as you can about what you do at every single point of that information from the moment it comes into your brain through to the day you actually need to use it, whether it's for an exam or whether you just need it for work or some kind of essay or problem solving, whatever it is. And if at any point you truly don't know, you're racking your brain, you're really trying your best, you're really trying to develop that awareness and you just don't know, like it would be essentially a guess. to write down anything, then you can write that down. You can just say, I don't know yet. This learning flow diagram, you will need to come back to this a few days from now, a week from now. As you continue through the program, as you continue to learn these techniques and you develop a better understanding of your own of your own process and your own system. Come back to this once a week, once every couple of weeks. Do the learning flow again. Just track and be really attentive to how things are changing. This is going to fast track your ability to develop this metacognition and your ability to make these powerful targeted adjustments, which in your case, if this challenge was recommended to you, is one of the things that's holding you back the most. And every time you come back to it, that I don't know goes down. But because you are aware of the fact that it's something that you probably should know, but you don't know, this is what drives self-awareness. So next time you study, you start paying attention to it. Okay, so this is what I'm doing. This is how I'm thinking. This is how I'm reacting. And the next time you go back to your flow, you can actually fill that information in. It's this process of self-awareness and realizing there's a gap and then coming back to build more self-awareness repeated thousands of times that allowed me to build the metacognition that I needed to eventually craft the learning system that I did. But we're not done yet. Once you've got your learning flow all mapped out and you know what you're doing, you then want to go next to each thing that you're doing, each major technique. and write down why. What is the rationale, the purpose behind you doing this? Like I said, there are many ways that you could study. There are many ways that you can learn. There are micro decisions that you're making. And you don't want to be learning a certain way just because that's how you have always been learning. You don't want to keep an old habit alive out of tradition. And so when you ask why very explicitly and you look at your learning flow, you'll probably realize that there are a lot of things that the only reason you do it is... You just are used to doing it. You don't actually know why you do this. You don't know what the exact rationale is. You just think there's, you don't know what the alternative is. It's how you've always done things. It just seems normal to do it this way. It's not that you're doing it because like, you know, that this produces this thing. And like, that's an effect that, you know, like, you know, scientifically valid. It's just, I do it because, well, what else would I do? These are red flags. These are areas that almost definitely can be optimized. And so, The solution is not to go and optimize absolutely everything in one go. I mean, that would be crazy to try to do that. That will take you years, literally, to fix absolutely everything. And you don't need to fix absolutely everything. You don't have to become the paragon of learning efficiency to reach whatever learning goal that you have, probably, unless your learning goal is to become like a multi-Nobel Prize winner. But at least by knowing what are the things that... should be replaced at some point, when we then go to teach you the technique as you will learn in the upcoming lessons, then you know where that technique fits. And so it's faster for you to swap and replace your existing learning system and adapt to it. If you didn't have a good understanding about your current learning flow, then when we teach you a new technique, it can be very tricky to... do anything about it because you don't know like how that's meant to fit with what you're already doing. But when you see a technique and we say, this is meant to achieve this effect for you, this is why you should do it, here's how you should do it. You can look at which part it overlaps with in your existing learning flow. You can remove what you're already doing and you can replace that with a new technique. And that becomes a thing that you work on improving for. however many days or weeks until you're good at that. And then you move on to the next technique and you replace that. And eventually that entire learning flow slowly starts getting optimized. And luckily for you, because I'm gonna teach you the most high yield optimizations to make first, your learning flow will be optimized with the high yield changes first. So do this now for the first time. And then put it into your schedule to revisit it, do it again, maybe a week or two from now. And just continue doing it every now and again until you have such a deep awareness of your learning flow and your learning system that you don't even feel like you need to write it down anymore because you're just so on top of it. And at that point, you don't need this challenge anymore.

catalyst.name: Overview of retrieval.txt
catalyst.content: Retrieval is the part of learning that is about taking your existing knowledge and then using it. It might be to solve problems, have a nuanced discussion, to create some higher level strategy, to teach someone about it, or simply regurgitate some facts. All of these are examples of retrieval. We have knowledge and we are using it for some form. Now, your ability to retrieve knowledge is very heavily determined by the other dimensions, especially deep processing and self-regulation. However, we have split it off into its own dimension because the practices and the habits of retrieval are nuanced and separate and specific enough that it's probably worth thinking about it separately. There are so many different ways that you can retrieve information at different levels of mastery and different levels of complexity and at different time scales. And there are so many methods that allow you to achieve that type of fluent expert level of retrieval. And how we practice that retrieval is often very different to how we would learn that information in the first place. Different retrieval practice methods also strongly influence our retention and depth of understanding as well as our ability to find gaps in our knowledge. An efficient learner is able to balance their retrieval practice with all the other dimensions to create a robust, comprehensive, relatively gap-free learning system to help them perform consistently at the standard that they need to. Starting from the very beginning of the program, you'll learn how you can think about retrieval practice and all the different ways that you can do it to perfectly suit your needs and challenges.

catalyst.name: Overview of self management.txt
catalyst.content: Self-management is our ability to manage ourselves, which includes time management, task management and prioritization, focus and attention management, managing your sleep routine, managing your skill development, managing everything that allows you to operate and move through life in a way that is productive. And by productive, I don't mean just being busy and doing things, but I mean having a clear understanding of your goals, creating a plan to get there, and aligning your actions and behaviors so that you can consistently do the work that allows you to achieve your goals, no matter what those goals look like. People with great self-management skills are more flexible, more adaptable and more resilient to changes and obstacles so that they can keep themselves in an optimum state even when they're busy and tired and overworked. And the reason self-management is so important is because Learning never in the real world happens in isolation. Maybe in some research articles learning is this mysterious, mystical thing that just happens inside a lab. But in the real world learning is affected by so many other factors outside of just the learning. For example there's no point being the best learner in the world if we procrastinate so much that we never even sit down to do the learning in the first place. And ultimately, self-management skills are the things that allow us to protect our time and energy and attention. In my view, self-management is about equipping ourselves with the tools to take back control of our own lives. In a modern, busy society, we are constantly pushed and pulled by different companies and industries that are trying to grab our attention and get us to do what they want us to do. And our meager human brains were never adapted to deal with that type of pressure, at that type of intensity, at that type of frequency. And so to regain that control and sense of autonomy over our lives, we need these frameworks and tools and techniques that we can apply to fight back. And then once those become new habits, they become part of just how we think and operate and navigate through the world. Even if our ultimate goal in life is just to have a nice settled job and go for hikes on the weekend and go camping with the kids or whatever it is, We need these tools and techniques and frameworks to achieve that goal in the first place. catalyst.name: Self management challenge training (level 1).txt
catalyst.content: Let's upgrade our self-management skills with the self-management challenge. I'll teach you the challenge and you should apply this every single day moving forward until your self-management has become better. The challenge we're going to do is called a reality check. And this is such an important challenge for people that struggle with self-management. Because self-management is one of these dimensions where it really doesn't matter what technique you use or what strategy you use, because if there is this issue, almost none of those techniques will work for more than like one or two weeks. So if you've ever felt the situation of like not being able to stick on top of your schedule or you always feel like you're busy but you don't have enough time to do the things that really matter and you can't prioritize or you are prioritizing but it doesn't seem to be enough or like you're always procrastinating and you never seem to be in control of your energy or your focus, all of these things. There are lots of individual techniques that can help with pretty much everything. That is actually the easy part about self-management because unlike with something like deep processing, where it's about your thinking habits that have been ingrained for years and years, and it just takes a long time to unlearn these old habits and then relearn these new habits, and it just takes weeks. Self-management techniques are like plug and play. Like you can pick up a self-management technique, learn it, understand it, use it, apply it in your life and get immediate value. And you can spend, you know, weeks and months like tinkering to make it even better and better. But it's very easy to get value and to solve problems with self-management techniques. And yet people that really struggle with self-management and have struggled with self-management for a long time, really struggle to use those techniques. This reality check challenge directly addresses the root cause behind why. So here's what you do. First of all, you're going to make a schedule. I'm not going to give you any instructions on how to make the schedule for now, at least. We'll teach you how to do this, you know, while moving forward. But to start off, I want you to just make a schedule. If you've already made a schedule, that's fine. You can tick this already off. The second thing you're going to do, try your best to follow the schedule, but I want you to track the time you actually spend. So you're going to end up with two different schedules. I would do this for at least a few days, maybe even up to a couple of weeks if you feel like you want to get a really accurate sense for yourself that's important for this challenge. So you end up with two schedules. One is a schedule you planned to do. And the second is the one you filled in retrospectively by tracking how long things took. So if you had like studying for two hours on Tuesday at 4:00 PM until 6:00 PM, but you actually, in reality, We're distracted from 4 to 4.45, and then you fell asleep from 4.45 to 5, and then from 5 to 9 p.m., you were studying, and then you fell asleep again or whatever it is. Your in-reality schedule is usually going to look very, very different. In fact, when you... look at your in-radi schedule. This is the thing that makes you feel sad and disappointed because it can be so far from what you originally planned. That's okay, we're gonna work on that. So once you have these two schedules, we're gonna calibrate and see what the differences are. And here, what I want you to do is look at this discrepancy and try to figure out what is a trend Like, is there a common reason why certain things take longer, certain barriers and obstacles? And usually these are things that you're well aware of. Like a really common example is that I have all these people that schedule in doing a certain amount of study as soon as they come home from work. It's like, okay, cool. That fits into your schedule perfectly. Except in reality, when they come home from work, they're so tired that they like, they're aren't able to focus, they don't do it, they procrastinate it, whatever it is. And so that's the reality. That's the reality check part of this challenge. We need to understand what the reality of the situation is and then isolate these barriers into specific factors. We don't want to just say, oh yeah, I've planned it in my schedule, but then knowing in reality that there's a 50% chance that it's going to happen like that because we know like every single day, like we're too tired. That's the reality. And one of the most dangerous things about self-management is living separated from this reality. Thinking that the reality is an exception and that our ideal schedule is like the baseline. But it's usually the other way around. Most of the time, the baseline is that plans do not go according to plan. Schedules get derailed. Priorities need to be reshuffled. Things pop up. Energy and focus and concentration, the amount of sleep that you get, these are things that happen in real life. And you cannot manage yourself navigating through the pressures of real life if we don't think about the influence that these real life pressures have on us. So be holistic and note down specifically what are the barriers and the trends that consistently seem to create this disparity between the way that you schedule it versus what you actually did. Once you've got this list, now we move on to the next part, which is to either accept or address. Accepting the barrier means that this is a reasonable thing to expect. And honestly, there's not much more I can do about this. Sometimes that's life. If you are working full time and then studying full time, you've got two kids and a mortgage to pay and you're tired as heck coming home from work and you have to make dinner and you've got to take out the trash and your laundry hasn't been done in a week, it's perfectly fine to say, "Hey, I feel tired when I come home from work and it's hard to concentrate." It's reasonable. And you might have already tried a lot of things to improve your energy levels and you may have made some nice good progress to get to the point that you are at now. So accepting is saying there isn't anything else that's reasonable or realistic or sustainable that I can really do about this. In reality, when I coach people, what I often find is that there are things that you accept can't be changed. but there are actually things that we can do if we think a little bit outside the box. So one great example of this is that one of the students I was working with in the past was in a very similar situation to what I just mentioned. You know, they had a young kid, they were working, they were also studying, trying to enter into medical school at the time. And so in that situation, I know it's terrible, like they were studying from like 11:00 PM until 3:00 AM every day because they were just busy all hours of the day. And so in that situation, it's true. Like there's not much more that he could have done. And so his solution, thinking outside the box, was first of all, he changed his job. He got a job that had more flexible working hours that he could also work from home. It didn't pay as much, but it was important enough for him to focus on his studying. He also moved house. He used to live quite far away from the city. His commute going to and from university was quite long. He moved closer to a suburb next to his parents that allowed him to save some time on commute. But also because his parents are so close by, the parents were able to help with childcare. Now, that wasn't the best financial decision. He did lose some money from doing that. But again, in the scale of what was important to him and his priorities, that was the decision that he was willing to make. And so when we think about whether there's a situation that we are accepting versus something that we can address and do something about, we should also think about it in terms of what is comfortable for us. Most of the things that we say there's nothing more that we can reasonably do about, are just the things that we think it's too inconvenient to do something about this. The inconvenience and discomfort of solving this problem is not worth it for the benefit that it would bring. And that's a perfectly fine decision to make as long as you are thinking about it critically and making that decision. I'm not saying like sell your house and move and whatever and change your job. Like I'm not saying to do that. But if it is important for you and if you think that Yes, it's inconvenient, but actually I'm willing to do that to achieve this and have this benefit and to solve this problem. Then this is something that moves from accept to address. It's just that addressing it involves multiple steps and it takes a little bit of time. One of the really common examples I see with students is with procrastination. They often say, well, I get distracted. I can't focus all of these things. We can put that in the column of accept. I just accept that I can't focus, I can't concentrate, I procrastinate all the time, I get distracted, all of these things. But if we are putting it inside there, we're saying we can't do anything about it, which means we're willing to just live with that or hoping it's going to fix itself, which it's probably not. And so when you think, am I willing to just live with that problem? forever. Then we start thinking again, okay, well, maybe there are some more things that I can do that I can't be bothered doing, but I probably should do to move it from the accept column into the address column. Now, when we do have things in the address column, we want to be very tactical. We want to be very specific. If you say, I've got an issue with procrastination, don't just say procrastination, be specific. What are you procrastinating with? When? Why? What have you tried doing about it? What can you do next? Now, I mentioned before that this challenge directly addresses a thing that stops most people who struggle with self-management from actually improving in the first place. And that's because most people either don't realize that the reality of what's happening on their days is completely different from what their intentions are, Which means that it's impossible to plan. It's impossible to improve because you're completely disconnected from the real issues. Or you know what the issues are, but there are so many things that you either accept that you cannot change or you think you should address them, but you're not specific enough in terms of addressing it. And if any of these things happen, then it doesn't matter what the technique is. is. Even if I teach you a technique, you either don't know that you should use it, or you don't think you can use it, or you don't know the problem specifically enough to actually use it. And so by doing this challenge, as you progress through the program, and we teach you new techniques on self-management, these become tools that you can use to address the things that you've identified need to be addressed. And by doing this challenge, not only do you gain an awareness about what reality is and issues and discrepancies and your plans and intentions versus what really happens, you also start getting an appreciation for the nuances of the challenges you're trying to address and overcome. If you have an issue with getting distracted, you realize that the solution isn't just focus more because you already tried to focus more and it didn't work. you're gaining more experience and you're gaining more self-awareness about really what it takes to address these issues properly until eventually you don't have any issues to address.

catalyst.name: How to upgrade your dimensions (level 1).txt
catalyst.content: In this lesson, I'll show you how the program is designed to help you improve each of your dimensions. By understanding how it's been designed, it allows you to move from a position of being just a passenger in this journey to being the driver since... It is your journey. And I think that if I'm able to help you understand the overall progression and how we achieve this transformation, the mechanism, then it means that you're in a better position to control that, take ownership of it and know how to make it work for you. So one thing straight off the bat, is that this program was designed in a very specific order and that order doesn't necessarily make sense immediately unless you think about it quite deeply. When I went through this journey, my own growth and transformation was very convoluted. Like, I was just kind of like looking at everything, seeing, you know, like, what could I improve on? What could I improve on? And I'd kind of travel down this for a bit and be like, well, that's not very useful. I'd come back and then try this one and then see where that led. And then I'd come back and then try this one and then realize, oh, this one was actually right. And then, so it was a very, very convoluted path, which meant I wasted a lot of time. I explored a lot of things that kind of led nowhere. I experimented on things that didn't provide me any value. And then at the same time, I didn't experiment on some of the things that would have fixed my problems very quickly. And so during those years, I had to sacrifice a lot, but I would have loved to have had just the sort of roadmap, this pathway laid out for me in a little bit more of a structured, thoughtful approach so that instead of all this convoluted sort of soul searching, looking through the research, I just had a clear path that I knew would get me to my goal much more efficiently and to do so in a tenth of the time. So here's how the program is designed to help you do that in a tenth of the time. The program is divided broadly into two main phases. The first phase is the high yield phase. And the second is the growth phase. And it goes like this. This green bar represents the time to value. So the stuff that we teach you in the high yield phase are things that you can start applying, learn how to be good at, and it provides you value very quickly in the short term. So we're talking hours, days, a couple of weeks. They also tend to be the more common So if we had every single student in the world and we looked at every problem that these students faced, 80% of the problems that they face are addressed in the earlier lessons. So most likely the problems that you are thinking of right now that you may have with your learning that are stopping you from being able to achieve your goals, those are addressed. Most likely those are addressed in the earlier phases. We want you to receive as much value upfront as possible. The blue bar represents total impact. So in the later phases, we start introducing things that are very, very life-changing. These are things that involve completely restructuring your existing habits and retraining the way that your brain naturally processes information. These are the things that allow you to have that permanent competitive advantage. These are... learning assets that are incredibly powerful, but the investment upfront required to learn these is much higher. It takes longer. It takes much more practice. You're not going to see value from this immediately. Some of these things in the early part of the growth phase, they do provide you value within just a few weeks. But some of those parts, especially in the more complicated techniques and the meaty part of the growth phase, these are things that can take some people months to get good enough at that it's providing value. And so obviously, unless you're in a really very easy position right now, we've just got lots of free time, you don't want to delay the value for months. You want to have most of your problems solved. You want to have a bit more free time, a little bit more confidence, a bit more certainty. And you have that extra capacity that you can reinvest into the stuff that you learn in the growth phase. But if you try to tackle the growth phase without having mastered the stuff in the high yield phase, you will have these existing problems that create issues for you, issues in your memory, issues with your scheduling, issues with your procrastination or with your quality of knowledge. And those things will continually hold you back and prevent you from learning the things in the growth phase anyway. And so, like I said before, you may not even need the stuff in the growth phase to achieve your immediate first set of goals. What I want is for you to be able to achieve your immediate goals very quickly. Like I want to give you the shortest path to success. And then I want you to feel so good about that and to feel so confident and to have so many doors open for you that you stick around with us to learn even more, to have even greater goals to reach. That's what I want to do with this program. So if you don't have a really firm foundation of the things earlier on, the later things are going to be quite confusing and you will feel like it's just not working for you. Now, the other part of this program that is, I think, quite unique, even if you've been in a lot of online programs before, is that there is a very clear distinction between online and offline. And actually, we call this the online... offline balance. So if you are taking a university program online and you've got like a semester worth of papers, you are not learning skills, not as a primary objective. Like, yes, you will learn some skills as you go through university, but the main objective of those papers is just to teach you the content, just to teach you that declarative part usually. And if you think about learning something like a sport, Yes, you can learn it online, but the much more common way of learning it is face-to-face because you understand how important the practice and the procedural and conditional knowledge is. It's obvious. It's less obvious when it's a cognitive skill. And people think about learning a cognitive skill like they think about learning like science or maths or history. They think about it as because I'm learning to learn and the stuff I'm learning is just content, that's how I learn the skills. But it's actually much closer to developing an athletic skill, like playing a sport. And there are not many online programs or courses that teach cognitive skills. And one of the big reasons is because online we can teach you things and I can give you instructions and tell you about techniques, but I can't control what happens offline. If we were face to face physically in a workshop or something, I can give you an instruction and I can say, hey, do this activity and then I can just watch you do it. So I'm able to control and give you feedback and observe and monitor that entire process. And it's what happens during that process that is the most important for learning that skill. In this context here, there's a limit to how much I can control when you leave this platform. Like I said, I just have to trust that you're going to follow through and do it, and that if you're running into issues, you're going to ask for help. And so as a result, it's actually very valuable for you to have a framework that you can keep in your mind So that even though I or some other coach is not there to observe you, you can use this framework to sort of track whether you are practicing things in the right way. And the best way to explain this is by explaining the learning zone. So we start with this thing which we call the comfort zone. And the comfort zone is our existing habits. These are the things that we are comfortable at doing. We already know we have a high level of correct application on this, and we're able to do it consistently and easily already. So these are skills that we've already learned. The stuff in our comfort zone doesn't necessarily mean that it's productive and effective. You may be very comfortable at learning in a certain way, but that may not be serving you anymore, or there may be a way to upgrade it to make it even more effective. And so the idea is that there is really valuable learning outside of your comfort zone and basically learning things that are new and different to what you are already used to. One of my favorite sayings is, if you change nothing, nothing will change. So if you're trying to improve your skills, gain a result that you're currently finding difficult, then to make that difficult thing easier, your processes need to be better. And better means better. different. And so this actually creates this other zone and this is the zone which we call the learning zone. And so based on this, it becomes very simple. In order to improve very quickly, in order to develop new skills, we need to be in the learning zone instead of just in our comfort zone. But the reason this is problematic and the crux of why I'm teaching this to you is that in between the comfort zone and the new different learning zone is this band, which is called the fear zone. And the fear zone occurs because as we go from our comfort zone to the learning zone, we also change in the level of uncertainty. In our comfort zone, there is a very low level of uncertainty because they're things we're already used to. Whereas as we get further and further from the comfort zone, that uncertainty starts increasing. And that uncertainty is what creates fear and apprehension. This is what breeds the thoughts of what if I don't do this well? What if I make a mistake? What if I fail this? What if it doesn't work? And so we have to start by understanding that In order for us to learn anything new, new means different, and therefore it's outside of what we are used to. Therefore, everything new will have a higher level of uncertainty than everything we're comfortable with. And so learning and uncertainty are inseparable. Learning always creates uncertainty. And so if we have an existing habit of reacting to uncertainty, which means that as soon as we feel this uncertainty and we feel this fear, if our judgment, if our decision-making process is to retreat back into our comfort zone, you can see that this reaction habit, this decision-making, this judgment we make will directly stop us from growing. And so we will creep towards the edge of our comfort zone. We'll feel this fear. And then as soon as we feel this fear, we retreat back into the comfort zone. And then we spend time just spiraling around here, wondering why we're not improving, eventually burning out and not getting any different result. In this situation, there's a lot of busy work. There's lots of time being spent, but there is not much actual growth or movement happening because we're not embracing that uncertainty. And so what's key is to recognize that the uncertainty is unavoidable and to learn to be okay with that uncertainty and see that the uncertainty is you being more exposed to new and different ways of thinking and acting. The uncertainty is the signal, it's the indicator that you are entering into the learning, which means when you are offline, and you're trying to do your practice by yourself, I want you to keep this in mind and I want you to think about how much uncertainty am I feeling as I use this technique? You don't want to be so high in uncertainty all the way out here because now it's so uncertain that like you're going to make so many different mistakes, you're not even going to know what to do with it. Right? But you do want to have some level of uncertainty, which indicates that you are slowly expanding and expanding and growing. And then as your habits grow, your comfort zone starts expanding, which is what translates to being a more capable individual. Similarly, if you are practicing offline and you realize I'm not feeling a lot of uncertainty, then you know that probably what's happening is that you're actually coming back into your comfort zone. And this can happen very sneakily. You can start off trying to use a technique and then as you use it, you feel this uncertainty and you can actually just... subconsciously make it mentally easier by making it closer to something that you're already used to. And in doing so, you end up doing the technique incorrectly. And you may not realize it because you started off trying to use a technique, But as you go through it and you see the uncertainty and it feels uncomfortable, you can actually start slowly changing it into a way that feels more familiar and it's back inside your comfort zone. And so we've actually avoided the learning. This can happen very, very easily because learning happens in the brain. And it's about how we think about things more than just the technique that we use. So you can write a mind map. You can write notes in a certain way. And when you look at it, I'm technically doing the technique, but mentally I could be avoiding the actual thinking pattern that this technique is meant to train us in. And the number one most important factor to influence whether someone is successful or not was their mindset. Improvements in mindset led more significantly to improvements in other dimensions and learning performance more than any other dimension or any other variable that we measured. And that comes down to our ability to engage in this learning zone and to accept and thrive in that feeling of uncertainty. And I want to tell you something, share something that my therapist actually told me, which made it easier for me to engage in the learning zone. He asked me, what are you afraid of? You're afraid of what screwing it up means about who you are. And he told me to be very careful of therefore I am. And I want you also to be careful about the therefore I am. Are you afraid of, I can't get this right on the first try, Or is it, I wasn't able to get it right on the first try. Therefore, I am never going to be able to succeed. I am a failure. So next time you feel that therefore I am coming through, I want you to try to reframe this from a therefore I am into a now I will. So full stop. And then now I will, I made a mistake. Full stop. When that discomfort is felt and your brain is telling you, "Hey, I'm learning something new and it's not comfortable." And because it's new, we are able to control whether we see that as a positive thing and we persevere and reflect on it, or whether we shrink back into the comfort zone. So I thought that was important to share with you because, again, a lot of the time in your growth will be spent where I can't be there mentoring you and observing you. And I want you to be able to have this sort of central tenet to have as your north star when you're offline practicing things by yourself. And remember, we are here for you. rapid_start.name: Introduction to High yield phase.txt
rapid_start.content: In this early phase of learning, people come in with all sorts of different approaches and strategies and techniques. Often these techniques are things that were picked up over years of trial and error and experience. There are some parts that work and there are some parts that don't. The problem is when we don't know which parts are effective and why. For example, even a single technique can be interpreted and applied in many different ways. There are lots of things that go on in the brain because learning is complex. Just like if I were to tell someone to pour water into a cup, there are many ways that that single set of simple instructions could be interpreted. It could be done with a different hand. The jug could be held a little bit higher, a little bit lower. The rate at which it is poured could differ. The way the cup is being held could differ. When it comes to something like pouring water into a cup, the end result is all the same. Unless you splash water over your desk and then destroy your laptop or something. But when it comes to learning, it is a much more complicated process where each component influences the other components. So unlike, for example, pouring water into a cup where the room for error is very wide and there are lots of correct ways of achieving the same goal, it's a little bit more like performing surgery. Surgeons will train for years to learn the exact approach, the angle, the pressure at their fingertips that they're exerting to make each cut as consistent and as safe as possible because the room for error, that margin, is very small. Learning is more like surgery than it is like pouring water into a cup. But unlike surgery, the results and the processes are not very observable. It's happening inside our brain, we can't see what is going on. And so mistakes are less obvious and it's harder to pick up on them when they occur. It would be like operating on someone with your eyes closed and the only feedback that you were able to get was, did this patient die or not? It's very difficult to know whether it was an artery that was cut 15 minutes ago versus a suture that got infected a week later. So in this high yield phase, we're going to work on narrowing down all the possibilities of how you might be learning and make sure each major component is heading in the right direction. We want to make sure that there's no single part of your learning that is actually holding you back. We won't have enough time to optimize everything and master every technique to the best possible level. It would take weeks, even months sometimes to master even a single component. And that would mean that you're not improving in a significant way for a very long time. Because even if these other techniques are very good, this one thing could be holding you back and stopping you from getting any better than a certain level. rapid_start.name: Increasing your success rate.txt
rapid_start.content: So I started looking into how you can study more effectively and learn more efficiently, digging through the research papers and all that sort of stuff back in around 2011, 2012. That's when I was really digging my hands into it. And around about 2014, beginning of 2015, I developed a system that I was able to use very effectively that I taught to several of my students. And by that point, I was quite confident in being able to tackle most academic challenges. It's not as refined as it is now and what you're going to learn, but it was pretty good and definitely a big improvement from back when I was just a wee baby medical student. However, when I started teaching that to people, I was surprised to find that very few of my students were able to learn and apply this system effectively. In fact, you may not believe it, but actually the success rate of being able to teach someone this system was actually probably less than around 1% in that almost every single person that I taught this to was not able to learn how to apply the skills. Now, if I think about it, it makes sense. This is something that I had created through thousands, tens of thousands of hours of trial and error and experimentation and Of years of practice, it's not reasonable to expect that someone will be able to learn the system after just a few months or even several months of being taught it. So from that point onwards, 2015, moving towards the present, I looked really intensively at what the factors were that were stopping people from using the system. I knew it worked because I was able to apply it and it was a complete game changer to me. The students that were able to learn it and pick it up said that it was a game changer for them. Their results improved dramatically. So why was it that people weren't able to learn this? That was the big question on my mind from the years of 2015 all the way through to around 2019. And by the beginning of 2020, I'd figured out enough things so that I was able to train a person around 80% of the time. That means that around 80% of the people that wanted to learn this technique would be successful in being able to apply it and actually use it in their real practice to get the types of results that I was getting. So what was the big difference? Well, the big difference was that to be an effective student, it's about more than just having the study skills. You need a huge host of other attributes and other skills, perspectives, methods of practicing, methods of applying things in order to learn and develop such a complex and deep skill. And in the following few lessons, I'm going to give you the distilled version of that. The three top things that I found were contributing towards the failures of almost all of the unsuccessful cases. What that means is that if you are able to avoid these major three mistakes that are extremely common, then your chances of being able to learn this technique and apply it and create a system that really works for you to get the types of results that you want, go from less than 1% to at least 80%. The techniques we teach, the system that we teach, it works. We've taught it now to thousands of students to a level of consistency where we can say that confidently. It works. The system works and it can be trained and almost anyone can learn it to achieve these types of results. However, it's almost impossible to learn it if you make these three mistakes. So we're going to dive into that.

rapid_start.name: Triggers and tactics for accessing the growth zone.txt
rapid_start.content: So we previously talked about accessing the growth zone and how you need to work through the comfort zone, through the fear zone, into the growth zone if you want to learn new skills. And in this lesson, I want to give a bit more nuanced and practical advice because even if you technically know that you need to navigate through this, the experience can still feel emotionally difficult and challenging. And so it's important to have certain tactics that you keep in your back pocket that allow you to navigate through this a little bit more comfortably. So in this lesson, there are two different triggers that I want to share with you. By triggers, I mean things that you need to become aware of because these are the things that if you can recognize these triggers appearing, it gives you an early warning sign that you are about to react counterproductively to the fear zone. And then I'm going to give you a single practical strategy or tactic. So we'll start with the triggers. The first trigger is something that we call the reversion response to fear. The reversion response to fear is essentially a habit that develops over years of experience where for whatever reason, every time we are able to access the growth zone and we feel this fear, we have trained ourselves to say, no, this fear is a bad thing. Let's turn around, go back into our comfort zone. And this can happen due to a number of reasons. There might have been previous experiences that were very negative. There may have been people around you that said things that put you off and discouraged you. In some cases, there are entire cultures or societal beliefs that make it feel that feeling this fear means that you are not good enough or that you're going to fail. And so all of this messaging that builds up over years trains us to see this fear as something to avoid rather than something that is unavoidable. And so in simple terms, the reversion response to fear It's simply our tendency due to habit for us to go back into our comfort zone when we feel that fear and discomfort. So that's relatively easy to understand. The part that is tricky to understand, and this is the part you need to focus on, is what that fear actually looks and feels like. Because it is not you shivering there, you know, afraid to turn around because you think that there's a killer behind you with a knife. This is not this kind of fear. The fear we're talking about is actually more correctly summarized as discomfort. So if we think about the process of learning, Learning involves taking information which we have very little insight about initially if it's a new topic. And from here, we are extracting bits of relevant data. Let's just represent each of these bits of data with a small circle. And in order for us to take this raw, essentially meaningless data, and then convert it into usable, functional knowledge, that not only do we understand, but stays within our long-term memory, and that we can also apply it in the way we need to at a certain level of expertise. In order for those conditions to be met, inside our brain, there are a number of very complicated processes that need to happen, and a lot of decisions we need to make. We could make the decision that this feels very overwhelming, There's a lot to learn. There's a very high amount of volume. There's a very high amount of confusion. And so in order to avoid that, we're going to just write it down into our notes. We're going to put it into a notion database or another second brain app. We're going to make a bunch of flashcards on it. We're basically offloading that overwhelm. by bypassing the need for our brain to do any heavy lifting and thinking. And so this is not going to result in usable knowledge. It's going to result in a very usable notion database, but it's not going to be in your long-term memory and you're not going to be able to apply it in the way that you need to. And so if it's just a series of random facts you need to learn and you can come back to it at any time, that could be perfectly acceptable. You don't need to generate expertise every single time you learn something. But if you do need to generate expertise, and this is knowledge that you need to make your own to be able to use, then this is simply not going to be good enough. And so the alternative decision that we can make is that we are not going to bypass that heavy lifting, and we're going to process this information very deeply. We're going to do lots of very deep thinking. We're going to do lots of higher order thinking. We're going to do lots of comparing and grouping and contrasting. And our note-taking is going to reflect and facilitate this. This involves a lot of mental effort. This is mentally strenuous, engaging, very active learning. And it's also much more useful. This is very productive. For every hour you spend doing this kind of thinking, you generate more of this. And so even though it might take you longer to do this, you're actually getting a better result out of it. as opposed to doing lots of this where every hour you spend, the only thing that you get out of it is the feeling and the illusion that you are learning. But your actual knowledge is pretty low if you were to test yourself. So when it comes to making this decision, which path am I going to take? One of low effort, which is less effective, and then one of high effort, which is more effective. Our confidence and our comfort at taking this more effective path is strongly influenced by how successful we feel this path is going to be. If we feel and we truly believe that this path is the better path to take, then we are more compelled to take that path because we know it's going to be worth it. But if we think about all of this thinking and all of this comparing and the methods that are involved in really making learning active and effective, and we feel even more overwhelmed, we feel like there's more confusion and we're not connecting that with the end outcome. then that combination is much more likely to trigger the sensation of fear. The idea of what if I do all of this work and it's still not enough? What if I don't get the result? There is so much volume I need to cover, what if I'm wasting time? What if I can't cover everything quickly enough? And again, this is losing sight of the end result. It's believing the assumption that by not taking the path and by doing this, which is more comfortable and usually faster based on pages turned, content covered, it means that therefore we're gonna be able to achieve the result faster, but that's actually not true. Even though this process here is faster, it's actually less effective. So when you measure it based on what really matters, it's much, much slower. It only feels faster. So this is what it means to develop an awareness of this reversion response to fear as a trigger. It's understanding that it's normal to feel overwhelmed at the beginning. It's normal to feel like you need to make this decision about covering content faster and more easily versus doing something that takes a little bit more mental effort. And at this point, it's important to recognize that if you are making the decision to take the easier, more comfortable, you know, quote unquote, faster path, then that may actually be your aversion response to fear. It's not a strategic decision. It's actually an emotional decision because we're losing sight of the fact that it's an illusion of learning. And as a final example, one very subtle way that this can occur is actually during the way that you use a technique. So you may actually start off initially deciding, hey, I'm really going to give this path a go. I'm going to I'm going to do some deep thinking. I'm going to mind map things. I'm going to use nonlinear note taking. I'm going to do all of these things. However, this is not a single step. That's not one and done. It's not like once you make the decision that you're locked in. you can actually revert at any point during this. And this is the really subtle part about it is that you can technically be doing something like nonlinear note taking, but mentally you might be treating it like a checklist. So yes, you're technically connecting ideas together, but the actual relationships you're creating are very superficial. You're not thinking about it very deeply. Yes, technically you're creating a mind map and there's arrows going everywhere, but actually you're not really evaluating the quality of what you are creating. You're not thinking deeply about it. And this again will be a situation where we've confused the act of studying for the act of learning, which is not the same thing. And we have to remind ourselves that the point of using all of these different techniques and studying techniques and methods is to trigger the learning, which happens in our brain. It's a thought process. The learning process is the goal. And so you calibrate the way that you're studying to make sure that that's being triggered. And you catch yourself anytime you feel like, oh, it's really difficult. It takes a lot of mental effort. to evaluate it and think about the relationship so deeply. Yes, it does. And when you feel that, it's important not to revert midway through and make it easier for yourself by just thinking about it less deeply. So that's another version of this reversion response to fear where you have consciously made the decision to take the harder path. But subconsciously, you actually end up reverting back once you get into the swing of the technique, when you come up against that difficulty. And that difficulty comes up hundreds of times in a single study session. When you're learning something, every new concept that you're going to integrate, every decision you need to make about where to put this and where to fit it through, that involves discomfort. And that discomfort and difficulty is going to flare up constantly throughout that study session. And every single time you need to make the decision not to revert if it is a habit for you, which for most of us, it is a habit. So that's the first trigger to be really aware of. And it's, it's one of the most common triggers that we see. The second trigger is something called the misinterpreted effort hypothesis. Some of you may have actually heard about this before, because I actually talk about it on my YouTube videos and things fairly often. And it's almost an extension of the first one, and so it's not going to take as long to explain. The misinterpreted effort hypothesis is actually something that was found in research, where learning science researchers found that when learning is perceived to be more difficult, Learners believe it is also less effective. So the belief here is that easy learning is good. And you really can't blame anyone for believing this because it's Really, I mean, this is quite an intuitive thing to feel like. Like, if you're good at learning, it should feel easy. And it is true, when you're good at learning, it should feel easy. But it's easy because you're good at doing what most people find to be difficult. But if you're a beginner just learning it for the first time, like with any complex skill, it is difficult. It is engaging. And that cognitive effort, the mental effort, it never fades away. The only difference is that you get just... You just get used to it. It no longer stimulates that fear anymore. It's just like good. Like you feel it's effortful. It's challenging. And you think, oh, good. Yeah, I'm doing it right. And so the misinterpreted effort hypothesis is just an extension of the version response to fear. It's saying when you get to this point where you feel like the learning is getting harder, you have to understand that there's a good chance that that is... the mental effort going up, which is a good thing. Now, this is not always the case. Learning that is hard does not necessarily mean it is good. So even though easy learning is not good, hard learning is also not always. Good. There are ways of making learning very difficult for you that are not productive at all. So for example, if you're learning from multiple different resources, you've got a video, you've got textbook, you've got an AI chatbot that you're using to quiz yourself and ask questions, you've got a simple Google search that you're using as well. And you try to piece together all of the different information from all of these multiple sources over time as well, you know, and you're constantly going back to it. All of this makes it very difficult. And in learning science, this will be called the split attention. effect. And this is an example where our mental effort, what's called cognitive load, is actually going up, but it's not going up in a productive way. This effort does not help you to form meaningful relationships and connections. It doesn't help you to form usable knowledge. It's actually the perfect type of thing that, you know, if you can get A.I. to solve for you, that's great. You should you should definitely do that. That's an acceptable shortcut of reducing effort. So those are the two triggers to become familiar with, the reversion response to fear and the misinterpreted effort hypothesis. They're extremely, extremely common to the point, and they're so detrimental to the point where if you're not able to pick up on these triggers in yourself, there is almost a guarantee that you will not be able to improve your learning skills. And in fact, if you look at all the people that are not happy with their experience of learning to learn, and not just through this program, but just like any experience of learning to learn using any technique, Most of the time, the reason that they're not able to improve and they're not having a good experience is because they're not aware that they are constantly reverting. So now there's the practical tactic to help you work through all of this. And the strategy I'm gonna introduce you to is something called practice blocks. It's a very simple concept that you can apply for any skill that you're learning, in fact. The idea of practice blocks is a simple reframer technique. Reframing in psychology means that you're taking something that you perceive in one way and then you're changing the way you think about it. So the main issue here is that this reversion response to fear, this misinterpreted effort, all of this comes around because we feel that mental effort And we're worried that we're going to make mistakes. We're worried about failure. We're worried we're not going to learn it correctly. We're worried we're not going to cover the content properly. And so we're very outcome focused. And so the chain here is that there is fear related to not being able to achieve the outcome, which leads to a judgment call. saying, "Hey, let's do something else. Let's give up, let's revert," something like that. And so what practice blocks does is it simply reframes it to say, "Hey, your goal is actually not to achieve this outcome. We're actually going to change the outcome. And your outcome now is to make mistakes." is actually to find weaknesses. It's the idea that on the journey from wherever you are now to getting to mastery of the skill, there are a certain number of mistakes that you need to make. It's like a checklist. And once you're able to check off these necessary mistakes, there are no more mistakes to make and therefore you achieve mastery. And so the faster you can tick off and find these mistakes, Because although there are maybe 10,000 different mistakes that people can make, usually you're not going to make 10,000 of them. You're going to make 10 of them. And if you're worried about making these 10,000 mistakes, and that puts you off from making the 10 that are actually relevant to you, you can easily spend months or even years umming and ahhing and delaying your experimentation when actually you might be able to make all 10 mistakes within one week and just check all of these off very, very quickly and then achieve mastery faster than most other people. And that is precisely what practice blocks is all about. It's saying your goal in this block of time, you set yourself a certain amount of time, maybe it's two hours on a weekend and you're going to take a new technique one of the techniques that we teach you that you feel like you don't have mastery over that you're not confident about that you know there is mistakes and you feel a sense of fear at achieving the outcome and you can say good that's the technique i want to practice you set aside this time and in that time your goal is to find as many mistakes as you can the way you fail is by not finding any mistakes. And so by reframing the idea of learning a skill to be process oriented, we can use practice blocks to break past these mindset plateaus. And in doing so, we almost in a way can have a safe passage through the fear zone. And instead of being a fear zone, it simply just becomes a practice zone where The outcome we know and we expect is that we're going to make mistakes. And we can see that every mistake we make is going to take us closer and closer to growth. There's a little bit more instruction on how to do this on the page below for this lesson. Have a look through that and start scheduling in your first practice blocks.

rapid_start.content: The final reason why people don't tend to learn a lot of these techniques correctly is because of what we call selective learning. So, as you go through the program, you'll notice that there are a lot of techniques, and in fact, some people get to the end of fundamentals and say they didn't really learn much, but there are a lot of techniques there. You have to go through it very carefully. Selective learning is when someone will look at something and they will decide that they are not going to practice that because they make a judgment, then it's not relevant for them. And this could be due to a number of reasons. The first reason, which is quite strange, but I've noticed this quite commonly, is that sometimes someone will go through and learn about a technique, and because they've never heard of this technique before, they think this is not relevant to them. It's sometimes because they're looking for something, they have a kind of already existing belief about how learning is meant to be, and therefore, when they're going through, they're kind of just trying to confirm that their idea is valid. So, anything that isn't talking about what they think is sort of disregarded, and anything that does say what they think is kind of anchored onto, that's very dangerous and actually extremely illogical because you don't know what you don't know. And if you're joining this because you wanted to gain this expertise, and it's just not a smart decision to ignore the expertise. I don't really know another way to explain that. I feel it's just really obvious. Don't ignore the advice that you paid for. So, don't go through and just disregard things because it doesn't fit the mode of what you already thought. And don't disregard things because you've never heard of it. Maybe the reason you haven't heard of it is because of just the fact that it's not common and maybe that's the thing that you need. All of the techniques that we teach, every single one is being field tested. We don't teach anything that's only theory. We never teach anything that is only in the research. Nothing makes it into the course unless there's been extensive testing with real students in real authentic environments, where we know that it consistently produces success. Holding accountability for the advice that I give is something that I hold on a pedestal. So, you can trust that the techniques that you're going to learn and have already learned are ones that we know work in the real world, in real world environments, not like the perfect artificial lab setting where nothing else goes wrong. The reason that selective learning is such an issue is because, especially in the earlier stages, the techniques that you learn in the early stages are very rarely going to be the final version of the technique that you learn. And in many cases, the techniques that you learn in the fundamentals are component techniques of something that becomes a composite technique. So, a component technique is a technique that in itself is not complete. However, it doesn't mean that it's useless. A component technique can in itself be a technique that produces a specific outcome, like a note-taking technique or a technique for you to use in lectures or in class or in meetings. This is stuff that you can use and you can get an immediate benefit from. The reason we call it a component is because it doesn't integrate fully with all the other techniques that are there. It doesn't produce the full effect of what it could produce. Component techniques can come together in some weird sort of like Digimon combining polymerization sort of thing that create a composite technique. A composite technique is as it sounds a composite. So, it means that it's comprised of multiple component techniques. The more advanced techniques, they're all taught in the growth phases and beyond. However, in order to be able to use a composite technique, you need to have mastery over each of its components. The reason selective learning is relevant here is because it's not going to be clear to you what techniques are components and how it integrates with other techniques because it's frankly extremely complicated to try to explain it for every single technique. So, in the fundamentals, 95% of everything that's taught in the early high yield phase of the course is in some way a component technique. Where the failure to have good level of mastery over it would mean that you are unable to learn a later, more advanced technique. Just trust that it's going to be necessary for a later stage, especially if it's something that you're not already fairly good at or you're not using. It's extremely important that for new techniques that you're not already using, you practice those. Because those are much more likely to be core component techniques later on. Now, in some cases, certain techniques legitimately won't be relevant to your particular scenario. For example, if you never attend classes or lectures and all of your learning is purely through written material, then the techniques for what you do in class are not going to be techniques that are directly relevant. But the catch is actually that there are methods of thinking. For example, in that example, the in class techniques that we teach in fundamentals, there are methods of thinking that you would need to practice in order to use more advanced techniques later on, even if you were to consume that information in a written format. So the situations in which you can actually legitimately truly skip a technique are very, very rare. So if you're wondering about whether you can skip something, the best thing to do is actually just to ask us, tell us about your situation and say, "Is it okay for me to skip this?" and we'll let you know. In most cases, the answer is no. But if you think your situation truly is fairly unique, then sure, you can let us know. But the blanket rule would just be just learn everything that we teach you. Please do not skip things, especially things in terms of how to approach the course. Remember, these are the things that I spent years figuring out that made the success rate of people this go from less than 1% to over 80%.
