fundamentals_1.name: SIR overview.txt
fundamentals_1.content: We're now going to introduce a series of really important foundational techniques called SIR. You'll hear people in the community talking about this all the time. And it stands for Spaced Interleaved Retrieval. And this is actually going to become a foundational technique that you use just continuously in parallel with everything else that you learn throughout the program. So to give you a bit of a recap, make sure that your knowledge on this is fresh. I'm going to cover through some of the concepts that we've already covered before and then go into some of them in a little bit more nuance as well. So if we think about learning new information, information comes in here, it goes into our brain and our brain is then going to process this information and then it's going to be stored into our long term memory, or at least we hope it's going to be stored into our long term memory. And the process of storing information into the long-term memory is called encoding. And the process of then using that long-term memory, drawing information out of it, retrieving it is called retrieval, which is what the retrieval up here is all about. So in order for us to use and apply knowledge, we have to retrieve that knowledge. So retrieval is basically any time we use any information from memory. That is broadly called retrieval. And so there are lots of different ways that we can use our knowledge, which means that there are many, many, many different ways that we can perform retrieval. For example, testing ourselves or being tested, teaching something or explaining something to someone else, solving a problem. having a discussion, simply recalling a fact, right? All of these are different forms of retrieval and we're actually going to go and dive into all the different types of retrieval and then how you can make the type of retrieval work for you. But more on that later. For now, let's focus on this balance between the two processes of encoding and retrieval. In order to be a good learner, you have to do encoding very well, you have to do retrieval very well. If you don't do encoding very well, it means that you're going to waste a lot of time and a lot of effort because you're spending time and effort consuming information but if you're not processing it in the right way and therefore you're not triggering effective encoding well that information is not going to make it into your long-term memory and at the end of the day it's actually just going to be forgotten so this is a rubbish bin forgotten And if we look at some studies around how much people forget, we find that after about a week of learning, people tend to forget about 50% or so of what they've learned. And this is a very general number, but the idea is that there's a lot. There's a lot of what we learn which is forgotten. And some of you will say that, you know, it's like 90% of what you learn, you end up forgetting. And this is actually true. Encoding is a very specific, complicated, effortful process. And doing encoding correctly does require a fairly high amount of mental effort. And so while you will learn many techniques to develop this, we're actually focusing on retrieval first. And there are some very strategic reasons why we're focusing on teaching you retrieval first. The first one is to do with the return versus lead time. So if you imagine this here is the importance, and then this here is time. then in the early stages, the importance of encoding is going to be relatively low. Whereas the importance of retrieval is going to be relatively high. So this is retrieval and this is encoding. The reason is that encoding is complicated. It's difficult and it's complicated. It actually takes a very long time to learn. So you're not going to be able to go from, let's say, if you're a level one skill of encoding, you're not gonna get to be level 10 of encoding in one or two weeks. The difference that this would make might be that at level one, you're able to remember 50% of what you've learned a week later. So 50% retention, and you're able to do it to a low level of depth. So lower order learning only. So this means that you might only be able to do it for basic fact recall, which you can do at 50%. However, you're able to not really solve complex problems. And when your encoding is low, you're sacrificing both your attention and your depth. So this is really, encoding is the biggest needle mover. When you're at level 10, you might have a 90 plus percent retention after learning something the first time. And your level of depth may be very good. You may be able to do pretty good fact recall And you may also be excellent at using your information in complex ways, solving complex problems. So in order to go from really struggling with learning to being absolutely a learning genius, you know, a super learner, encoding is really where you're gonna get most of that long-term value. This is the thing that's truly life-changing when you're able to do it correctly. However, as you would expect with something that's life-changing, it takes a very long time. And so it is not a good strategy for you to start by putting all of your eggs in the encoding basket. It's going to take you weeks, months, maybe for some of you even years, depending on how fixed your habits are, to get to a very, very high level of encoding. And if you're going to wait years for that to happen, all of this time, you're just going to be struggling. And all of this time, you're going to be losing motivation, You're going to be spending and investing time on trying to improve your skills without getting the types of results that you want. And so that's not an effective way to improve and it's not a good time and it's unnecessary as well. And so by starting with retrieval, what we can do is we can teach you effective methods of retrieval that take a very short amount of time to learn. After you know, really only just one or two days of practice. So here we're measuring this in days. You can learn some really effective retrieval strategies that significantly improve your retention and your fluency and your ability to apply and recall that knowledge. Unfortunately, because it's not fundamentally changing your level of retention after the first time. Retrieval often involves a level of repetition. It means that you are not gonna be saving much time while studying. And in fact, you may actually spend even more time studying to do these retrieval strategies. Of course, by spending that time, you will get better results. But in the initial phase, your studying and learning time may actually go up slightly in order to make time to do this retrieval practice. Your results will also be going up. And then over this duration of time where you learn and get better and better at encoding, the amount of retrieval you need will start going down. And so you'll need less and less retrieval. So for a beginner learner, and I say the word beginner very generously, so I honestly would say most people, the amount of time that they spend on retrieval is going to be 70% plus of all their studying time. And that's completely normal. And then if I think about my own personal journey now after spending so many years learning to be better at encoding, less than 20% of my time is spent on retrieval. and I'm able to save a lot of that time by just encoding it better. So this is why we're focusing on retrieval first. And it's really, really important that you learn to do retrieval well before moving on to encoding. Otherwise, you're going to have a really big knowledge gap. So if you think about it like a leaky bucket, this is your bucket here. There's a bucket handle. And there's a... If there's a hole in this bucket and then water is constantly leaking out of this, Encoding very effectively is basically you plugging this hole so it's not leaking anymore. This is what it means by having higher levels of retention. but it's gonna take some time to plug this hole. And if you are constantly leaking out this entire duration of time until you plug that hole, that's gonna result in poor performance and undesirable results. So what retrieval is doing is saying, "Hey, let's have a cup down here to catch this water so that we can pour it back into the bucket." So yes, this is repetitive. This is potentially time consuming. However, it is necessary to maintain your performance overall while we plug this gap with better quality encoding over the coming months. And so with that being said, let's anchor into the core objectives of retrieval practice and spaced interleaved retrieval as a whole. When you do spaced interleaved retrieval, which again is basically just any kind of practice that causes you to recall information from memory, there are a few objectives that we want this to reach. And this is basically the objectives of why it's part of your learning system. The first major objective of doing retrieval practice is that we want to be able to find gaps in our knowledge. With learning, most of learning is invisible, which means that from the moment you start processing information on a page to the moment you actually test yourself, that time in between is unknown. You may have misunderstood something and just not realized it. And until you test yourself, that gap in knowledge actually remains invisible. The issue with having an invisible gap in your knowledge persist over a long period of time is that the consequence So if this is consequence here, and this is time, the consequence of having that gap in knowledge actually grows exponentially. At the very beginning, there's a very low consequence to having a gap in the knowledge. And this is because later on, you have so much time to fill that gap. But if you let that gap remain unaddressed for too long, and let's say that you find this gap over here, but let's say that you need to use this knowledge right here. this may not be enough time for you to fill that gap. So there may just not be enough time for you to fill the gap if you leave it too long. But there's actually another issue that makes it compounding in nature, which is that your knowledge builds on itself. So if this is a layer of knowledge and we've got another layer of knowledge above it, that kind of connects with this. And then when we learn another layer of knowledge above that, you know, this knowledge might connect down to this. So your schema of knowledge is always building upon what you already know. And so if there was an error down here in the early segment, then this actually influences how you've understood everything above it. And so it magnifies and amplifies the risk of misunderstanding and having errors in your knowledge. And so if you find an error over here, then it means that not only do you have a limited amount of time to fix that gap, you also now need to find all the other gaps and issues along the way that you have missed, and you have to fix all of those in the same amount of time as well. Most of the time, if you leave it too long to find that gap, you will not be able to fix it. There's just simply not enough time. So the best way to learn something is to try your best to get it accurate to high quality the first time you learn it, but accept that it's impossible to get it 100% at the very beginning. And so you test yourself and use retrieval practice to find the gaps in the knowledge very early on, within a few days, within a week. And we'll go through some of the systems and methods on how to set yourself up for correct spaced interleaved retrieval in the coming sub-lessons with a bit more examples. The second objective of retrieval practice is to improve your fluency of recall. Fluency is really talking about how quickly you can recall that information and to the level of complexity and depth that you can use that information. So if you have a very low level of fluency, then it means that it will take you, let's say, 30 seconds of thinking about the answer to figure out the answer. And maybe the types of answers and types of problems that you're able to solve are very simple. only simple fact recall, simple explanations of simple concepts. Whereas on the other hand, having a high level of fluency means that you might be able to recall it pretty much instantly. And the types of information and problems you're able to solve are very complex. And so it's not always necessary to have ultra high levels of fluency. There is lots of stuff that we learn where it's okay to spend 30 seconds thinking about the answer, or we can just look it up. And we only need to use it for very simple levels of recall. Whereas there are other types of information where it's completely unacceptable to have simple recall only. One of the best examples of this is if the reason you're learning this thing is to develop genuine expertise in this field. So think about knowledge that is foundational for your career, for solving real world problems, where you have to make high stakes decisions and use your knowledge for solving really important problems. then it's not going to be acceptable for you to not have a complex level of problem-solving ability. And it's not going to be acceptable for you to think about it for 30 seconds every single time you need to recall a fact or a concept. You need that information to be working together like a well-oiled machine, connecting back and forth in multiple directions very, very quickly. And so if expertise is the reason you're learning something as opposed to just learning a fact for remembering someone's name for like a networking event so that you don't forget it an hour later. fundamentals_1.name: Spacing.txt
fundamentals_1.content: So we're going to be focusing on spacing of spaced interleaved retrieval, which is the easiest one to get your head around. Basically, when you learn something for the first time, you will forget it. This is called knowledge decay. We've covered this before. And then when you repeat it again, your knowledge decay will slow down. So that curve is not as steep as it was before. And then when you repeat it again, that curve will flatten even more. And this is called the spacing effect. When you repeat things, your knowledge decays more slowly. And so utilizing spacing and retrieval practice means that after you learn something, you will just retrieve that knowledge again and test yourself in some form of recall. So the actual method you use when you're spacing can vary by quite a lot. But right now, what we're going to talk about is just the spacing interval and how to do your spacing. So the spacing interval refers to the gap between each of these sessions of retrieval. So a regular interval might be, let's say, two days, which means that you learn something, you retrieve it on, let's say, day one, and then you leave two days between the next one. So that'd be day three, and then you leave two days, that'd be day five, then day seven, so on and so forth. Whereas the alternative is something called an expanding gap interval, where you might start with day one, you might have two days for the first one, that's day three. And then instead of two days, you might increase that gap to five days. So now this is day eight. And then you might repeat this again, let's say 10 days later to make this day 18. And then you might do it a month later, so 30 days, so it'd be day 48. And expanding gap in most situations is where you want to go for a number of reasons. First of all, it's way more time efficient. Especially when you consider the fact that you're actually learning new information every day. So if you're using a regular spacing interval, it means that every single day, not only do you have to repeat the stuff that you did two or three days before, you then have to add on the new information that's dropping in every single day or every few days. So the amount of stuff that you have to cover with a regular interval is just increasing, you know, geometrically. And so that's mathematically unscalable. Unless you're learning something one-off and then you never really need to learn any new information again for like a very long period of time. Most people are going to find regular intervals hard to manage. And so a time-efficient way of doing that is instead using the expanding gap. It's also better for recall and studies are a little bit mixed on this, but I tend to lean towards the conclusion that it is a little bit better in terms of a learning experience. Number one, because it makes it more challenging, which is actually a good thing. If you space something after only two days, you're actually not really letting yourself forget. You're not testing your long-term memory in a more realistic setting. So for example, if you actually are only using an information like 30 days after you've learned it, you want to see how your memory fares up after 30 days of not reviewing it again. unless you plan to just review it every two days for the rest of your life. So having expanding gap actually simulates a real world kind of challenge more accurately, and it gives your brain the opportunity to forget and prune the weakest connections. So it shows you where those gaps are more accurately. So that challenge is actually a good thing. And the most common example of something that uses the spacing effect is flashcards. where you have a prompt or a cue and you test yourself on that whenever, usually your flashcard app will dictate for you when you do that. And most flashcard apps will use this expanding gap spaced repetition. There are different algorithms based on this. The most common one that's found is called the Leitner algorithm, but there's actually plenty of adaptations and modern flashcard apps are basically all equivalent to each other now in terms of their ability to give you a good expanding gap algorithm. And flashcards would be one example of a spaced retrieval method. But it's not the only type of spaced retrieval method. Any type of retrieval method, as long as you're spacing it, becomes spaced retrieval. So you could do a practice test for yourself on day three and then do a different type of practice test for yourself on day eight at a different level of complexity or a different level of depth, you know, testing on deeper details, whatever it is, that would be an example of spaced retrieval as well. Same thing if you were teaching someone And it doesn't even have to be the same thing. So you can teach someone, let's say on day three, but then on day eight, you do a practice test for yourself. And then on day 18, maybe you're doing some kind of problem solving or using it and applying it in some kind of project that you're building. So these would all be examples of retrieval practice that use a different method every single time, but it's spaced out. So now by using different methods of retrieval, this has actually become spaced interleaved retrieval, which we will cover in the next lesson. But the point right now is to say that a spacing schedule is just any time you leave some kind of interval between your retrieval sessions. And it's generally a beneficial thing to do for anything that you're learning. Now, what is the optimal spacing method? There's a lot of studies on this and the overall consensus is that it doesn't seem to really matter as long as there are some kind of expanding gap repetition. There is no exact science that we've figured out in terms of what the best timing is. But the rule of thumb that I have been recommending for years, which research consistently finds to be good enough, really, for most people, like this is your spacing schedule is almost never really going to be your bottleneck unless you're not spacing it at all. So this is going to be good enough for most people is to try to review it within one day and then one week and then one month. And you do not have to follow this exactly. It's a very, very general guideline to give you a rough idea of when you should try to cover it. So most of the time I will try to learn something. So let's say that the day you learn something for the very first time is day zero. I will usually try to review it on the same day. So same day review. And then I will try to review it again after, like within the first sort of three days or so, say less than three days. So if I learned something on the Monday, maybe I'll review it on the Wednesday. So I'll do it on the same day. I'll learn it for the first time on the Monday. I'll do a quick review of it on the same day, and then I'll test it again on the Wednesday. and then let's say I learned something on the Wednesday, I'm gonna review that one on the Friday instead. And then I'll do one roughly about five to seven days later. So usually for me, this means it's on the weekend. So if I learned something on the Monday, reviewing that same day, Wednesday, and then one day on the weekend, And then at the end of the month, I'll have a day where I'm reviewing everything in one go. Everything I learned over that last month, I'll review in one go. And so you can see this is not getting very exact because let's say for example, Wednesday, if this is Wednesday here, if Wednesday becomes my review day, well, the thing I learned on Monday, sure, there's a two day gap between that. But what about the thing that I learned on Tuesday? For Tuesday, there's only a one day gap. I don't care, it's good enough. And then on the Saturday, obviously, if I learned something on the Friday, when I review it again on the weekend, well, I only learned that the day before, it's good enough. Like I'm gonna cover this stuff later on the following weekend anyway, or I'm gonna catch up on it at the end of the month. So the idea here is that I'm just trying to get a general trend of expanding gap occurring across my learning. I'm not trying to optimize it so that every new piece of information tracked down to the daily level is exactly following the same spacing interval. That's really not going to make a measurable difference in your performance. It's going to add a lot more logistical burden on your scheduling, and it's probably not going to result in a better performance or attention at the end of the day either. One good thing that I will say about flashcards though is that the app generally controls it. And because flashcards are great for learning very simple, discrete facts and doing that low level recall. So this is a great method for lower order, very simple, direct fact recall. This is something that you can actually run in parallel. So you don't even have to worry about the spacing schedule here. All you need to do is let the algorithm run. And then whenever the app tells you, hey, you've got some cards to do, just do the ones that are due when they become due. And if you find that as you're doing this, you can't keep up with the amount of flashcards you need to do because the amount is overwhelming and every day you're spending like hours and hours and hours doing these flashcards, then that is not an issue with your spacing. That's actually an issue with having too many flashcards because your encoding ability is not high enough or you're misusing flashcards and you're essentially overusing it. And that's kind of a different, problem that's related to a different part of your learning system. But with flashcards, it's very much sort of a set and forget. You make the flashcards, your app tells you when to do them. You can just do them. They don't have to be sort of scheduled into your normal spacing schedule. So that is spacing. And the next thing we'll talk about is the interleaving aspect of this, which is talking about what do you actually do on each of your spaced retrieval sessions.

In the later stages, there are a few things that we are able to do now because we did it this way in the early stages. First of all, because we found our higher order gaps early on, we don't have to worry about those so much. The reason is that when you find a higher order gap and you fill them, your knowledge is stronger. And because it's higher order, which means that it's integrated and it's in a relationship, it means that your memory is gonna be deeper and stickier for that gap that you've already filled. So once you fill a higher order gap, it's actually less likely that you're gonna forget that same gap later in the future. This is not the case with lower order information. Lower order information is much more detailed. There are naturally less things connecting to it. So our memory on these things is going to be weaker. We're more likely to forget it. So this is the type of thing that First of all, strategically, it's better to tackle this stuff closer to an exam or some kind of assessment when you need to use the knowledge because it's gonna be fresher and it's gonna be easier to cover the stuff after you've done your higher order knowledge because this lower order stuff has a really good foundation to connect to. If you start with the lower order thing without having a really solid higher order foundation, nothing's going to make sense. Nothing is going to feel relevant to you and you're going to have just lots of random pieces of information with nowhere to belong. Your brain doesn't really know how to think about it. If you do it in this order, your brain knows how to think about the lower order information. So even though it is quite tedious and time consuming to go over all the tiny little details, at least those details feel more relevant building on that

So even though it is quite tedious and time consuming to go over all the tiny little details, at least those details feel more relevant building on that higher order foundation. So mixed order really just means that you're doing lower order and a level of higher order. If you're really confident in your higher order knowledge up until this point, you might just focus purely on doing some lower order retrieval to just get those details whacked in before you need to use your knowledge. Or if you want to keep testing your higher order knowledge in different ways, just continuing to deepen that mastery, you'll throw in a little bit of higher order in there as well. The low volume part means that we want to be targeted with the gaps that we're identifying. And so there are a number of ways that we can do this. The first way of doing this is that when we do that early stage testing, For example, if we're teaching ourselves or if we're building some kind of app, because it's a high volume strategy and because it's higher order, we will probably find gaps in our lower order as well. The only difference is that we're not concerned about filling them, but you can still note them down to give yourself this really targeted list of lower order gaps to come back to later. And so automatically by doing this, you've actually set yourself up for a really effective later retrieval session that's automatically more targeted. Another way of making it more targeted is actually by testing yourself. By testing yourself, you realize where your gaps are without having to basically go from start to finish until you find a gap. And so you can make this more effective by prep or by testing. And so this is the general trend that you want to have when you're arranging your retrieval sessions is that you want to start kind of big picture, high volume techniques that find lots of gaps and then progressively funneling narrower and narrower with our knowledge until we're at those fine details, which are easier to learn once you've got the big picture. Again, when it comes to something like flashcards, which is a fantastic method to use for lower order retrieval, you want to have that starting sort of in that mid to early stage of learning onwards where you've got those flashcards kind of running in parallel. fundamentals_1.content: So we'll now go through a few more nuances and tips relating to applying correct retrieval. And if your brain exploded after the interleaving lesson, don't worry, this one is a lot simpler to wrap your head around. There are only three concepts that I'm going to explain in this lesson. The first is the idea of planned versus opportunistic retrieval. The second is the idea of gap filling. So once you've found a gap, how do you actually go and fill that gap? And the third is this quick recap on the limitations of retrieval. And this one especially is gonna have implications on your expectations for progress and how your learning efficiency will grow over time and how your learning system will evolve. So the first one is about plan and opportunistic retrieval. So, If you are in a purely academic setting, then almost all of your retrieval has to be planned. This means that you are deliberately making time in your schedule to have retrieval sessions. This is not the case if you are learning in an environment where you naturally have to use that knowledge as part of your day. So for most professional learning, so let me just recap on that here. So if you're an academic learner, You know, usually it's planned. However, for a lot of professional learning, there are opportunities for you to do your retrieval without needing to actually dedicate any additional time, which is kind of the best case scenario here because you're saving time. So let's say that I'm a doctor and I'm learning about heart disease and heart attacks. Now on my day-to-day basis, I may be seeing patients with heart disease all the time. And so I may use that knowledge of heart disease that I'm studying every single day. And that is already creating a interleaving retrieval practice for me. It wasn't deliberate, But because, remember, retrieval is any time you're using that knowledge and recalling it, as a result, that is indeed retrieval practice. And so you can view that as a form of your spaced interleave retrieval. So if you study something on a Monday about heart disease, and then on Tuesday, you see a patient with heart disease, and now you're thinking and recalling the knowledge that you learned about, that is retrieval. retrieval. Now all you have to do is retrospectively label what that session was. So you can say, okay, so I used my knowledge in this kind of way. Is that a higher order method or is that a lower order method? And is that matched to the knowledge type? And then that allows you to find where the gap might have been. So let's say that you only needed to use your knowledge at a fact recall level, and you were using it purely declaratively in this example. So, okay, the declarative part may be perfectly fine, but you know that there's gonna be a gap in your higher order because you didn't really use your higher order knowledge for that patient interaction. you now have two options here. Number one, you can actually just do a planned session. You can create a planned retrieval session specifically on the higher rotor part that you think you might have missed. Number two, you can try to convert that experience so that it uses more higher order knowledge. So there may be an opportunity for you to recall and utilize the information at a higher order, creating a different type of strategy, evaluating the management plan you create, whatever the application is, there might be some way for you to tap into a higher order, more strategic, more big picture view of the topic. that allows you to test your higher order knowledge as well. But sometimes this is not the case. Actually, oftentimes this is not the case because time pressures and job responsibilities mean that you can only just do something a certain way and then you just kind of have to move on and move on with the next task. But either way, you are saving time. Instead of having to have extra time scheduled in to test yourself and do this recall, that time is just naturally there as part of your day. And in a lot of situations, if you are learning things that directly relate to the work that you're doing on a daily basis, and you are using that knowledge in exactly the way that you need to use it, and there's a very close transfer between what you learn and then how you perform in your job, then you may actually not even need any planned retrieval practice whatsoever. Your opportunistic practice may be enough, for you to keep that memory strong. Also, you may not need stronger, deeper memory on that. What I was saying before in terms of developing expertise, you need to have good long-term memory and retention, and you also need to have good level of depth. But let's say that you only need to know enough about something to solve like a very isolated specific problem or to present something to a client or, you know, you don't need to develop like true expertise on this. So you don't necessarily need to have optimal space interleave retrieval to do that. Just natural interleave retrieval you're getting at work may just be enough for you to achieve that goal. Likewise, even if you're studying for an exam, you know, you may be doing planned retrieval sessions, but you may not need to have like perfect, perfect for every single piece of knowledge that you're learning. If that's not really how your exam is going to assess you on it, now it's a different idea if you're looking further than the exam and you, you know, like let's say this knowledge is something that you need to develop expertise in the future, that's a different story, right? Then in which case long-term it's going to be beneficial for you to think about developing expertise regardless. But, you know, if it's just like some random elective paper that you really have truly no interest in at all, it's just to fill a grade and you just need it for an exam that's coming up in a month from now, like, Hey, whatever, like you don't need PhD level higher order expertise. Just get it good enough that you can answer the questions. Get it good enough that your memory isn't too leaky. Get it good enough that your attention and depth is there and then fill in the lower order stuff as you need to. So, you know, there's a level of strategic. Don't be optimizing purely for the sake of optimizing because optimization is always goal oriented. And if your goal is way below your standard of optimization, then that's actually going to be counterproductive. So that's just a, you know, one side point about the standard that you can hold yourself to. There is another flip side to this though, which is the idea of how if you do have opportunistic interleaved retrieval, you can actually make the opportunity even greater by being proactive about what you're learning. So sometimes there's a situation that arises where you can choose what you will be learning and studying, but you usually can't control what you are doing at work. So you can't control the opportunities for retrieval, but you can control what you're gonna be learning in the weekend before. So if you know what kind of things you will naturally be forced to retrieve and what type of knowledge you're gonna have to work with, and you know that at some point you're going to have to learn that, then instead of doing it, let's say, like in the order of a curriculum or in order of like some certificate course, do it in the order that you're going to encounter at work so that you are able to make use of the opportunity that you wouldn't normally be able to make use of. So I'm going to go back to the medicine example because it's really simple to understand. Let's say that you are working in the cardiology department right now, But then two months from now, you're going to be working in the renal department, working with kidneys. So if you need to study both about the heart and the kidneys at some point, but your course curriculum teaches you kidneys today, but then heart stuff two months later, just flip it around. Learn the heart stuff now because you have the opportunities every day that you can reap. and then learn the kidney stuff later. That's a very simple example. Obviously, it's more complicated than that in real life. But this idea of sort of trying to match what you learn based on the natural interleaving opportunities that you've got available to you is another thing that can make the retrieval burden much more manageable. The next point here is about gap filling. Once you've done your retrieval, so you've tested yourself in some kind of way, you record the information and then you have found a gap. Great, this is the win condition of retrieval. So how do you then go and fill that gap so that when you test yourself again, you just don't have the same gap all over again? The good news is that retrieval and testing yourself automatically increases the stickiness of that knowledge when you go to fill the gap. So you could learn something with a certain method. I'm not gonna go into the method right now 'cause it actually doesn't matter. So let's say you use a method of learning and then you've learned some piece of information and then you test yourself on this information and you get it wrong. So a gap has been found. You use the exact same method to learn that information again naturally, your attention is actually going to be better than it was before. So you may not require anything else other than to revisit the material, read it again, think about it a little bit more, make sense of it in relation to how you just got it wrong. And that alone might be enough and you don't have to do anything else. However, If you feel still a little iffy about it, you're not too confident, or you do try that and then you test yourself again and you find, okay, the gap has repeated itself, then we need to kick it up a notch. And the way that we would kick it up a notch is by connecting it even further to how we tested ourselves and evaluating the types of connections that we had formed. So we're basically making the assumption, hey, the way that I've connected that information and form that information in my mind and the way that it's made sense to me wasn't enough or maybe wasn't accurate. So you're going to deliberately look for a different way to connect that. So this is basically giving you permission to go down the rabbit hole a little bit, spend a little bit more time. You might not want to spend this amount of time the first time you learn it because why would you? But once you've gotten it wrong sort of two times in a row, it's probably worth spending a little bit more time learning a little bit out of scope beyond what you would really need, trying to find more ways to make it interesting to you, to make it more relevant to you, to connect it more with problems that you understand easily, or to create some analogies that force you to think about it at a big picture. And by doing that, it's gonna force a few more connections, force some different and new types of connections, and then hopefully that will allow that gap to be filled. more effectively and then sometimes you have to do this again and again and for some reason there are some pieces of information that just really hard to wrap your head around but you just continue this process and with enough repetition they will get filled and so the final thing here is the limitations of retrieval as I mentioned before retrieval and encoding are two sides of the same coin and so encoding is the thing that's going to make your bucket stop leaking retrieval is the thing that's going to keep filling that bucket giving you the time and space to patch the leak. So encoding is where you get that great long-term benefit and transformation. Retrieval is what gives you that short-term performance so that you're not struggling so much immediately. Retrieval becomes your foundation in the short-term and coding becomes the foundation in the long-term. And it's really important to recognize that because of the natural limits of retrieval. So first of all, retrieval is really only as good as your initial encoding is going to be. If your initial encoding is absolutely terrible, that's a terrible way of spelling terrible. If your initial encoding is absolutely terrible, then you're going to have lots and lots of gaps. Which means that number one, your retrieval session is going to take longer. It's going to take longer to fill those gaps and it's going to overall mean that you need to test yourself more times to check that all of those gaps have been filled. Retrieval is also by nature a repetitive process, right? Which means that this is going to loop again and again and again. And so each gap in your knowledge requires like five times more time. sort of repetition for that to eventually be filled. So it creates a lot of learning debt. And ultimately, if your ability to gain expertise is hinging on just retrieval practice, you're gonna have a tough time unless your deep processing is just really, really, really good. And even then you're gonna have a tough time eventually. It's just a matter of the work volume being high enough and you not having enough time. But there's sort of this mathematical limit on how good retrieval can get. But a lot of people are in this situation. A lot of people, the only studying method is like practice tests and flashcards. That's it. Like their encoding method is so terrible that it doesn't do anything. It's just writing and rewriting notes, constantly rereading notes. And then 99.9% of their learning is actually just happening from retrieval. And so this is the reason why you would feel like you're constantly relearning things, your memory is shot, you're spending lots of time doing very repetitive, boring, tedious types of studying, and you're gonna hate the process of learning. because you're not really learning. You're just kind of gap filling constantly. And so yes, focus on being really good at this retrieval and practice doing the spaced interleaved retrieval until you get enough of a feel for what it's like using different types of retrieval methods. So for most people, I would recommend stay at this level, just practice your spaced interleaved retrieval. Don't go ahead and learn any other new techniques. Just stay at this point practicing a few different types of space interleave retrieval and feeling the benefit of it, getting used to testing yourself in this way, creating schedule for yourself for maybe a couple of weeks. Just spend a couple of weeks staying at this level, let's say two weeks. It's not enough for you to master this. I mean, you might be able to master the actual technique because the techniques themselves are fairly easy, but it's not enough for you to feel like supremely confident in doing space interleaved retrieval like properly for the rest of your life. You know, there's still going to be things that you can tinker around with and improve, but you don't need to stop here for like the next six months. You can continue to work on the encoding thing and you can continue to slowly build up your encoding skill. Just get to a point right now where your retrieval is giving you that short-term benefit, which might take two weeks, might take three weeks, might take four weeks. And when you feel like, okay, you're getting the hang of it, go back and continually work on your encoding and slowly see the effect that that has on your retrieval needs. And so over time, you should feel that you need a little bit less retrieval. You should not be deliberately reducing the amount of retrieval that you have in your system unless you naturally find that there are less gaps. This is really the important part that I've noticed across the thousands of students that have gone through the program and I've seen some of the mistakes that they've made. which is that some people will think that because as their encoding gets better, they should naturally have better memory. So they deliberately do less retrieval. You will automatically, without trying, end up doing less retrieval because when you test yourself, you will find less gaps. So because there are less gaps, you will spend less time doing the retrieval. You do not need to try to reduce the amount of time you spend on retrieval. And in fact, I would say that you can use the amount of time you need to spend on retrieval as an indicator for your progress on encoding. because that's gonna be an indicator for how many gaps that you're still leaving behind with your encoding methods. And so I wanna make this really clear. The amount of time you spend studying overall might not improve for most people like weeks, but for some people even months. depending on how much you practice and how much studying volume and therefore practice volume you have. And this is normal. What we want to get to is we want to get to being able to do this accurately first. Once we do this accurately, we have... a reliable base of short-term improvement. Then we work on doing encoding accurately, and it takes time. When this is accurate, the gaps naturally start going down, which naturally feeds into less retrieval. And then we have more time available to potentially learn about doing encoding better. and then we can again reduce the amount of time there. So this should all happen very naturally, but the important part is that we're focusing on building the accuracy of technique first. You will not get good results or improvements in time efficiency If you focus on time first, accuracy leads to efficiency. Accurate methods and strategies result in the best results, which means less time spent relearning plugging gaps. And that's where the time efficiency really comes from. So these are just a few smaller tips around retrieval and how to sort of think about adding retrieval into your learning system. Again, for some people who, you know, depending on where you're trying to get to and the type of opportunities you have for retrieval in your everyday life, you may not need to deliberately do retrieval at all, or it may be as simple as, taking the opportunity of taking what you've learned and then teaching it to a junior to create and plug that single higher order gap you might have in your natural work into leaving.

fundamentals_1.name: The problem with studying.txt
fundamentals_1.content: Welcome to the course. We're going to get started with some few basic principles that you need to understand about how learning works and different approaches to learning. Now, most of you probably have never even heard of different approaches to learning. You may have heard of different techniques, you may have heard of different strategies, you may have heard of spaced repetition or active recall or doing flashcards or different ways to write your notes. And those are all good and they're all techniques and yes, they have their place, but actually there are some fundamental ways of looking at learning that actually make a very big difference and many of the techniques that we teach throughout the mini course that you're about to go on to soon as well as our full course a lot of these techniques are based on different approaches to speed things up so the way that we look at it is that instead of learning to ride your horse faster to win the race what we're teaching you is that you can actually bring a car you know in the analogy sense, bring a gun to a knife fight. And so we're trying to change the way that you view learning. So there's a few things that we need to understand. And the first thing that I actually want to talk about is the idea of learning through repetition and memorization and why that is very unsustainable when it comes to not only learning when you're at the lower levels in high school, but especially so when you're in university or later years and getting used to that system and getting really good at learning that way is not going to get you very far. So let's actually have a very quick look at what I mean here. So there's this very famous curve, it's called the forgetting curve, and we will talk about this a little bit more in the course as well. However, it says that when you learn something, let's say that's this blue dot, we learn something for the first time, we actually forget this information very quickly. And so our knowledge, which is on the Y axis over time on the X axis, reduces very, very quickly. And then if we were to repeat that information, let's say the green arrow immediately after that or shortly after that, our rate of forgetting the information would be a little bit slower. And then if we were to learn it again sometime later in red, we would actually forget it slower still. So obviously this is a good thing. We're able to learn something and through repetition or in this case, spaced repetition or more specifically, because the gap between each repetition is increasing, this is expanding gap, spaced repetition, we're able to increase our level of retention because we're able to hold on to this information more. Makes sense so far, right? Nothing wrong with this and this is indeed a very good principle to use and if you're not using this already, you definitely should be. However, there's a problem with this in that it definitely requires this level of repetition and it depends on this curve here. And so obviously if we are forgetting things very, very quickly, then we are going to need to repeat it relatively soon in order to hold on to that information. And so if we're able to change this curve so that instead of forgetting it this quickly we can actually shift this curve up so that the first time we learn it we're actually forgetting it much more slowly then it means that we don't need some of these repetitions, we can actually repeat it much less and still have the same level of retention at the end of the day. And so a lot of the time spent studying is actually wasted because that time is spent on simply repeating and relearning the things that we've already forgotten. However, when we look at this, we will quickly realize that that is not sustainable. And the reason is because of something called the Leitner system. So the Leitner system is a method of spaced repetition that allows us to prioritize the information that we forget more often. So for example, let's call this box number one, box number two, and box number three, and box number four. We take a piece of information that we've learned the first time, and we might repeat this initially, let's say, four hours later. And if we get it correct, then we would repeat it again later, let's say 12 hours later. So this gap is 12 hours. And then we repeat it again, and then this gap is now, let's say 24 hours. And then we repeat it if we get it correct one more time. And then the next time we repeat it, maybe it's one week away. But then what happens if we get it wrong? So if we get it wrong, we actually go back a box. So at any point, if we get it wrong, we go back a box. So if we got it right at the beginning, after four hours, then 12 hours later, we need to repeat it again. If we get it wrong, then it goes back and we need to do it again in four hours. If we get it right, 12 hours later we get it right again, and then we repeat it again in 24 hours. We get it wrong, then we need to repeat it again in 12 hours. We get it wrong again, we need to go back to four hours. So you can see how information has the potential to just constantly go back and forth between these boxes without you ever having effectively cleared it because you want to get to the point where you don't have to repeat it for at least the next few months or at least until your exam or assessment. Otherwise, you're just going to continue accumulating more things to repeat. Now, the late nurse system is often talked about when it comes to flashcards, but it's actually the same for basically any other type of revision that is based on memorization. When you repeat something, you need to get to the point after repeating it that the gap between the review that you've just done and the next review is after your test or exam. Otherwise, by the time you set your test or exam, you may have forgotten that piece of information. When we look at it this way, we can see that information has the possibility to constantly flicker between these boxes so that every day we're adding new information from class or lectures that we are learning, and because we are forgetting very, very quickly, initially, it means that we're getting a lot of these wrong. And because we get a lot of them wrong, we're not able to progress and clear them sequentially. So the rate at which we get new information that we need to repeat is faster than the rate at which we can clear them. So one week later, instead of having just that week's worth of information to repeat, and then the week after that, being clear of that and having the next week's worth of information, we may end up after two or three weeks having a full two or three weeks worth of information we are needing to repeat on maybe a single Saturday. which becomes not viable. And as time goes on, it gets harder and harder to hold onto this information. The retention actually ends up going down because now we're even more overloaded and it forms this negative spiral. So we have less time to study because we're spending so much time relearning the stuff that we are forgetting from previous study sessions. And then because we have less time to study, we're less able to retain the information we're learning now, which means that a week from now, we're going to have even more stuff that we forgot, which means that two weeks later, we're going to have even more that we need to revise. So it continues to build and build. And this is often why students that use systems like this that don't fundamentally change the rate of forgetting, These students who don't change the rate of forgetting and still forget things very quickly and struggle with this kind of constant re-revision, they will struggle in later years of high school or in university, especially when it comes to competitive degrees where everyone is an excellent student and now you have to be excellent among the excellent. A lot of the times people will fall off on the academics, which previously may have been their strength because of the fact that they're using a system of studying that's mathematically not viable. It does work for some people, but the amount of time it takes is a lot. And also, you have to be a little bit lucky in that you know, thousands, if not millions of students will be studying with this kind of strategy. For the majority of these students, it's not going to allow them to achieve at the top level. And for the minority of students that do, they probably have an approach to learning that allows them to fundamentally shift this line so that our rate of forgetting is slower, so we actually don't need as many repetitions. So to summarize, spaced repetition is good, lateness system is good, but if we're forgetting things very quickly because our approach to learning is very memorization based, or very rote learning heavy, or we're spending lots of time studying, but after we finish writing our notes, we still don't have a clear idea about what we've written, then it means that we are setting ourselves up for sabotage later on, later down the line, and that's not going to work. So we're changing the way that you're seeing that, and we're really going to be shifting your focus from this memorization focus of learning towards one that's a lot more sustainable and shifts that curve. So all the techniques that we teach later on in the course will teach you how to shift that curve fundamentally. But if this is the case, and there's actually other ways of learning, then there's a really interesting question that you have to ask yourself, which is, why is it that people only give tips on things like active recall and spaced repetition if all it's doing is helping you get better at a game that is already difficult to win? Why aren't people talking more about how to change the game entirely? Why aren't people talking more about how to shift this curve. Why do people only give advice on when to do these repetitions, how to do these repetitions, you know, when to do it as base repetition, how to do it as active recall. And the reason is because people only give tips on what they know. And there is a lot of information out there I'll be using the terms neuroscience and neuropsychology and learning neuropsychology throughout the course. When I talk about neuropsychology, we're really talking about the frameworks and theories behind how learning works. Whereas when we talk about neuroscience, we're talking about more at a physiological basis, neurotransmitters, neuroplasticity, and how neurons from a biological point of view allow the frameworks and theories of neuropsychology to play. When you look at the research in these fields, you'll find that there is a lot of findings on different methods and approaches to learning that are not at all mainstream, yet can be extremely effective. Now, most people don't know that simply because it's not already common, it's not taught in schools, most teachers aren't trained in this. In fact, most people, unless they're in educational research, wouldn't really know about this. And so even though the knowledge is out there, it's not like I'm summoning it from the universe myself directly, the information is there. However, it's kind of hidden behind a lot of research articles and things, and it's very difficult for a student like yourself to actually take that and do something meaningful with it, whereas something like spaced repetition or active recall, Pomodoro technique, these are things that you can learn straight away, you can apply straight away. Yes, they're effective, but again, it doesn't change the fundamental game. And unfortunately, because of the Dunning-Kruger effect, the level of confidence that people have that are giving advice tends to be reflective of overall a low level of knowledge. So if you look at this graph, you'll see that there is a really high level of confidence when you know a little bit because compared to knowing nothing, that's a lot. However, as you start to learn more and more, you realize that there's so much more about this that you don't know, your confidence goes down, and then it starts picking up again slowly over years and years of time when you realize that actually you are becoming an expert across all these years. But this first point, this highest point of confidence when there's not a lot of effort, this is actually called Mount Stupid. And so a lot of people are, while intentionally, on Mount Stupid, they know a little bit about studying, they've had a little bit of experience, they've tried certain things before and they think, wow, this is the best thing ever, and they teach it and they're very, very confident about it, but actually they haven't spent years and thousands of hours working with students, looking at the research, experimenting with it, not only themselves, but with other students as well to see what works. So when we introduce some of these techniques to you, some of them will be easy, some of them will be hard. Keep in mind that when I use a technique and I demonstrate it to you, it may seem a lot easier when I'm doing it, but that's because I've spent over 10 years in this process and I've had a lot more experience down this journey. You can't expect to get this perfect straight away. You do have to spend a little bit of time to practice some of these techniques. Some of them you will be able to use straight away. Some of them will require practice, but that's where that hard work, effort, diligence, dedication really comes in. And at the end of the day, it's actually very logical to think about all of this because if you're just studying the same way as everyone else and everyone is kind of giving you the same advice, then you got to wonder, How good can you possibly be by doing the same thing as everyone else? And so to help yourself free yourself from the Dunning-Kruger curve and know exactly how much you do know about it, a really simple activity that you can do is think about what conditions are necessary in order for your belief or opinion to be true. and I actually want you to do this activity with me. So there are a number of different beliefs that people have and there are very specific conditions in which this belief would actually be true. So let's have a look at some of these. So there are a number of misconceptions that people have about learning and what it takes to be a successful student. And as someone who I would say I have been a successful student and have worked with a lot of students that were previously unsuccessful and transforming them to become successful, There are a few ones that I've picked as my favorites. And so let's do this activity together where we look at what are the conditions in order for these statements to be true. One of the first ones is, how good can you be by doing the same thing as pretty much everyone else? Well, in order for you to use the same techniques as everyone else and to perform significantly better than them, The conditions in which that must be true are that either you are very lucky or that you try significantly harder. Now, the lucky one is kind of stupid. We all know that we don't want to create a plan based on us being lucky. However, the effort one is a little bit more nuanced. So the thing is that the amount of effort you put in obviously has an impact on the results that you're going to get. However, When you're trying to get to a very top level of performance, let's say you're not aiming to be in the top 10%, but you're aiming to be in the top 1% or the top 0.1% or even less of students either locally or nationwide, especially if you're at university and everyone in your class was in the top 1% and you need to be in the top 10% of them. When you look at it in that perspective, then even if everyone tries their hardest, The way you try is actually going to make a massive difference. So first of all, at those high levels, everyone is trying hard. So if you want to be 20% better than someone, but you can only try 3% harder, obviously the maths on that doesn't really stack up. But even if everyone is trying their best, People are gonna have different results. And we see this very commonly with highly competitive degrees. We see this a lot with the medical field, people trying to enter into medical school. Everyone is trying extremely, extremely hard and yet still only a very small percentage of those people will actually succeed. The reason is because it's less about the effort you put in And it's more about the direction in which you're putting that effort in so that when you put the effort in, you actually go in the direction that you want to be going. So the conditions for same techniques but different results to be true is either substantially more effort or you're just a lot luckier so obviously that ends up being a misconception the reality is that you can't really get that much better by doing the same thing as other people generally people that perform better are doing things better they're behaving better they're studying differently they're using different processes and it could be as as simple as the way they're writing notes but often it is more complex and there's an entire system that's built around that Another favorite of mine is that people have different learning styles. So in order for people to have different learning styles, for example, often people will say, I can't learn through reading and writing, or I can't learn through someone speaking to me. In order for that to be true, the condition that needs to be met is that your human brain is incapable of learning that way effectively. The truth is that the research shows and is what many educational psychologists and researchers will comment on is that the human brain is an incredibly powerful learning machine and is adept at learning in a multitude of different ways and a multitude of different environments for a multitude of different disciplines. So generally speaking, You can learn in virtually every style. However, the extension of this is that people do have different preferences and there are different things that you're trained at. But again, what the research shows is that those who train themselves at learning through multiple styles of learning and have stronger preferences for mixed mode learning rather than single focus learning, they consistently are able to outperform and get better exam and test results as well. Another assumption about learning is that most learning is done through rote learning or memorization. The condition for that to be true is that your brain is not capable of learning this material more effectively through anything other than rote learning and memorization. We know this is not true because you don't apply this method of thinking to anything else you learn in your life. And potentially one of my favorite assumptions to disprove is the idea that this is something that you can start picking up and getting better at instantaneously. Learning to study is a skill. fundamentals_1.name: Marginal gains.txt
fundamentals_1.content: So what are marginal gains and why do you need to be obsessively focused on honing in on marginal gains while you go through this course? Well let me tell you a story about a British cycling team and I know you're thinking what does a British cycling team have to do with studying but it's a really good story to prove the point so be patient with me. So the story goes like this, in 2013 someone called Dave Brailsford was appointed as the performance director for the British professional cycling team. Now something you have to know about the British cycling team at this time was that it had had about a hundred years of being just average. So since 1908 they won only a single gold medal at the Olympic Games and the Tour de France they did even worse. So in 110 years no British cyclist had ever won their event which is honestly pretty poor performance. In fact they actually got so bad that other bike manufacturers would refuse to sell their equipment to the British team because they thought it would damage their brand image so much if they saw British people using their equipment. That's how mediocre the British cycling team was. So what are Dave Brailsford do differently? Well what he did is probably not what you expect. You may expect that he did this huge revamp of their training regime and got them better equipment and got them better nutrition and all of that stuff but actually this is what he did. They redesigned the bike seats to make them more comfortable. They rubbed alcohol on the tires so they'd have better grip. They got the riders to wear electrically heated shorts to maintain ideal muscle temperature while riding and they used biofeedback sensors to monitor how each athlete will respond to particular workouts. They tested with different fabrics so that they could optimize their aerodynamics when they're riding and they even looked at the environment which bikes were stored. For example they painted the inside of their vans white because it's easier to spot specs of dust which would increase the maintenance cost and degrade the performance of their bikes. They hired massage therapists and massage gels to increase muscle recovery during training. They even hired a surgeon to teach the riders how to wash their hands properly to reduce the chances of the riders catching a cold. So these are all changes that would seem absurd and trivial at the time. How much difference could it make to spot specs of dust in the van to a team that has been losing for over a hundred years. So these were the results. Five years after Braille's foot started doing all these marginal gains, the British cycling team completely dominated the 2008 Olympic Games in Beijing. They won 60% of the gold medals available. Four years later in the Olympic Games in London, they set 9 Olympic records and 7 world records. I mean results speak, so this is a very famous story when it comes to marginal gains. The idea with marginal gains is that when you stack and aggregate 1% improvement over a period of time due to basically compound interest, the results are much greater than what you would expect and this is so, so true about studying. You could study like a pathetic rock, however, if you increase the efficiency at which you're able to study by just 1% per day, by the end of the year you will be vastly more efficient at studying compared to the beginning, vastly more efficient. In fact, if you do the maths on this, you would be around 37 times more efficient just from having a 1% improvement every single day. Now, I'm not saying that it's possible to get 37 times more efficient, but what I am saying is that even 1% per day is extreme. So a lot of these students that we have coming into our course that don't do well, there is a clear pattern. We call these students and we ask them why they weren't finding it as effective. We ask them for feedback. We really dive into this to figure out why are there some students who use a system that we know works, we have proven and we've got a track record that we know works, it makes it scientifically, there's research around it, we have consistent results, yet for some students they go into this course and it does not work and this could be you, which is why you need to pay attention to this and that could impact on your exam results. This could impact on your test results. When we ask them this, there's a strong pattern that comes up and the pattern is that the students are number one, demotivated because they don't feel that they are improving in a measurable way, they don't feel that they are really achieving leaps and bounds of success and those tiny 1% gains that they're stacking up, they're going unnoticed and because they're going unnoticed, they get complacent, they're not as strict about it, they don't use them as religiously in their study, they get lazy, they don't practice as much, a lot of these things start affecting their experience of the course and the techniques they're using because they don't realize that it's the cumulation of these 1% gains that actually makes an enormous difference. fundamentals_1.content: So it's time now to deepen our understanding of the concept of marginal gains. So we understand what marginal gains are and we understand why it's important. It doesn't really matter. It's all the same principle. So obviously one of the characteristic principles of marginal gains is that the gains need to compound and by compounding what I'm talking about obviously is the idea that it's not just a straight line going up because if you were only adding 1% per day for example then after 365 days you would only have 365% of gain which is not bad but it's a lot less than what we could be doing if we had compounding gains. And so the most common example obviously of a compound gain is the idea of compound interest. So if you're not familiar the idea of compound interest goes like this is that if you had for example $100 in the bank and that was earning you interest of 1% and let's just say that it's calculated at the end of every single month. So one month later, one month we would have 1% of $100 which would be $100 plus $1. That's 1% and so therefore we would have $101. Now the next month we would have an additional 1% on top of that but 1% of $101 is obviously greater than 1% of $100 and therefore it wouldn't be $100 and $1 plus $1 it would be $101 plus 1% of $101 which brings us to $1.01 in addition which is $102.01. And if we were to continue this trend then what we would see the next month is that would be at $103.03.01 and so on and so forth. So you can see that the gap is increasing. Initially the difference between the first month and the second month, this is in red, this difference is only $1 and then the next month this difference here is $1.01 and then the difference between this month and this month is greater than that still. !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!! Now the only reason that compounding works and the only reason that aggregation of marginal gains creates this compounding effect is because when we make the gain, it changes the state. So it's changed the state of our balance. It went from $100 to now $101. Which means that when we add 1% more onto this, then it changes the state once again to $102. And 1 cent. 30_days_plan.content: So what we're gonna do now is start creating our perfect plan. Now, planning is obviously very important, but what we found is that a lot of people, no matter what age and stage they're at, don't plan very effectively. You may have heard of smart goal setting, specific, measurable, attainable, or achievable. realistic, measurable, time-bound and these are good basic guidelines to follow if you're completely new with goal setting. But I've often found in my personal and professional experience working with a variety of people that this isn't really enough to help someone lock in a high propensity plan. And when I say high propensity plan, what I'm talking about here is a plan that is optimized for success, that has the highest chance of success. A lot of people, instead of creating a plan or instead of doing proper goal setting, they actually just do intention setting. And they are two completely different things. One of them is a lot less effective than the other. Intention setting would involve simply saying, "I want to achieve this thing and let's just try to do it." Most people don't have goals, they have intentions. And intentions make you feel good because they're new or maybe novel or you feel like you're really committed and you can make a poster out of it and stick it on your wall to remind yourself. It can help with motivation, things like that. However, It doesn't actually mean that you're going to necessarily achieve your goal. You can want something as much as you want, but it doesn't actually mean that you are going to get the outcome that you wanted. A lot of students that we talk to when we ask them what their goal is, they may have a goal of achieving the top 1% grade in their cohort. When we ask them, well, what's your plan of achieving that? It's often just, I'm going to study really hard and then I'm going to try my best. And then if we asked, well, what are you going to actually do when you are trying your best? What will you be trying your best with? It's often met with a kind of a blank face and they've never really thought about this before. The concept of actually Thinking about the actual tasks, actionables, things that will turn that intention into something in reality is often not thought through because it's much more difficult and much more complex to think through. It can feel overwhelming at first. Even for professionals, and you would think that someone that is much older and more mature would be better at this, but actually it's pretty much the same thing. Someone may say that they're wanting to do really well in a job interview or get into a certain particular profession or be promoted or whatever it is that they want to achieve. And it's mostly just intentions. There are some very, very loose action plans in place, but they haven't really been evaluated to see is that the best course of action compared to the alternatives, is it actually what's gonna produce the right result? And so the way that we go through our goal setting is very strategic and systematic. You can not only go through this now to see how you can make the most out of the opportunity that you've got now with this course and the program, but you can also come back to this in the future. Anytime that you have a goal that you want to achieve, that you feel is complicated or maybe overwhelming and you're not sure exactly how you want to get there but it's important enough that you want to set aside some time to make sure that you can really hit that goal, you can come back, go through this again, make a new plan and use this as a template for you to work off of in the future. So, without any further ado, let's get into the first few stages.
