30_days_plan.name: Goal setting.txt
30_days_plan.content: So the first thing we're going to start with is a medium term goal. Now we start with a medium term goal that is within the next six to 12 months because setting goals that are too short term means that you may not actually be able to do that much to change the actual outcome. For example, If you have an exam coming up in a month from now or you've got an important job interview in a few weeks, there may not be that much you can do between now and then to actually achieve that goal. And that may be disheartening for you if that was one of the most important things for you to achieve. And you can certainly try your best to achieve whatever you can in that time. However, you have to understand that also if you're not able to reach the bar in that short period of time because You missed out on doing months of prep work before that. Well, if you don't have the skills or the experiences to hit a certain goal, you're not likely to be able to develop those skills and that experience in a short period of time. So the best thing, to do may actually be to say, well, I may not do well in this immediate goal. Let's try to do well in the next goal instead and not make the same mistakes. One of the saddest things that I see is a student going through, especially university, they've come from high school, they did pretty well in high school, they went into university very confident, they went into a heavily academic program and they just got hit in the face with a shovel with the amount of content that they were thrown at. and they were completely overwhelmed and they didn't do well in their first semester test or exam results. And then they don't actually do anything to overcompensate for that. They don't try to fix the underlying issue. Everything is just a band-aid solution. So after the first semester, they think, "Okay, I'm just gonna double down. I'm gonna try even harder, work even more." And the second semester, they do maybe a little bit better or sometimes even worse. And so going into this, if you did poorly in the first semester, we have to think, well, what was the reason I did poorly? What was the reason I did poorly? Especially if you're putting in a decent amount of effort, why did that effort not translate into the result? And what can I do moving on beyond that to make sure I don't make the same mistake again? A real critical dissection and a reflection is necessary. And in some cases, it may not be that you can overcome that in the second semester. It may be something that you have to think, well, let's try to overcome that by next year. So at the beginning of next year, I'm not making the same mistakes that I made This year, underestimating the content, feeling overconfident, because what I see often is that people will go through, for example, their first year of university and they'll go into their second year feeling they learned so much from that first year. They made heaps of mistakes. They didn't do very well. But second year onwards, that's going to be where they turn things around and they realize that second year is actually harder than the first year. And they run into the exact same problems that they ran into in first year because now the jump is greater and they are still relatively underprepared. And this can happen if you're looking only at short-term goals. For example, if you did poorly in that first semester exam, and now what your goal is to do better in that second semester's first test that comes up in the next five to six weeks, well actually, you may need to learn a whole new system of studying. And for many of you, this will legitimately be the case. you may need to learn a whole new system of studying to be able to achieve that goal. And learning that system of studying might mean that for the first two or three weeks, you're not going to be making those huge, massive gains in efficiency because you need to lay the groundwork to build more effective techniques on top of later. So in order for you to succeed six to nine months from now, you actually need to sacrifice a little bit in that immediate short term. Most of the students that I talk to are very unwilling to make that decision. They're not willing to give up anything in that short term, even if they know it's unlikely to help fix things in the long term. And as a result, they're always living short term to short term to short term, just trying to keep up, just trying to meet the immediate next challenge. And always when the challenge after that comes along, they're always just the same level of unprepared 'cause they're only just meeting the minimum requirement or sometimes even unfortunately failing to meet that minimum requirement for each challenge. Most people do not go through this pathway and then do well. And what happens to them? They simply lower their standards, they do something else, they choose different pathways, whatever it is. And this is why uncommon results are uncommon. Not because people are not willing to work hard, but because it's about where you're working hard, the direction you're working hard in, and what that goal is and the time scale. how much you're willing to put in to develop a strong foundation that you can build on year after year after year so that every year it gets easier and more consistent and predictable to achieve your goals, not the other way around. That's why we start with a medium term goal, six to 12 months, it gives us lots of time to prepare, evaluate, reflect, experiment, and really build up something that's solid and dependable, not just for six to 12 months from now, but for life going on after that. Why don't we do long-term goals that are even further than 12 months? Because it's unpredictable. Especially going through this program, you're gonna learn lots of new skills and your perspective on things is going to change. It's hard to predict how you're going to think about things, how the way you tackle different goals and the types of challenges you perceive are going to be six to 12 months from now. You can spend time to create a plan for three years later. However, there's an almost guaranteed chance that that plan is going to change as you learn more information about what it is you're about to go into. If you think about it, it will be really weird for you to set a plan and then two, three years later, literally every single thing happened exactly as you thought, because that would have meant that you had all the information for every experience for the next three years from the onset. And that is just not likely. So long term, it's better to have general directions, a general vision, just a path that you're kind of traveling in the general direction of. But in terms of planning, really detailed long term planning is often not a great investment of time because it just changes so much and it's not going to be reliable. When it comes to creating solid plans, we wanna start at that medium term and then work our way backwards to the short term, which is what we're doing here. So set that medium term goal. You can pick a couple that you think are important enough for you. Keep them relatively simple. It could be as simple as saying, I wanna get a certain grade at the end of the year. It could be, I wanna get this particular job or position at this company that I really wanna work at. It could be something a little bit more, holistic and you want to be really fluent at another language or pick up a new skill and be really fluent at it, pick some kind of goal and just try to make it relatively specific and relatively measurable. Specific meaning that it's not something very abstract. So for example, setting a goal like "I want to be a better learner" is very abstract. We don't really know what it means to be a better learner. We don't know exactly what that is actually going to entail. When we try to break that down as the plans to reach it, it's a little bit confusing what direction we need to take. It's also not very measurable because how will we know when we reach the threshold of "better"? Getting a grade is very measurable. Getting a promotion, getting a position, these are very measurable. If it's just for your own learning, you can set your own metric. So it could be, I would like to be able to hold a basic conversation where I'm using another language 80% of the time when I'm talking with my family, friends. That could be something and that is relatively measurable. You don't have to get super, super nitty gritty on this. Some people are very obsessed about creating goals that are like extremely specific and extremely measurable. I don't feel that there is the equivalent amount of value from going that extensively. I think as long as it's specific enough that you can really visualize it and you know how to break it down and it's measurable enough that you know if you've actually succeeded or not, that is enough. So pick that goal, write that down in your plan, and then we're going to move down into the performance goals.

Especially if you're putting in a decent amount of effort, why did that effort not translate into the result? And what can I do moving on beyond that to make sure I don't make the same mistake again? A real critical dissection and a reflection is necessary. And in some cases, it may not be that you can overcome that in the second semester. It may be something that you have to think, well, let's try to overcome that by next year. So at the beginning of next year, I'm not making the same mistakes that I made This year, underestimating the content, feeling overconfident, because what I see often is that people will go through, for example, their first year of university and they'll go into their second year feeling they learned so much from that first year. They made heaps of mistakes. They didn't do very well. But second year onwards, that's going to be where they turn things around and they realize that second year is actually harder than the first year. And they run into the exact same problems that they ran into in first year because now the jump is greater and they are still relatively underprepared. And this can happen if you're looking only at short-term goals. For example, if you did poorly in that first semester exam, and now what your goal is to do better in that second semester's first test that comes up in the next five to six weeks, well actually, you may need to learn a whole new system of studying. And for many of you, this will legitimately be the case. you may need to learn a whole new system of studying to be able to achieve that goal. And learning that system of studying might mean that for the first two or three weeks, you're not going to be making those huge, massive gains in efficiency because you need to lay the groundwork to build more effective

be the case. you may need to learn a whole new system of studying to be able to achieve that goal. And learning that system of studying might mean that for the first two or three weeks, you're not going to be making those huge, massive gains in efficiency because you need to lay the groundwork to build more effective techniques on top of later. So in order for you to succeed six to nine months from now, you actually need to sacrifice a little bit in that immediate short term. Most of the students that I talk to are very unwilling to make that decision. They're not willing to give up anything in that short term, even if they know it's unlikely to help fix things in the long term. And as a result, they're always living short term to short term to short term, just trying to keep up, just trying to meet the immediate next challenge. And always when the challenge after that comes along, they're always just the same level of unprepared 'cause they're only just meeting the minimum requirement or sometimes even unfortunately failing to meet that minimum requirement for each challenge. Most people do not go through this pathway and then do well. And what happens to them? They simply lower their standards, they do something else, they choose different pathways, whatever it is. And this is why uncommon results are uncommon. Not because people are not willing to work hard, but because it's about where you're working hard, the direction you're working hard in, and what that goal is and the time scale. how much you're willing to put in to develop a strong foundation that you can build on year after year after year so that every year it gets easier and more consistent and predictable to achieve your goals, not the other way around. That's why we start with a medium term goal, six to 12 months, it gives us lots of time to prepare, evaluate, reflect, experiment, and really build up something that's solid and dependable, not just for six to 12 months from now, but for life going on after that. Why don't we do long-term goals that are even further than 12 months? Because it's unpredictable. Especially going through this program, you're gonna learn lots of new skills and your perspective on things is going to change. It's

up something that's solid and dependable, not just for six to 12 months from now, but for life going on after that. Why don't we do long-term goals that are even further than 12 months? Because it's unpredictable. Especially going through this program, you're gonna learn lots of new skills and your perspective on things is going to change. It's hard to predict how you're going to think about things, how the way you tackle different goals and the types of challenges you perceive are going to be six to 12 months from now. You can spend time to create a plan for three years later. However, there's an almost guaranteed chance that that plan is going to change as you learn more information about what it is you're about to go into. If you think about it, it will be really weird for you to set a plan and then two, three years later, literally every single thing happened exactly as you thought, because that would have meant that you had all the information for every experience for the next three years from the onset. And that is just not likely. So long term, it's better to have general directions, a general vision, just a path that you're kind of traveling in the general direction of. But in terms of planning, really detailed long term planning is often not a great investment of time because it just changes so much and it's not going to be reliable. When it comes to creating solid plans, we wanna start at that medium term and then work our way backwards to the short term, which is what we're doing here. So set that medium term goal. You can pick a couple that you think are important enough for you. Keep them relatively simple. It could be as simple as saying, I wanna get a certain grade at the end of the year. It could be, I wanna get this particular job or position at this company that I really wanna work at. It could be something a little bit more, holistic and you want to be really fluent at another language or pick up a new skill and be really fluent at it, pick some kind of goal and just try to make it relatively specific and relatively measurable. Specific meaning that it's not something very abstract. So for example, setting a

It could be something a little bit more, holistic and you want to be really fluent at another language or pick up a new skill and be really fluent at it, pick some kind of goal and just try to make it relatively specific and relatively measurable. Specific meaning that it's not something very abstract. So for example, setting a goal like "I want to be a better learner" is very abstract. We don't really know what it means to be a better learner. We don't know exactly what that is actually going to entail. When we try to break that down as the plans to reach it, it's a little bit confusing what direction we need to take. It's also not very measurable because how will we know when we reach the threshold of "better"? Getting a grade is very measurable. Getting a promotion, getting a position, these are very measurable. If it's just for your own learning, you can set your own metric. So it could be, I would like to be able to hold a basic conversation where I'm using another language 80% of the time when I'm talking with my family, friends. That could be something and that is relatively measurable. You don't have to get super, super nitty gritty on this. Some people are very obsessed about creating goals that are like extremely specific and extremely measurable. I don't feel that there is the equivalent amount of value from going that extensively. I think as long as it's specific enough that you can really visualize it and you know how to break it down and it's measurable enough that you know if you've actually succeeded or not, that is enough. So pick that goal, write that down in your plan, and then we're going to move down into the performance goals.

30_days_plan.name: Performance goals.txt
30_days_plan.content: So performance goals are different from other types of goals because they're not outcomes. Performance goals are goals that indicate that you are on the way to achieving your outcome goal. So for example, if you're in a race, like a driving car race, and your goal is to finish a lap in one minute, and you know that in this curve that's coming up right here, there might be a particular corner, and you know that 100 meters before that corner, you need to be going at 80 kilometers an hour. And by the time you exit that corner 100 meters down, the time needs to be sitting at exactly 43 seconds. And if you're over that, you're probably not going to reach that goal. So these performance goals are essentially indicators for you that tell you that you are on the right track. And performance goals are very important for two reasons. Number one, because it tells you if you're on the right track, and it gives you early feedback, and gives you opportunities to correct yourself at an early stage. People that don't have that type of feedback, maybe going down the wrong path, investing effort and time doing something, and then only right before the outcome, but right before the exam, whatever it is, they then get a little bit of feedback, and they find, holy crap, I am probably not going to achieve this. I've been on the wrong track. And that is obviously an unideal situation. So to avoid that, we give ourselves lots of early feedback opportunities to self-correct and find opportunities for when we are making mistakes. If we're making mistakes, we definitely want to know early on. We don't want to bury our head in the sand and just be oblivious to it until we get the outcome. That is if the outcome really does matter to you. The other reason that we set performance goals is because performance goals are often focused on the process and the attributes required to achieve the goal. And that is a lot more controllable, a lot more consistent, and it's much safer. So for example, if we use learning and we say that we want to achieve a 95% for this end of semester exam or recertification exam or a course that you're taking, whatever the goal is here. If we think about that outcome, how can we create performance goals along the way to know that we're actually hitting all of those metrics? See, this is where it can be very misleading because people are often very used to setting goals on relatively arbitrary and superficial metrics. So for example, I'm going to be studying five hours a day or I will have finished covering this much material by this point. But does that actually mean you are necessarily on the right track to achieving the goal? So if you're spending five hours of studying, but you're using a very ineffective technique, you may not actually get the knowledge mastery and the ability to retrieve that knowledge to a very high level. In fact, after five hours, maybe you've only achieved the real level of knowledge mastery that someone who studied two hours with a more effective technique was able to reach. In this case, hitting those number of hours per day of study is essentially completely meaningless. And in my practice, I know that the range is considerable. Students that use an ineffective technique can easily spend 10, 15, 20 hours a day on studying and not achieve the same outcomes as someone using a much more effective technique that is able to get the same result or even better with two or three hours of studying per day. So I know because not only have I worked with students like this, but I was one of those people, as you know now, I studied many, many, many, many hours a day. And now I'm able to achieve pretty much better results with a tiny fraction of the amount of time that I used to spend. And with the amount of sleep deprivation that I put myself through over the years, I'm sure I didn't get smarter. If anything, I got dumber during that time. So the trick here is to make sure that we are measuring based on meaningful metrics that truly correlate with the success outcome. We're not just saying the amount of stuff that we've covered. We're not saying hours. We're not saying number of flashcards or pages of notes that we've written. These are all meaningless metrics because they focus on the studying process rather than the learning process. And lots of studying doesn't necessarily mean lots of learning because if there's not much going on in your brain at a cognitive point of view, maybe there's nothing in your brain from a knowledge point of view. So when we set performance goals, a good way that we can track this down is to think about the attributes. Imagine a person that would be able to achieve your goal as fully and as safely as possible. It doesn't have to be a real person. It can be a completely imaginary or hypothetical person that you think about and think, this person would definitely be able to achieve this goal. If this person cannot achieve this goal, no one can achieve this goal. You want to find the avatar of goal success. I want you to think about what attributes this person may have. And I'll give you a few ones that are very useful for you to think about because these are often ones that people don't think about very much. However, it has a very strong correlation to actual outcomes. So time management skills, focus and procrastination, learning efficiency and what that's talking about really is how much learning is someone able to achieve in a certain period of time, learning consistency. So this is talking about how consistently is someone engaging in their learning activities, right? So it's different from efficiency. So learning consistency, their stress management abilities. One thing I often say is that stress cuts at the knees. It doesn't matter how tall you are, it cuts at the knees. So if you have amazing time management and you have great study efficiency and every other box is ticked, if you don't have good stress management or if you don't have resilience and you're not able to push through discomfort or you, for example, going through this program, we teach you techniques and every time you do it, you're like, oh, it doesn't feel comfortable. I'm just going to go back to what I'm used to. It feels safer because I'm worried about screwing up the study session. That is directly going to detriment your ability to actually learn new skills. For that person, it may take them easily, actually 10, 20, 50, 100 times longer to learn something compared to someone else that has more resilience and is able to push through that discomfort. And that is indeed one of the things that we see. Students that really have that strong response against fear and like to stay in their comfort zone can sometimes really never develop the appropriate skills to achieve their goal. So we need to think about stress management. We need to think about how that affects every other domain, including our ability to get good quality sleep and stay mentally optimum and efficient. We might even be thinking about the support network that we have. We know that these are things that are very important for building mental resilience. So if you take this list and you think about this, if you had someone with great time management, great resilience, high level of learning efficiency, very consistent and diligent and very, very focused, would you expect that that person would have a great chance at achieving that goal? Probably, yes. In fact, it may even be to the point where it will be surprising for them not to achieve that goal. Now, depending on your goal, you might want to add other attributes in there, and that's completely up to you. So for example, maybe your goal requires a lot of networking and interpersonal communication skills. So that could be a separate attribute that you put in there. Communication skills, sociability, networking skills, size of the network itself. These are things that you may want to add on relative to your goal. But the key is to look at the goal and think, what are the attributes that are required for someone to ideally achieve this? And then what we do is we think about those attributes and we think about ourselves in relation to those attributes. How do we fare when it comes to those things? So this is a completely different perspective on goal setting that people normally have. Normally, people will be going into goal setting thinking, I want to achieve a certain score in an exam. I need to do this amount of studying by this day, this day, this day. However, the learning that you get, the result that you get, your ability to actually reach any of those performance goals is completely dependent on your attributes. If you have terrible time management, your learning efficiency is poor and you're not consistent, and you always get distracted, then it doesn't matter what goals you set yourself along the way. You're just not likely to achieve it because you simply don't have the attributes required to hit the goals. Whereas if you do have the attributes, you will hit those goals probably without even really trying to. You will hit them as just a natural side effect of you engaging in your process of preparation. Trace back those perfect attributes that you think that the imaginary person that you've thought of would have, and then compare that to your current set of attributes and see what is the difference there. Maybe you're pretty good in some areas, but you're deficient in others. And a lot of times, especially for younger students, those that are in high school and early university, they're often deficient in multiple areas of these goals. But it's not necessarily a bad thing. It just means that there's many areas that we can improve on, which means that the potential is that much higher. So we trace back to those goals and we measure ourselves on where we are. And then we think 30 days from now, how can I make it so that some of these attributes are in a better position? How can I develop these attributes 30 days from now? And that becomes a short-term performance goal. We are able to see how we're progressing based on our progress through these attributes. Now, if you've got seven or eight different attributes that you need to work on, don't work on seven or eight all at once, because that's overwhelming, extremely overwhelming. You're not likely to be able to hit all of those goals. And you're probably going to fail at all of them if you give yourself too much on your plate. Instead, pick maybe the two or three at most that you think are the most important for you right now. If you think all of them are very important for you, just pick two or three. It doesn't even matter. The idea is that you can't do more than that because it just increases your risk of failure. So even if there's heaps to do, you just have to put it off until later. Right now, focus on really nailing and making some good progress on two or three very important attributes for you to work on. And then think, how will I know whether I've progressed to that 30-day point? So let's take time management, for example. Let's say right now you're thinking, well, I'm able to schedule my time three times a week, and I'm actually following that schedule to about 20% accuracy in real practice. And I'm actually getting through only, let's say, a third of my task list every single day. 30 days from now, we're probably not going to expect that we are suddenly now the manifested demigod of time management. That's probably a little unlikely, but we can be a little bit better. So we can be scheduling our time maybe four or five days a week, and we can be following that schedule accurately, maybe 60% of the time. So it's not perfect, but it's definitely a strong improvement from where we were before. And so by doing this, we're really stacking those marginal gains in a strategic way that we know is going to get us closer and closer to those goals. Because again, remember, when we get to the point where our attributes are really as good and as consistent as they need to be to achieve the goal, the outcome itself is simply a symptom of the right underlying process. The outcome is simply a symptom. You don't control the outcome directly. You control the process through which you engage in to try to achieve the outcome. And you cross your fingers for everything else and hope that luck doesn't ruin all of that. And sometimes it does, and that's just life, but we can't control that. All we can control is our process and making sure that we tick all of the boxes that we know we have to tick to give ourselves the best chance. Working down to the 14-day goal is very straightforward. We simply do the same thing, but instead of 30 days from now, we think 14 days from now. And at 14 days from now, it's not going to be impressive at all. The golden rule here is to make a goal where in the worst case scenario, you will still achieve it. And in the best case scenario, you far exceed it. It's important that we develop momentum, we develop reflective capacity, we build the system for improvement so that two weeks from now, we're making progress. And that progress may not be enormous, but we have stacked together a few bits of marginal gains here and there. And so we are well on our way. So 30 days from now, when we look back, we've actually made a decent step towards our progress. Two, three months from now, we're definitely gunning down that path and we're in a much better position. People that don't tend to focus on breaking down their goal in this way, they can get very absorbed in just kind of doing the daily grind. They can easily spend days, weeks, months just kind of doing the same thing, wondering why the results aren't as consistent, getting anxious and frustrated because they don't feel like they're making progress because they're not really seeing the progress they're making because they're not seeing those marginal gains. I was choked on how frustrating it felt for me, just even explaining that. And then eventually, they'll get to the goal and have the outcome and their fingers are more than crossed. Their fingers, their legs, their toes, everything is crossed because at that point, it is just relying on luck because they did not commit and engage with the process. And the unfortunate thing is after having done this for so many years, I know that even after saying this, there are some of you that will be thinking, I'm different. This doesn't apply to me. I can just do things my own way. I'm going to be the one that doesn't have to follow this advice. However, what we consistently see is that people that think, I'm different. It doesn't apply to me. They're actually all the same. They run into the same mistakes. They have the same habits of thinking. They rush in the same ways. They ignore the same types of advice and they all go on to regret it. Well, except for the very few that are lucky.

So it's not perfect, but it's definitely a strong improvement from where we were before. And so by doing this, we're really stacking those marginal gains in a strategic way that we know is going to get us closer and closer to those goals. Because again, remember, when we get to the point where our attributes are really as good and as consistent as they need to be to achieve the goal, the outcome itself is simply a symptom of the right underlying process. The outcome is simply a symptom. You don't control the outcome directly. You control the process through which you engage in to try to achieve the outcome. And you cross your fingers for everything else and hope that luck doesn't ruin all of that. And sometimes it does, and that's just life, but we can't control that. All we can control is our process and making sure that we tick all of the boxes that we know we have to tick to give ourselves the best chance. Working down to the 14-day goal is very straightforward. We simply do the same thing, but instead of 30 days from now, we think 14 days from now. And at 14 days from now, it's not going to be impressive at all. The golden rule here is to make a goal where in the worst case scenario, you will still achieve it. And in the best case scenario, you far exceed it. It's important that we develop momentum, we develop reflective capacity, we build the system for improvement so that two weeks from now, we're making progress. And that progress may not be enormous, but we have stacked together a few bits of marginal gains here and there. And so we are well on our way. So 30 days from now, when we look back, we've actually made a decent step towards our progress. Two, three months from now, we're definitely gunning down that path and we're in a much better position. People that don't tend to focus on breaking

30_days_plan.name: Protecting time.txt
30_days_plan.content: Now that we've got our goal in place, let's think about the barriers that we'll have to achieving our goal. We'll always have barriers for a challenging goal because if we didn't have barriers, it wouldn't be challenging. So we need to anticipate those barriers so that we can actually do something about them. This seems a very logical step to do in goal setting, but it is frankly very rare for me to see someone that proactively tries to anticipate and overcome barriers before it holds them back. So when we're thinking about the types of barriers that we have, it's important to realize that there is a difference between action and plan versus intention, like I said before. So I want you to think about this analogy. Imagine that you have a baby. Okay, and then let's assume maybe you have a baby. If you don't have a baby, just imagine you have a baby. And let's assume here that you love this baby. It's your baby. And you want to keep this baby safe. Let's say now that you're at home and you're home alone with the baby and you're thinking, oh man, I need to go out and buy some groceries. It's going to be, you know, 40 minutes to go out, get the groceries and come back. hmm, I need to make sure that the baby is safe while I'm gone out of the house for the next 40 minutes to an hour. So in order to make sure that the baby doesn't choke on something or touch something that's dangerous, we may hire a babysitter, we may put the baby in like a cot or something, set the baby alarm, let a neighbor know, or we may just decide there's no really good way to make sure that the baby is protected here. I'm probably just not going to go out to get the groceries, I'll just UberEat something instead. We wouldn't say, hmm, let's just put the baby in the middle of the room, and I'm just going to really hope that the baby is safe. Alright, and I'm just going to leave for the next hour, and I just hope that nothing happens. You know, I didn't really do anything about this, but I hope. I'm sure my willpower and intention alone will form some kind of... ...epherial barrier around this baby to make sure that it doesn't touch anything. That's just kind of not how the world works. And that's not how the world will work when it comes to your plans either. And the way that we think about this in the context of a baby is very obvious. But time is one of the most important things that we have, and we do very little to protect our time. So let's say now that you've scheduled something, you've said, hey, one of the things that I really need to engage in is mindfulness meditation. I'm going to do 10 minutes every single morning and then 10 minutes in the evening. Great, great plan. And as soon as you make that plan, you may feel good about yourself and you may feel like you're turning your life around or whatever great feelings that you have. And that's good. It's good to feel good. However, it doesn't actually mean anything unless you're executing on that schedule and that plan. It's another reason why people will often send me a screenshot of their calendar or whatever it is and say, hey, Justin, what do you think about my schedule? It means nothing what is planned. The only thing that matters is the lived, real experience of trying to execute on that plan. So let's think about protecting our time like we would protect a baby and do actual things to make sure that that time is protected. The first thing is that if the spot that we are scheduling something, if that block of time is a high risk zone, There just may not be anything that you can do to realistically protect it. So for example, if you are in a battlefield and there's bullets whizzing past you everywhere, it's just not a good idea to leave a baby pretty much anywhere in that environment because it's just high risk in general. Short of building like a nuclear bunker and putting the baby inside whatever it is, there's not really much that you can do. A common example of this would be people scheduling things early in the morning before work or before school or whatever, but they're not a morning person to begin with. They don't wake up early to begin with. And actually, this is the thing that's meant to motivate them to get up. If you previously have not really demonstrated that you're able to consistently get up and do things in the morning, there's no reason to believe that actually scheduling something that you think is an important task at the spot where you've never demonstrated the ability to execute on that is going to suddenly now make you do that. it's like needing to create a habit and so you pin it upon the entire premise that you'll be able to create an entire new habit just to create that it's like being a you know a serious smoker and saying okay well in order for me not to smoke i'm gonna make sure that every time i'm riding my bike any day that i ride my bike i'm not gonna smoke a cigarette except I don't ride a bike. So we're basically saying that in order for this to succeed, we need to develop an entire new fundamental habit on top of that. Don't do that. Start with making the habit of getting up early first. Once you're able to develop that consistently with resilience, then yes, you can start utilizing that time for other things. But don't put things in slots where you know it is high risk. I personally do not do things in the mornings, early in the morning, that I know are very important, that require my own self-volition, because I know that the early morning version of myself will require way too much willpower to get up to do something that doesn't feel imminently urgent. I know, I've tried it before, and I know that it's difficult for me. So I just don't. And therefore, I'm actually able to schedule it somewhere else and get it done. This is the difference between intention versus a real actionable plan that has a high propensity for success. Another common example is scheduling things in straight after work or straight when you get home from school or work or uni. A lot of people, especially if there's a long commute home, you're tired. You may be drowsy, you're drained when you get home for the first time at the end of the day. Scheduling something important straight after that may not be the best place because not that you don't have time, but because you don't have energy. So we want to think about this. We want to think, where are we going to schedule it? We want to make sure that we're scheduling it in a place that is not fundamentally high risk. If it is a high risk spot, don't schedule something important there. Schedule the meaningless things there. Check your emails at that time. Don't do something that you know is an important thing that's going to make a long term difference, like going through this course material, practicing something like mindfulness meditation. These are game changers in the long term or medium term. but they're not gonna be things that are urgent enough that it's gonna drive you, for example, out of bed and summon up your willpower from the abyss of your burnout to be able to tackle this. That's not a good plan, don't do that. So if that spot that you have decided to block out and schedule something in is not a safe spot to do it, do not schedule it there. Some safer spots may be, for example, immediately after dinner. Think about the times that are the least likely to be disturbed. Those are the times that you should allocate for the most important things in your life. That could be learning new skills. It could be spending time with family. But you want to work in that way where the most important things are the things that are the safest and the most protected. Very logical if you think about it. If you are putting something where there is risk involved, then we want to evaluate what are the possible risks that might make it so that my schedule deviates. Think about what those could be and then do things to protect against that. Put those safety rails in place. So for example, if getting home after work is a great time for you to do something, let's say 5.30 PM just before dinner, that's a great spot for you to fit in 10, 15 minutes for something that you think is relatively important. Great. You can use that. But you also know that the risk there is that I'm often very tired as soon as I come back home from work and I may not do this. Okay. That's fine. We can maybe still work with that as long as we do something to protect the time. So instead of just scheduling that block in, maybe for the five minutes before that, we schedule something else that will do something to mitigate the low energy. It could be a five minute walk. It could be do 30 press ups. It could be, I don't know, make your favorite smoothie, whatever it is. You do something that you know is easy to do, that's not going to require lots of energy and willpower, but it's going to help you be more energized at that time. If it's because you're worried about someone coming in to distract you, what can you do to protect that and stop people from distracting you? Is it a routine of putting your phone on do not disturb mode? Maybe even setting that up as an automation. Is it about letting other people know that during this time you need five minutes to focus on things and not to disrupt you? Is it about setting up a space in your house where you can dedicate an environment where you can focus and you use that and condition yourself as a focus area? What are the things that you can do to protect and minimize the risk of that time being derailed and hijacked by something that you did not intend? So that's number one, the most important thing that we can do when we're fundamentally first just scheduling and blocking something out in our day. And then we document this in our plan in terms of our specific actions that we're going to take to protect our time. And again, do not add intentions. Simply saying, I'm going to try my best to do it at that time means nothing. Intentions mean nothing if it's not backed up by the actual actions in the real world that make it occur. And it can be very frustrating if you're full of intentions, but the problem is in your execution because now you're walking around full of intentions, trying to do the schedule. At the end of every day, you're disappointed and frustrated that you weren't able to follow the schedule. It's just not a combination that goes well. There's a reason you're going to have issues with execution. Execution issues are different from intention issues.

30_days_plan.name: Limiting habits and scripted actions.txt
30_days_plan.content: Once you've figured out what your tasks are to protect your time, then we move on to identifying limiting habits. So some of you may remember that several years ago, there was a story about a man, I think it was in Japan, who found that food was missing from their kitchen every now and again. And this had been going on for an extended period of time. I believe it was around a year or so. And so this man put a security camera in their kitchen. And on reviewing the footage, they found that there was actually a homeless person living inside some sort of cupboard or attic space in their house. And they were coming down at night to take the food, which is number one, horrifying, but number two, not something that you would normally think of. You wouldn't normally think that when food goes missing from your home, that therefore there is a homeless person that's living somewhere in your home for the last year that has been eating it. It's not a natural thought to have. So what we wanna do when it comes to limiting habits is we wanna identify what are the things that are escaping our radar that may be coming out without us realizing it to steal the food, to sabotage our progress. And we wanna put a security camera on it right now to identify what those limiting habits might be. Because if the security camera wasn't up, maybe this man would have thought that Well, it's just one of those things that happens. This food just goes missing from my kitchen. Maybe if this person just grew up in an interesting way in society, they just would come to develop the belief that Food just goes missing sometimes. It's just one of those things that happens, you know? Like, they have their friends over and their friend says, hey, can I, you know, grab a drink from your fridge? And, oh, yeah, go get it. It's in the top shelf. And they open up the fridge and say, hey, your fridge is empty. And it's like, oh, just one of those things that happens, you know? Like, my fridge just makes food disappear every now and again, you know? You know how it is. It's one of those things. And then the friend would look at him and say, no, I don't know what you mean at all. But actually, we have that same belief when it comes to some things, right? For example, socks. We just have this accepted belief that socks just go missing sometimes. It's just one of those things that happen. And we know that it's not just dematerializing into the ether. We know that it's probably behind the washing machine or the dog chewed it up and buried it in the garden or something. We know that it's somewhere, but we just accept that it's one of those things. And likewise, we can actually form these beliefs about ourselves and our ability. start forming beliefs over time that I just can't do this. I'm just not the type of person that can exercise regularly. I'm just not good at studying. I just can't sit and concentrate. I'm not really good at reading from a book. I'm not so good at paying attention in class. I don't really do well when I'm sitting down in a library. We can form these limiting beliefs about what we can and can't do and what we're good at or not good at based purely on the fact that we're potentially using a system and a habit of techniques and approaches that are self-sabotaging and we may completely not realize that that's the case. So here are a few prompts to get you started. How do you tend to respond when you are in time pressure? When you're running out of time, how do you tend to respond to that? How do you tend to respond when you're stressed? How do you tend to respond when you feel tired? How do you tend to respond when you are in a bad mood? And how do you tend to respond when you're being pushed outside of your comfort zone? These are potentially limiting habits. They're subconscious autopilot processes that often go on without us realizing it. It's a very fast process. It's usually automatic. We don't know what's going on. We are completely usually unaware of it until we really focus and critically reflect on it. We want to think how we tend to respond to these. Limiting habits and identifying limiting habits is one of the best things that you can do for your medium and long-term success. probably the most single most common reason that people don't do well going through this program is because the techniques that we teach and the approaches that we teach are not brainless to do. They do spark discomfort. They do induce what we call cognitive load. It's something that makes us exert energy and brain power, and it can make us feel tired. The process of actually doing it, especially if you have years and a long history of very passive or linear learning strategies, it can be very involved and so engaged to the point where it feels uncomfortable. There can even be insecurity at the fact that you're using this technique that's so different from what you're already using and you may have that fear of failure kicking in again. At those times when we're maybe time pressured, stressed, we're feeling uncomfortable, we need to know how we tend to respond to that. What I've found over the years is that there are students, not just students, but people of all ages, when they encounter these situations, they can often revert back to something that makes it a little bit more comfortable for them. So for example, instead of using a particular technique that we teach, when it gets difficult and uncomfortable, they may actually start modifying that technique, deviating from exactly how it's meant to be done, and making it a little bit easier for themselves to avoid that discomfort. And many times, they're completely unaware of the fact that that deviation has bypassed the most effective part of the technique. So this is the reason, especially going through this program, we need to be very aware of the limiting habits. There are people that can be practicing and using the techniques again and again and again for months, and they get very frustrated and demotivated because they're not making progress. And when we sit down and really evaluate what they're doing and the approach that they're taking, they weren't actually practicing the technique. They went in with the intention of practicing the technique, but every time they sat down and practiced it, when they encountered that new cognitive element that they weren't used to, instead of pushing through, feeling it out, and then developing that into a new habit, they kind of just reverted back and modified to what they're more used to. And because they're not aware, they think every session they're going and trying to use this technique and they're consistently not getting the outcome. But actually, the reason is because the technique itself was modified. So think about those limiting habits, how we tend to respond to those things. You can think of other limiting habits that you might have as well. This could be things like anxiety or fixed mindset or, you know, an aversion response to fear or fear of discomfort or fear of failure. It could just be that when you're in a bad mood, you get more tired. or you just feel like you procrastinate more when you're not feeling the best or if you're tired, you tend to procrastinate more. These are all very common things and they're very normal. Every human being has limiting habits. We can't often do anything about them straight off the bat, but it's important to recognize them because then we can start making the next part, which is scripted actions. Scripted actions are automatic responses that we have pre-programmed that we can initiate when we notice that we have fallen into a limiting habit. so if you're in a hospital and someone is going into a cardiac arrest then there is a protocol in place doctors and the nurses and the intensive care specialists they come in and you know they'll assess the situation and they're very fast to act no one is thinking okay well cardiac arrest okay that means that the heart is not working correctly and that could be because of this or this so maybe the best drug to give in this situation is going to be this thing but oh, well, the side effect of that will be the... No one is going through that thought process. There is an algorithm that everyone is trained in and needs to demonstrate competency in to actually maintain a medical license. So if that situation were to arise where there is a life or limb-threatening situation, people can jump into action. There's no thinking time required. There's no delay. That's the importance of a scripted action. Because limiting habits are habits, they're automatic autopilot subconscious processes. They happen too quickly to prevent from happening. It's very difficult to catch a habit before it has already been initiated. 30_days_plan.name: Environmental optimization.txt
30_days_plan.content: Environmental optimizations means that we are changing our environment by recognizing that our environment is very strongly associated with the actions and the behaviors we're likely to take. Now, back in the old days, when it came to psychology research, we really thought that behaviors were just in and of themselves. And to change a behavior, we needed to just really focus on the behavior to change it. More modern research, and this is largely the popular belief now in the research space when it comes to behavioral analysis and behavioral psychology, is that our behavior is more so a product of our environment and our habits than it is about the behavior in itself. So I call this the cookie jar effect. I personally quite like cookies. And so for me, if there is a cookie jar on the kitchen table, I may have a cookie every day after dinner, maybe twice a day, maybe three times a day. Maybe I just finish the jar of cookies. If that cookie jar is not on the kitchen table, I may not even think about having a cookie for months, maybe even years. So this is the reason why a lot of companies out there are so insistent on making sure that their icon and their logo looks attractive, making sure it's on your home screen, on your desktop, making sure that there's advertising everywhere because they understand that what's important for you to make the decision to engage with their app or play their game or to buy their product you need to be trained, you need to be brainwashed into associating that thing, that logo, that trigger with a certain type of behavioral habit like doom scrolling on social media or whatever it is or binge eating. We are living in a world right now where we are filled and surrounded by stimuli with billion dollar companies investing money into researching how they can take and hack your attention. We are surrounded by stimuli that is far beyond the dopamine hit that our brain originally adapted to cope with when we were chilling out in caves and stuff. So it does require extended more effort. It's not a natural process to be able to avoid these distractions. If you're someone that gets easily distracted, it just simply means you're a normal human being living in this current modern society. To break free from that, you do have to fight against it and put in effort. But the difference is that the effort can't be put in at the point of the behavior itself. It's very difficult to change a behavior by simply trying to change the behavior. Instead, we need to think about what are the triggers for that behavior and what are the things in our environment that make that behavior either more easily or more frequently triggered or more easily or frequently accessible. So let's use an example like online games. If you play lots of online games, but you uninstall the game from your computer, you're a lot less likely to play it because now it takes so much effort to reinstall the game that you often can't be bothered. Now, I'm not saying you have to go to that extent, although if you are really struggling with procrastination and being productive because of the fact that you're just getting soaked up into this game, then maybe that is something that you want to consider if you think that's important enough. For example, uninstalling or deactivating social media accounts. These are things that you could also engage in. We've got a Discord channel, but maybe you get distracted from that. Maybe you want to mute certain channels at certain times of the day. So it's about setting up your environment to prevent distractions. A very simple thing like not having your phone right next to you when you study. Some people, it's enough just to put their phone down and put it on do not disturb. Some people, that's not enough. A lot of people, that's not enough. A common strategy that I recommend is to take your phone and put it underneath the pile of clothes inside your wardrobe. So the idea is that we want to make the behavior that we're trying to avoid, the bad behavior, so difficult to access that we just straight up cannot be bothered. We want to use our laziness as a weapon against ourselves. distractions and undesirable behaviors. It's very common for someone to message me saying, Justin, I've tried to avoid these distractions and blah, blah, blah, but it's not working. And I say, okay, well, tell me what you're doing. And a lot of the time, all they're doing is they're saying, oh, well, I put my phone on do not disturb. And then I got rid of the desktop logo and that's it. It's like, okay. Well, what else have you done? And if they haven't done anything more, the indication is that they're not taking this seriously. They're still relying too much on the willpower. You have to understand, and the research is getting increasingly clear about this, Putting in effort and willpower and trying to change a behavior by focusing on trying to change the behavior in itself is very, very inconsistent and difficult and usually not sustainable. As a doctor, when patients come in and they have a terrible diet and they're on all sorts of different types of narcotics or illegal drugs and substances, and they've got terrible lifestyle decisions and they're coming into hospital again and again, I know that even if we're able to get them to quit smoking and go into rehab and blah, blah, blah, all the other stuff, and we are able to change their lifestyle, As soon as we put them back into their living situation where they may be surrounded by friends or family or whatever that is enabling that behavior, it's just a matter of time. It's incredibly ineffective to try to change a behavior by focusing on the behavior in itself. You have to recognize that you cannot use environmental optimization as just a little helping hand for your main approach, which is still just to try to not do the behavior. Don't try to do that. Invest all of that willpower and energy into optimizing your environment to make sure that that behavior is difficult to access or you're removing the triggers. Get rid of the cookie jar. Figure out all the different ways that you're triggered. Have a sheet next to you. Every time you're triggered by something, note down what it was that triggered you and think about ways that you can overcome that. Don't look for excuses why you can't. Look for solutions to make it happen. Let me say that again. Don't look for excuses, look for solutions. A lot of the time people will say, yeah, well, I can't really get rid of it because of whatever reasons. And it's like, look, what's more important for you? Do you wanna achieve this goal or do you want to just get distracted and at the end of it, fail your goal and say, oh yeah, but I couldn't because of blah, blah, blah, blah. Figure out what's important for you and just commit to the decision and just do what needs to be done, right? Sometimes it's not fun and you know, that's just life. You just gotta suck it up and do it. So commit to the process of it, figure out where the environmental optimizations need to happen and optimize the environment as much as you possibly can. I don't think I've ever encountered a situation in my years of practice across thousands of students from literally every possible scenario that you can think of. I've worked with refugees literally living in countries with active war going on where their parents are domestically abusing them and they're studying at 3 a.m. in secret under their covers at night. And I've worked with people attending Ivy League universities, I've worked with competitive international level athletes. I don't think I've ever found someone that really committed to optimizing their environment and wasn't able to consistently really get a hold of their distractions and procrastination tendencies. It's just a matter of how far you're willing to go to make sure you can optimize that environment. Now, there is another side to this, is that you can actually optimize other people's environment for your benefit. So a common example that I see is with students, parents barging into the room and distracting them or siblings or something. And you can tell them, hey, don't distract me if my door is closed, but they just walk in anyway, and it can be very disruptive and annoying. So instead, what you can do is you can interrupt their habit, because their habit is to just walk into your room. So instead, what you can do is maybe put up a sign on your door that says, studying, please do not disturb. That may be the environmental cue that is enough to break their habit of walking into your room. I had a student that, actually I've had many students that had the exact same story where they've had years of telling the parent, please don't disrupt me, please don't disrupt me, but they still do and they sort of yell across the room and the door and the personal privacy thing is just kind of like a meaningless concept. And they've struggled for years and years and years. Just try it. Just try to tell them, I need to focus and then put up the sign. Just see if that works. And you may be surprised how much it works because it's not necessarily that they're intending to distract you. It's just that they are engaging in their own habit without realizing it. Another common example I see is in corporate situations or people that work in a shared office space where there's lots of distractions back and forth either through online messages or actual sort of shoulder taps. So in a physical office environment, you can actually use something like a post-it system. This is one that I often teach to more corporate professional clients is to set up a consistent post-it system. Have different color post-it notes and put it up on the monitor or screen or somewhere like a cubicle divider thing next to you. When a person is about to come up to talk to you, if there's a certain color post-it note that's on there, They know this person is in focus, I shouldn't disturb them. It acts as a visual break, as a do not disturb indicator in real life. And obviously in online formats, there are literal do not disturb features that are built into most software. So utilize these things and optimize your environment and make a list of all the environmental optimizations that you will be making to make sure that you are not triggered into negative behaviors, that you are removing aspects that are gonna distract you. You're planting things that will trigger good behaviors and you're optimizing your environment to make bad behaviors more difficult to access and good behaviors easier to access. One great tip that I'll give you for blocking websites is installing an app blocker. But if that's not enough, what you can actually do is you can actually go into your router settings. It's usually you type in an IP address for the router. You can go into the router settings, log in as the admin and turn on parental controls. Parental control features are available in most commercially available routers. And what that will do is it means that you can select which devices when connected to that network can access different things on the internet. And you can block certain words from appearing in the string. So even on something like YouTube, if you're searching for something, that appears in the string. So if you don't wanna search for videos related to I don't know, let's say you don't wanna search for videos related to cats, then have the word cats in the search string and block it. So every time you search for the word cats, it's gonna be blocked. You can set up different websites to be blocked depending on the actions that you are trying to prevent yourself from accessing. and then change the password to your router admin, make it really, really long and impossible to remember, write it down on a physical piece of paper and give it to someone else like your parents. Now you can't even change the settings even if you wanted to. Another pro tip is that you can access certain websites like YouTube in incognito mode. If you're accessing a website in incognito mode, especially something like YouTube, the algorithm doesn't know what to optimally recommend you for you to be interested in watching it. So when you log on, you just see all these irrelevant music videos and stuff that you don't care about. And so you're much less likely to get distracted from that homepage. An alternative is if you're looking for a video, search for the video in Google first. and use the videos feature to find the video that you're looking for and get linked directly to it. In some cases, when you search for it, Google will be able to show you that video in the search results directly. So you don't even have to go off into the website. So you're now not even getting distracted by the comments or the sidebar or anything like that. So here are a few tips that you can use to just help yourself get less distracted, as well as obviously the common ones removing desktop icons things like that this section on anticipated barriers and what we can do to avoid them is as you can see a very lengthy session it's a very involved process many of our students will spend hours really thinking through this to figure out exactly the initial scope of their limiting habits and what they can do, what type of scripted actions work. Often the mistakes that are made is that not enough limiting habits are identified, scripted actions require too much motivation, and environmental optimization is not done sufficiently. These are the most common three things. If you avoid these common mistakes, your chances of success at achieving your goal are gonna go through the roof. So obviously, if a goal is challenging, like I said, it's gonna have barriers, but if those barriers are not in place, if you're able to overcome those barriers with pre-programmed responses in advance, your path forward is gonna be much more predictable and much smoother. For me, now having used this type of approach for years, I wouldn't imagine Ever, for any goal I take even moderately serious where I feel like I'm not willing to risk the consequence of failure, I would never even imagine going about creating a plan or setting goals that don't think about these limiting habits. Maybe because I've already tried doing it the old way and have encountered so many attempts of disappointment and failure. But if you haven't encountered that amount of failure and disappointment yet, you don't always have to learn through the experience. Sometimes you can just take someone's word for it and put in the steps yourself so that you don't make all of the same mistakes.
