fundamentals_2.content: let's talk a little bit about the basics of exam technique now remember these are just the fundamentals we will be elaborating on these much more in the course to come but this is just to make sure that any big gaps are filled in with your exam technique so that you're not left stranded until you learn those techniques maybe a month from now so these are the fundamentals get used to them and get competent at them and then later on we will be upgrading and replacing them as part of the system but There are a few things that you need to make sure you do when you are sitting an exam or the immediate preparation bef ore the exam to ensure you have the optimum performance possible and give yourself the best chance of getting a high result. And one of those things is sleep. So you have to get sufficient sleep before the day of a test or an exam. And the reason is purely because there is something called sleep dependent memory consolidation, which we talk about later in the course as well. But long story short, it means that you will not be able to use the information you studied until you actually go into a deeper level of sleep. where it's taken to higher level courtesies, and then you can use that for your test and exam. So it means that all the time you spend learning material may actually be in your brain, locked, unable to be used. which is obviously a huge shame and a waste of time. So if you're going to cram, cram beforehand. If you're going to do all nighters, do them beforehand. But at least the day before your exam or test, make sure that you're getting sufficient sleep, more than enough sleep. Be conservative about it. The additional sleep is going to help you perform better. And also, sleep is also related to your level of cognitive functioning and executive processing, which includes problem solving. So if you're sleep deprived, you can get up to a 10 to 20 point IQ drop due to sleep deprivation. So the last thing you want when you're walking to exam is to not only be unable to use the information that you studied, but also to be literally dumber and less able to solve problems or answer the questions in the exam. So sleep is one really major one. And another thing that is very common and a lot of students will ask about is the idea of double checking and the system behind, should I check the answer? Should I change my answer? What should I do? So there's a few different schools of thought around this and some of the older research and a lot of the common understanding of it was that you shouldn't change your answer if you were to double check it. So it was against double checking. However, some more recent research that's coming out suggests that actually that may not be so true and that double checking can actually make you change to a better answer. The system that I use and the one that I do generally recommend for most students is to do what I call priority checking, which is very simple. It just means when you're answering the question, try to go at a pace where you have a little bit of time left over at the end of your test or exam to go through and check some of your questions. but keep your pace relatively consistent. So for example, if you have 10 questions and you're spending one minute per question, then don't spend five minutes on one question because if within that first minute you aren't able to figure out what the right answer is, there's a chance that you could just be tunneling in further and further and it's actually getting more and more confusing for you. and you might exit the exam and 20 minutes later think, oh man, the answer was so obvious, when you bring yourself out of that tunnel vision. And that's a very dangerous place to be and I think we all know what that tunnel vision actually feels like. So the system that I use, and it's one that I've developed throughout the many thousands of questions that I've answered through the many exams that I've had over the years, is to keep a pace that allows me to get through each question comfortably, being relatively sure about the answer for each question. But at the end of the exam, I still have time left to go back and revise the question that I didn't feel so confident on. And the ones I didn't feel so confident on are the ones that I needed to spend longer on. So let's say if I'm trying to do one question every 30 seconds and there's a certain question that I've already spent a minute on, I'm going to give my best possible answer. I'm gonna leave a little star or something next to it. And then at the end, I'll come back to see if I'm able to answer it. And often just taking that time away from the question and coming back to it at the end, just opens up your mind a little bit more. And when you look at it again, you think, oh, actually I'm able to think about this a little bit differently. There may have been a question 20 questions later that allows you to understand that first question a little bit more easily. And so overall, maintaining pace means you're likely to cover all the questions properly, but because you are keeping a list of priorities, of which questions that you wanna come back to when you do get the chance, you're giving yourself the chance to change the answer for some of those that you did feel weak on without wasting too much time and increasing the risk of changing correct answers into incorrect ones. Because if you felt pretty certain the first time you answered it, chances are that when you look at it, you're gonna be changing to a wrong one after that. So we use a little bit of a combination, and I just call this priority checking. Maybe it's already got a different name for it, and if you do, you can let me know, but this is a system that tends to work pretty well. And then the third thing that we can do for exam execution is just in the immediate preparation before the exam is trying to get inside the mind of the examiner and try to prepare for your exam in the way that you think the examiner would test you. And so this is quite different from doing just practice questions. When you do practice questions, you rely on questions that already exist to help you answer different types of content. When I say think like an examiner, what I want you to do is take the information that you know, and I want you to think, if I were to make a really challenging exam or assessment, if I wanted to throw some curve balls to these students, how would I do that? And what that would actually do, it may feel like a subtle change, but it puts your mind in the perspective of someone who is using the information in a way that you know you may not be applying that information. So you may know that you're really good at answering questions that are like A, B or C. But because you're deliberately looking for the way that they might test you, that you don't know about, you will open yourself to thinking about type number D, and that could be the type of question that they throw as a curveball. And it's an excellent revision strategy to use. Even when you learn the advanced strategies later on, you should still keep doing this. Put yourself in the mind of an examiner. Think, how can I really challenge my knowledge? How would I really trick students out by using this information? Create some questions for yourself. Maybe create questions for, if you've got a study group, between your friends, make maybe a list of 40 or 50 different questions about a specific subject and give them to your friends. and swap them and use that as a way of challenging your learning, you'll actually find that not only can it be more effective than past papers, yes, it can actually be more effective than past papers, but also trains your brain to think about the information from the instructor's point of view, which makes it easier to study in the future, because you're starting to think about information from multiple different angles and it makes it easier to understand some of the more difficult concepts. And it's also a technique that you can use through university. A lot of university papers, you don't have access to that many past paper questions, or they change their style of questioning very often. And so relying on past papers is actually not a good idea for a lot of university papers. Not all, but a lot of them. And so being able to do this very comfortably because you've practiced it throughout high school is gonna be a really great weapon in your arsenal that you can carry in through university. So that was number three. And then number four is actually just a very simple technique that we can use In an exam, I was always the type of student that got really nervous in an exam and I would feel myself tunneling in a lot. I hyped it up too much for myself in my mind. And one thing that you can actually do in this situation is simply take long, slow, deep breaths. And this may sound very hippie and not what you're expecting, but there's actually a very physiological reason why this works. And the reason is because there is a nerve that's connected to your diaphragm, which is a muscle that's used to help you breathe. When you take a slow, deep breath, it actually forces your diaphragm to contract which activates a certain nerve that's connected to it. And that nerve directly invokes what's called the parasympathetic response. And the parasympathetic response is otherwise known as the rest and digest system. It's the opposite of the sympathetic response which is the fight or flight. So when you're feeling anxious, What's normally happening is that your brain is sensing danger and that danger is in the form of an exam Not like some kind of predatory lion or something in the bushes, but it's in the form of an exam and your brain is sensing real Danger, even though there isn't real danger. Well, I mean, I guess you could argue that point but you know There isn't something that's life-threatening. I guess you could argue that point as well. But anyway, it's not a lion It's not gonna kill you So your brain is sensing this danger. And now from your point of view, you need to calm your brain down. And it's very hard to do it because it's a biological response that's activating the sympathetic fight or flight response. Deep breaths forcibly deactivate the fight or flight response. And in fact, if you're feeling really anxious, when you take slow, deep breaths, it's almost impossible not to calm down. Now, when you stop taking deep breaths, you may actually end up working yourself up again. but it stops you from going into that spiral. It gives you a bit of, well, breathing room to just relax a little bit, get a bit more comfortable with the environment, get a bit more comfortable with where you're sitting, look around you, get out of your own headspace a little bit, and it gives you the mental freedom to just relax a little bit and open your mind because that also is gonna allow you to be more creative and a bit of problem solver because your brain is less tunneled and you're actually gonna perform better in the exam. Like I said before, when you leave an exam and you think, man, I knew the answer to that. Why couldn't I think of that? A lot of that is due to the fact that you are in this tunnel vision and that can be driven by anxiety. I know that for myself it was, and it was really a big problem for me while I was going through high school. I only really got a handle on it while I was in, my mid to later years of university and even still it will affect me and this is a technique that I consistently will still use and you can actually use this for any time you're feeling anxious but in the context of an exam obviously very very relevant so have a little bit of a breathing routine maybe 10 seconds of very slow deep breathing especially if you exhale going out really, really slowly, really, really deep. And if you feel yourself trying to breathe a little bit faster, you just force it down. You force your breathing to a slow until you feel yourself calming down a little bit more. You give yourself that little bit of mental space. You sit back a little bit and then you can reengage with a task at hand at maximum focus with a calm, clear and confident mind so you can get the results that you worked so hard and deserve.

So that was number three. And then number four is actually just a very simple technique that we can use In an exam, I was always the type of student that got really nervous in an exam and I would feel myself tunneling in a lot. I hyped it up too much for myself in my mind. And one thing that you can actually do in this situation is simply take long, slow, deep breaths. And this may sound very hippie and not what you're expecting, but there's actually a very physiological reason why this works. And the reason is because there is a nerve that's connected to your diaphragm, which is a muscle that's used to help you breathe. When you take a slow, deep breath, it actually forces your diaphragm to contract which activates a certain nerve that's connected to it. And that nerve directly invokes what's called the parasympathetic response. And the parasympathetic response is otherwise known as the rest and digest system. It's the opposite of the

fundamentals_2.name: In class techniques.txt
fundamentals_2.content: So let's now talk about some techniques when you're in class to maximize your focus and concentration and engagement and even your level of enjoyment. What we want out of class is to feel like we got a lot of really good learning done. We don't wanna feel like we're just more confused about what's going on. We wanna have a pretty good understanding of this topic. Now, remember these are just fundamental techniques and so we will be, especially these sets of techniques, we'll be significantly upgrading and replacing them in the system later to come. But if these are new to you, then please get used to them, apply them, practice them. They will give you an immediate benefit. But the idea with these fundamentals is it just gets you used to experimenting a little bit. It gives you some immediate tangible tips that you can use before you're studying straight away, there's almost no real learning curve to any of these techniques. They will give you an immediate form of benefit. Now, it may not be the level of benefit that we want to achieve by the end of this, but it will be a benefit nonetheless, and you're not gonna have to wait, let's say, three to four weeks until you learn the upgraded version of the technique, and in the meantime, you're falling asleep in class. Obviously, we don't want that. Few things that we can talk about when it comes to in-class technique. Now, one of the things that people generally underestimate is the power of pre-study and learning a little bit of the material beforehand. I'm not talking about getting a full understanding of the entire topic before you sit in class so that you're not even needing to listen to what the teacher says or the lecturer says if you're at university. What I'm talking about is simply getting a familiarization with a few basic elements of the topic so that we know how to think about this. the in-class performance is actually really significantly impacted by how familiar you are with the content. So for example, if I put you in a fourth year university lecture, you are probably not gonna be able to follow along very well unless you're in fourth year or above university. However, if I put you in a year eight class, Even if you technically forgot what you learned in year eight, it's gonna be very easy for you to pick up on it because you have so much other knowledge that's related to it. So when you learn something new, it's like, oh, that's kind of obvious because you can relate it to so many other things. This is an idea that we're actually going to talk about and elaborate on a lot more coming up. But one of the first things that you can do is train yourself to do a topic level pre-study where you take the entire topic of what you might be learning for the next, you know, maybe few weeks even, and you just try to isolate what are the three or four main ideas. And they may be the same as what your teacher tells you. They may be the same as what's in a study guide or whatever other resource you have available. It doesn't really matter. Or you may find that actually there's another way of grouping it together. There may be seven main concepts that you find in your resources. But when you really think about it, you might think, you know what, this one and this one are kind of the same sort of thing. It's sort of talking about the same thing. And you can start grouping it together. So I challenge you to find just three or four main ideas. And working with students from the range of curriculums within not only Australia and New Zealand, but also within university levels and IB, which is obviously international, multiple other countries, I've never really found a situation where the topic couldn't be broken up into roughly three or four main concepts. or groups of concepts and basic ideas. So that's what you need to do in the pre-study. You're not aiming to read and learn absolutely everything about everything. You don't need to memorize anything really. All you're doing is you're just kind of going through the material enough and just thinking like, okay, what is the main idea here? So for let's say three weeks of class material, which is an entire topic, let's say, that whole topic might take you a dozen hours to study. Normally doing it this way, you might only spend 30 minutes to one hour. So you'd spend 30 minutes to an hour just reading through all the different class material, the lecture material or whatever it is in there in the topic. And then by the end of that, you're able to say, okay, so this is a topic that really talks about these three or four main ideas. And then on top of that, you want to think about how do those ideas kind of relate to each other? What's the relationship between there? You're forming a basic backbone. You want to say, all right, this is a topic which talks about this idea of A and how without it, you really can't get B. But then because of it, you get C occurring and then D happens. So, a few ideas and the relationship or flow of ideas between those basic groups of concepts or concepts. And the reason this is so beneficial is because primes your brain and priming is a proper terminology to use here and that it gives your brain a way of thinking about this to help it make sense of what you learn in class. So when you learn something in class, now there's an anchor point that it can attach to. You can think, oh, this is new information, but I can see how it is kind of related to this. It gives the information a place to belong and when we learn more about the learning psychology we see how that can drastically change that slope of the forgetting curve that we talked about before and it can increase your retention by a lot so it's an incredibly powerful technique that you can use it's very easy to do you don't have to stress too much about it like i said just 30 minutes to an hour going through an entire topic's worth of of content, just figuring out the main ideas and groups of ideas and how they're related to each other and then take that knowledge with you into your class and you will find that the class starts to make a little bit more sense and hopefully you start having a few more questions about what the teacher is saying, which is a sign that you are activating more innate curiosity-driven learning processes, which are actually from a neuropsychology point of view, more effective and increase your attention compared to other things that are not curiosity-driven like rote learning. So that was the first thing, which is just doing a little bit of basic pre-reading or priming for the three to four main ideas and the relationships between them. The second thing that we can do, now this is truly when you're sitting there in class, is always make sure to paraphrase what's being said. Now, a little bit of a disclaimer here, this particular technique about paraphrasing, we're actually very quickly going to start up because there's a lot of stages that we can upgrade this towards and it gets more and more efficient as time goes on. But in the early stages, if you're not already, and I know a lot of you will be, but if you're not already, make sure that you're not just copying verbatim. So there is research that says that people that copy verbatim, and verbatim means word for word, they have a lower level of retention and their test results and exam results are actually worse. And this is another reason why typing your notes is actually not very effective because typing makes it so easy to get information down because it's so much faster than writing. that it stops you from needing to filter out what you're going to write down and what you're not. And as a result, your notes end up being more word for word. And so the closer it is to being word for word, the lower the level of retention. And this is actually what's called passive learning. And you may have heard of the difference between passive versus active learning. Passive learning means that your brain is not really switched on and so even though you are taking notes, there's not really any learning going on in here. It's just like a mindless activity like kind of you if you're like washing dishes and you're really paying attention, you're just kind of doing the action. That's sort of what's going on is that you're sitting there, you're listening and you're writing stuff down, but there's no real learning going on in your head. And yes, you can go and take that information and learn it properly afterwards, if you don't have much content to study or if you have a lot of time available for you during the week, then that's fine. But what you'll find and what the university students out there will know is that that's not really possible at a university level, especially for more difficult content or even if you are doing a difficult type of curriculum in high school or a difficult subject, Information is usually thrown at you quite quickly. You've got a lot of stuff to study and if you need us then spend time afterwards to study it again to actually understand and gain proper learning from that. that is a lot of time wasted which you're not going to be able to afford. And even if you can afford it, I would personally rather spend that time, you know, socializing or playing games or picking up new hobbies or just sleeping more or like saying hi to my mom or something like that. There's better ways to use that time. And so you don't want your words to be verbatim. You want to make sure you're always paraphrasing and if possible paraphrase to the simplest and most concise form as as is possible for you. And you should feel mentally there's a bit of a challenge here. And that's a good sign. You should feel that, man, this is requiring quite a lot of concentration for me to take this information in and really condense it down and really quickly make notes of it. If you feel that, that's actually a good sign because it means that the time spent in class is contributing towards increasing your attention. And it's also a foundation skill that you need to develop Otherwise, you're really going to struggle with a lot of the more efficient techniques out there when it comes to note taking because word for word note taking caps off in efficiency very, very early. Okay, so we've covered number one, the idea of pre-study in that very specific way, topic wide, not lesson wide. And then number two, the idea of being much more active and engaged with your learning throughout extreme paraphrasing as much as possible and not typing your notes. And again, there's going to be study gurus that are out there that say that's not the case. But you know what? They probably can't justify their recommendation. They probably will just say, oh, it's much faster for me to type than it is to write. Well, I'm telling you, it's much faster for you to learn by not typing. than it is to type and get notes down without having actually learned anything. And not only is there research on this, but you ask any of the students in our community, they will know what we're talking about because they will have felt the difference that it makes when you have an effective freehand note-taking strategy. And on that note, we move on to point number three is try to represent some ideas or relationships between ideas with arrows and spatial arrangement. So the thing is that you can actually represent a lot of information without having to write. So I want you to imagine something like this. A leads to B and then B leads to C and D. So we have A and B leading to C and D. If we were to represent that in a mind map or a flow chart or using arrows and spatial arrangement, we would say, okay, A is a pretty big topic. I'm going to put it right here. B is a pretty big topic. I'm going to put it right here. C is a little mini piece. I can put it right in the middle and I can put D directly underneath it. And when you look at the page, you're going to see A, B, little C, and then D. And you'll see the flow of information going down the page. That actually gives you a lot of information and it uses a different part of your brain which is involved in spatial arrangement of things. This is why sometimes you can remember where on your notes information is but you don't actually remember what it is in itself. Or you remember generally the position of items in your room or something but you don't actually remember exactly where it is. Your brain's ability to process information in space on the page, the position of things on the page actually provides it with a lot more cues that allow to record that information much more easily and increases your attention. Now, when we add arrows into the mix, it makes it even easier because let's say the relationship between A to B and then C to D in words, we would have to say A is a process that allows B to occur, which leads to C. And once C is occurred, D is able to occur. That's quite a long list of things and when you write it, the only way that you can really process that information is to read it from start to finish. You can't really look at an entire paragraph and just instantaneously take that information in. Visual processing is tens of thousands of times faster than your ability to process written words. So if you have A arrow B arrow C plus D, that communicates the relationship almost exactly the same way and you're not really losing any detail there, nothing that was actually really meaningful at all, but your ability to take that in and also to revise it as much faster because when you look at your notes, you will be able to process, take that information in and work with it literally tens of thousands of times faster than if you were to read things. This is actually something that we already know. For example, if you've ever read Lord of the Rings, JRR Tolkien spends like an entire page describing a single door. When you watch Lord of the Rings the movie, you just see the door and you think that's a quite a fancy door and you know about the door and the characteristics of the door and the lighting and all of that sort of stuff. The picture speaks a thousand words. Actually, the picture speaks like 10,000 words. And if you're writing 10,000 words with something that can be represented with arrows and spatial arrangement to form kind of a picture type understanding, you are going to be able to study that much faster. And again, this is something that students often find very difficult when they enter into university. because you're so used to writing everything out. So it's a good thing to start breaking away from this early on. It's very easy to do. Take it to a level that you're comfortable with. I'm not saying replace everything that you're writing with these crazy diagrams or anything like that. All I'm saying is that when you see there's a certain relationship, instead of writing it as a paragraph or a list, think, can I express this through some arrows or a mini mind map? Is there another way that I can represent this information that will make it a bit visually clearer for me to see and make kind of a bit of a story or a flow or an image out of it? And always think about that and start getting used to that. And the earlier you practice this, the easier it will be to transition into some of the high power, high efficiency techniques that we're going to teach later on. So, so far we've got pre-study, we've got the idea of active learning through paraphrasing and also now not only just paraphrasing but introducing the idea of this visual element, arrows and spatial arrangement as well. Now again, you don't have to be perfect at this. What I want you to do is give it a go. I want you to experiment with it. I want you to try, feel it out. Don't worry if you feel like it's not giving you the perfect result. The most important thing is that you give it a go, You see how you find it. And if there's something that you think you can do to improve, give that a go as well. It's a very low risk thing that we're trying to do here. You're not going to fail a paper by doing this. You're not even going to impact your exam result negatively by doing this. We're simply getting you to just taste a little bit of these different things. And if it works for you, great, keep going. And if it doesn't, think about why, experiment a little bit. And then after that, you can ask us why. You can say, we've tried this. and it doesn't work, what would you suggest? And then eventually as you move through the course, you will actually again upgrade all of these skills. Everything that I talk about in these foundations, we will upgrade all of that eventually anyway. So for now, just get your feet wet, just get a little bit into it, just start experimenting a little bit and start building a bit of that basic competence. And the final thing that I want to add on here is the idea of listening first. So this kind of is a bit implicit. So if you're paying attention to the previous stuff, it's kind of impossible to paraphrase information without having listened to it first. And I know it's tempting, especially in a fast-paced lecture or a class to be constantly writing things down or to be typing things as the person is speaking to you. But this is actually really disadvantageous for your learning because not only is it not activating your brain, so it means that you're spending time sitting there listening to this person and you're not even learning from it. All you're doing is just trans, you're like a human photocopier, like a transcribing machine. and you're not getting the learning that you could be getting out of it. So it's time wasted, but also is not training your brain to be able to learn through that auditory type of learning. And remember what we talked about before, training yourself to learn through different modes is extremely beneficial so if you know that you struggle to listen and really learn through listening training yourself to do that is going to be enormous benefit to you for for later in life i mean imagine if you could learn extremely effectively, whether that's spoken, whether it's experienced, whether it's read, whether it's seen or visualized, all of these methods, they are under your domain. Your ability to work with and manipulate information and turn it into something that you can really use and get those top marks in the tests and exams, is so much higher than someone who's very fixed and only is able to work in a certain way. And again, the research is very clear. Unless there's some kind of medical abnormality with your brain, and this is not a meme, so it's probably not going to be you, but actually even with learning disabilities like ADHD or dyslexia, it's actually still the case that you can learn through all these different modes and you are able to train yourself to learn through these different modes. So it's definitely a worthwhile investment. So four things that we covered the pre-study, the paraphrasing, the arrows and the spatial arrangement and the listening first before you write. And again, most importantly, don't worry about getting it perfect. The idea is to just give it a go, get a taste for it, experiment with a little bit. Once you're comfortable, start experimenting a little bit more, a little bit more, getting a bit more comfortable so that later on when we teach you other techniques that are more advanced, then it will be a lot easier to use those techniques and incorporate them and upgrade your system even more efficiently.

fundamentals_2.name: Notes and processing.txt
fundamentals_2.content: At this stage we know a little bit more about effective note-taking. So I want to introduce a transitionary or sometimes what I call a crutch step that can help you transition away from really linear note-taking to something that is more deeply processed. A lot of students have very strong habits of writing very, very linearly. And because there's a sense of security attached to that style of note-taking, it can be very difficult to move away from it. Not only do you have to deal with the difficulty and burden of actually processing the information, activating a little bit more of that higher order learning and higher order thinking again, which is desirable difficulty, We also have the other difficulty of having to fight against all of our old habits and that insecurity, which There's also desirable difficulty because we have to make that transition, but it doesn't make it easy. Desirable difficulty doesn't mean easy difficulty. It's still challenging. And so we can make that a little bit easier by instead of having a big jump from linear to non-linear where we're trying to create all these great maps and relationships and groups and things straight off the bat, if you really, really, really struggle with that, and I would recommend that you do try to push yourself If you're finding that it's just too much to handle, then you can ease your way through with this simple technique that I call collecting and processing. It's very straightforward to do. Really anyone can do this straight away. You simply take your page and you divide the page in half. On the left side, we're going to do our collecting and on the right side, we're going to do our processing. And so here's how it works. The collecting step is completely linear. It's essentially a passive process. This is the linear aspect. It's not very effective. However, it does allow you to bridge your current level with where you want to get to. So collecting simply looks like how you normally would think about it. It's just linear sets of nodes, maybe some bullet points on a few things that you've jotted down, something like that. The processing happens every block or chunk, and this is quite a subjective thing. You might want to do it for every two or three concepts down the page, or if the concepts are really dense, maybe you break down just a single concept. It really just depends on your own feel for it and competency, remembering that the entire purpose of this technique is just to start transitioning you to more and more non-linear until we fade away the linear aspect altogether. But when we are first doing it, let's just say, for example, that we've got three concepts here. We've got one concept introduced here in red. We've got another concept introduced over here. And then maybe we've got another one that is introduced here as well. And so we've got these three different concepts. And this could be done during class. It could have been done maybe when you're studying by yourself, whenever it is. And let's say that we spent just the last 15 minutes studying. on this collecting part. And by the way, this number 15 minutes is completely arbitrary. It's just to give you a sense of reference. Like you're not going to be collecting for two hours is the idea. You're just going to be doing it piece by piece. So for example, maybe collecting for anywhere between two or three minutes all the way through to 10 or 15 minutes. And then you're going to take that and you're going to try to create a little bit of a nonlinear representation of the same information. So it's just giving yourself a little bit of a test. You know, you're just dipping your toes. So you might say, okay, I'm going to take these concepts and I'm going to try to arrange them like this. I feel like maybe there's some relationships that look like this. It seems like uh these two things are contributing to this one okay and that's it and then maybe there were a few points coming off from this one as well that you might jot down and there's maybe uh three points coming off of here that you jot down as well so now you can see that it's not quite a fully fleshed out non-linear mind map or chunk map or concept map or anything like that but it is starting to have that sort of flavor to it and over time you want to do more and more of these concepts so for example you may end up eventually having an entire page of non-linear notes maybe there's four or five concepts and main ideas on this and then you want to start being able to map all of that onto this as well and you'll feel a few things happen when you start to do this the first thing that you'll notice is that this process becomes easier that's natural because you're developing the skill your method of thinking is getting better your higher order thinking skills are improving actually getting smarter. Crazy, right? Not that hard actually. The second thing that you'll realize is that you're starting to become frustrated at using this collecting and processing step because you'll start feeling like the collecting is just a waste of time. That is perfect. You've won. You did it. Once you start feeling that, fade away the collecting more and more and more. What you can do now is instead of dividing the page into two columns, you can have one thing where you're just doing the collecting and then you can have another page where you're just doing the processing on like a bigger piece of paper or on a tablet or something and then eventually you can fade away the collecting all together until you're left simply just doing the non-linear note-taking uh processing it straight away in a non-linear format and at that point the default base way that you're thinking about this is going to be through a non-linear form now here are a few things that can help you to transition through this process this is already easy enough But there's another thing that you can add onto this that makes it even easier. And this is what allows your brain to have a bit of a clearer focus in terms of how you can start grouping the ideas together. And actually, even if you find it easy enough, like if you're at the point where already you're able to just do it non-linearly, and that's not too challenging for you and you're not really struggling with it non-linearly, you feel like the collecting is already kind of frustrating for you, you're pretty happy to just jump into that, you can ignore this technique completely. You don't have to do it. fully optional, you can just go straight to being nonlinear, you do not have to do the collecting and processing if you're already happy to go and process nonlinearly to begin with. But even if you are in that situation, this next tip will probably help you. And it may be obvious to some people, but I'm just saying it because it isn't obvious to everyone. When you're thinking about how to group the information together, when you're thinking about how to create the relationships, don't just spam all the relationships on the page Arrows everywhere, big chaotic mess, prioritize the relationships and think about a few things like what is the purpose? What is the reason? If you're studying law or something to do with legal structures, what's the reason I'm learning this? Why is it important? Or if you are learning about science, how is this relevant? What does it lead to? What is the greater importance behind this? there are a number of ways that you can think about information and questions you can ask yourself that make you take a step back. And this is really the key part about this extra tip is that if you're too deep in all of the details, It's very difficult to find relationships and form a big picture structure. Because there's so much density that you're thinking. Like, if I said, "Okay, map out the shape of this forest." And you're busy counting the number of millimeters from one scratch on a tree trunk to another scratch on another tree trunk. And you're trying to sketch out every piece of bark on the forest floor. you are never going to map it out. And in fact, even if you do end up mapping it out, it's probably going to be wrong because you've lost perspective. If any of you have tried to draw or sketch something before and then you draw like a person's eye and you're like, oh, I'm going to try to make this eye really nice. and then you do the other eye and you're like, "Oh, I'm gonna make this eye nice and I draw the nose and I'm trying to make this nose really nice." And you zoom out and the face just looks like hideous, it's like malformed and everything because you didn't have that perspective. Or like the head is like really, really small and the body is like massive because you were zoomed in for too long. We do not want that. Zooming in focuses us on details and the details make us isolated and the isolation makes us fall into lower order learning and lower order thinking, which reduces our retention and reduces our memory and makes our brain less engaged and increases passive learning. Wow, what a negative spiral, right? It's like a deathly spiral that's just appeared. We do not want that. We want to stay in that higher order for as long as possible. There is a time for the details, but that time is not when you're forming your overall structure. If you don't have an overall structure yet, Form the structure first, and then go through the details and you'll be surprised. You will be shocked. at how much easier it is to learn the details when you have that structure in place. Take that tip away with you. We'll include some additional supplementary material below this video where you can see some of those questions that you can start asking yourself and just start getting a bit of familiarity with that. You'll see that this exact technique that we're teaching and this exact tip, how that then merges together with other techniques and becomes even more advanced later. But of course, and I'm always saying this, You can't merge them if we haven't mastered each component first. So just focus on this for now. Transition into that non-linear note taking. Get more used to that higher order thinking. start bringing it all together and then later on when we start bringing it together a little bit more it's gonna make more sense but for now this is a super super high-yield technique there's many of you will know I've taught similar things like this on my YouTube and it's one of those things that's easy enough that produces immediate positive results but again you have to go and if you are struggling with it then ask for feedback, get help. That's what we're here for. That's the purpose of the program. That's the difference between just videos that are out there, you can watch on YouTube versus a structured program is that the program is structured in the order in which you need to learn it and the way you need to learn it with the exercises and the supplementary material for you to learn it and the feedback and the practice opportunity that you can use to really accelerate your growth by folds faster. So make sure to ask for that feedback and engage with all the resources that we've got.

fundamentals_2.content: Let's talk now about study scheduling. And when I say study scheduling, I'm talking about big picture over the days, weeks and months. When do you revise the material? When do you study so that you can get the best marks possible? Again, as with all the lessons here, they are just fundamentals and we will be upgrading them significantly later in the course. So for now, the most important thing you can do is build a little bit of momentum, get your hands dirty and start experimenting with some of these very basic techniques, get comfortable with using these and that will allow you to transition into the more advanced techniques much more easily. The worst thing that you can do is just twiddle your thumbs, sit on your hands and not apply any of these techniques because either you're lazy or you don't feel like you've got enough time. None of these techniques are very difficult to use. they are all things that you can start using straight away they are all techniques that have a pretty immediate benefit even if it is in some of these cases a small benefit they're things that will allow you to start stacking up those marginal gains and improvements so make sure that you get a little bit of experience and your hands dirty with experimenting on this so We're talking about study scheduling and there are some very basic rules that we should be following, which is the first being the one day, one week, one month rule. And that is saying that it's very simplistic form of expanding gap space repetition. When you learn something, let's say that you learn it on a Monday, try to revise it again on the Tuesday, roughly a day later, maybe the Wednesday. But I would generally not leave it any further than that and then revise it again around a week later. So if you learnt it on the Monday, you might learn it on the Friday. If you learnt it on the Thursday, Friday is only a day later. Maybe you'll learn it the next So you don't have to get it down exactly, but as long as we're generally following the principle that the first revision happens within the first day or two, and the second revision happens round about seven to 10 days after the first one, and the second one happens around about three or four weeks after the second one. That ends up being a good general timing that you can use. Now, there are flaws in the system. Again, it is just a very basic strategy. It's not gonna work all the time. It's gonna be excessive sometimes for some subjects, and it's not gonna be enough for other subjects. And that's where some of the more advanced techniques are going to come in. But this is a really good foundation to start off. And actually, this foundation is what I used for really the first two to three years of university. I didn't outgrow it up until that point. And I was studying some serious content at the time. So I would suggest that for most people, this is gonna work reasonably well. And if you do have problems, we will solve them later in the course anyway. All you have to do is just get your diary or if you're using an app, just set aside a half a day at the end of every week as your weekly and then set aside another day or half day or a couple of days, depending on how much you need to study at the end of every month and keep that as repeating. So every let's say, for example, Friday or let's say that your weekly revision is done on a Saturday. So every Saturday you are revising what you learned for that week. And then every final Saturday and Sunday, you're revising everything you learned for the entire month. Now, again, it doesn't have to be an exact science. You don't have to time exactly when you're getting everything. As long as you're generally following these principles, it's going to be fine. The most important thing here is actually not the whole one day, one week, one month thing. It's the actually scheduling it into your day so that you know that at a certain time, this is what you need to do and keeping yourself accountable. There are a lot of students that we teach some of the more advanced scheduling tips to and then they start school and it just goes out the window. They just don't use any of those things anymore even though they know that using it is gonna make their life easier and then they start using it and then they think, wow, a lot of my problems have just been solved. There is a force of habit of how you schedule your time and use your time that we need to overcome. And the first step of that is keeping yourself accountable, having a diary, literally write in your diary, have it on your schedule and just keep yourself accountable. Keep your friends accountable and just follow these basic tips. The other thing is something that I've already talked about when it comes to the in-class techniques, which again, if you haven't watched that, make sure to go watch that and then come back to this, is the idea of doing pre-reading. So pre-study, so learning an entire topic before you learn it in class or learn it in lectures is extremely beneficial. And because it makes it easier for you to learn during the class itself, it's good because it actually saves you time later. So let's say that you spend one hour or even two hours studying an entire topic at a very basic level before the class or the lectures begin. That means that during class and lectures, you're more focused, you're more attentive, it makes more sense, it's more relevant, your attention is higher, which means that you now need to spend much less time revising it later to catch up on all of this stuff. so you will easily save that amount of time through your revision through not having to relearn or spend additional time or forgetting things that you know you studied once and then two weeks later you completely forgot about so i usually say that an hour of pre-study saves 10 hours later down the line between learning it and your test or exam and i've always found this to be generally true and sometimes even more than 10 hours are saved you never want to be so busy that you de-prioritize pre-studying you always want to make sure that you're on top of your pre-study game so again set this in your schedule maybe every monday morning maybe every sunday evening maybe every saturday you're going to spend some time to pre-study the material to come and that's gonna make your week a lot easier and you're gonna save so much time in the revision by doing that. So this is the hottest of hot tips is make sure that you schedule time for pre-study. And again, it doesn't really matter. You could do it on a Tuesday if you wanted to as long as you're scheduling time and you keep it consistent and you're getting it done. And then the final thing is the idea of the snowball effect with learning. And so we want to always leverage the snowball effect. And this actually ties into the idea of pre-study as well. When we combine pre-study techniques and in-class techniques and note-taking techniques and study scheduling one day, one week, one month, when we put all of that together, we get the snowball effect. And the snowball effect is one of the most powerful things in learning that you want to capitalize on. It simply says that the more you know about something, the easier it is to learn about that. And again, it makes sense because when you learn something that's brand new, that new information, your brain may not know what to do with it. When you're learning something that you already have some related ideas about, it's much easier to connect to that. You can create analogies. For example, you may be a really good swimmer and there may be a certain technique you use in swimming that allows you to understand a particular enzymatic process and chemistry more effectively. So, There are a lot of different analogies that can be created in learning. There are a lot of different relationships that can be formed. And ultimately, there are a lot of ways that new information can be made sense of much more quickly. And when something makes sense, you will tend to remember it longer and much more easily than something that is just purely rote learned. So, The snowball effect says you want to take your tiny little snowball and start that as early as possible. You don't want to be in a position where the first time you learn it is in class or in lecture. You want to have primed yourself beforehand with those techniques we talked about, building a little bit of those anchor points that new information can connect onto and we want to do that as much as possible we don't want to think by the end of the day i need to know everything we want to accumulate that snowball so that by the time you sit the exam you have everything because it's easier to accumulate knowledge when we're slowly adding more to it rather than trying to cram all of it in in a single space which is why another reason that it's easier to learn diligently over time than it is to cram information. Last minute, not only is it just risky in general, but also the cognitive processes that are involved are much less taxing and much less error prone. And also you'll find that especially among the science subjects, when you learn something about, let's say chemistry, it actually could make physics a little bit easier, which actually might make biology a little bit easier. There are certain things, bits of information that are a little bit transferable across the different topics. And you may have already felt this already. So when you're studying broadly, building little bits of knowledge, making sure you do the pre-study, learning a bit more and adding to that snowballing class, adding to it after the day, adding to it at the end of the week and then adding to it at the end of the month, you will find that that process is much easier and much more doable than just being thrown into the class and not knowing what's going on. Likewise, actually later on we'll teach techniques that take this idea and really systemize it so that you can be way ahead of your class by an enormous amount and potentially even study an entire year's worth of curriculum before halfway through the year, purely through self-study to the top 1% level, actually very doable when we leverage the snowball effect almost in a weaponized, systematic way. These are things that we will teach you, but you need to get, again, a little bit of experience with doing this. The snowball effect should occur naturally if you are doing the pre-study, you're using the in-class techniques, and you're following these study schedules. fundamentals_2.name: Revision.txt
fundamentals_2.content: So we've got a good understanding of how to do pre-study, at least to a basic level, and then that's gonna make a big difference in class, studying it afterwards. Every aspect that comes after the pre-study is changed because of the fact that we have a little bit better organization in our mind, and it's just a little bit easier to understand the information and put it in place. And again, we're gonna develop those skills as we go along. Now another skill that we're going to develop even further later on is revision, and that kind of forms the other side of it. So if pre-study is what changes and deviates the trajectory early on, then revision is what is there to make sure we're catching anything that we're missing and consolidating our information to a really comprehensive, safe level. So good revision takes a few steps. So again, these are just the basics. You need to be able to do this at least before you can move on, otherwise you simply don't even have the equipment, the gear, that's stable enough for you to climb anything meaningful. This is the minimum requirement for your gear. So let's have a look at what it takes to do good revision. So good revision is composed of a few steps. The first one is that you actually need to, well number one, you have to actually do it. So a lot of people will revise very sporadically. They will kind of just wing it, and what that means is that there isn't a level of consistency. There isn't really a system that they're following. You may relate to this if you are just spending time during the week to do your homework and study whatever you can. And then later in the week, if you manage to get some time on a weekend, you look through your notes again, or usually even a couple of weeks or few weeks later, or maybe just before the tests, you're just looking at everything and that's almost like the first time you've ever learned it, and you just cram in countless past papers to fill in all the gaps and somehow manage to get through. So it's a very weak, inconsistent model if something doesn't go right or if something is unexpectedly more challenging, it throws a huge spanner in the works and it's just not something that's safe to go off and it causes a lot more stress than is needed because you haven't done really any good preparation before that last minute cram period. So we obviously want to overcome that by having a set revision schedule. And a good revision schedule that you can follow just as a rule of thumb and again we're going to make this more advanced we're going to tweak it and make it more efficient and effective later on but for now this is a rule of thumb that's pretty safe if you don't already have any kind of revision system that you're using is to just do one weekly revision and then one monthly revision where at the end of every week you dedicate time to go over everything that you studied that week at the end of every month you dedicate time. Now you may not have that much time because of just time pressure to do really comprehensive reviews but even a little something is better than nothing and the idea is that the longer you wait the more you will forget and that's just the way that the brain works is that if we're not revising something there's a chance that we're going to forget it and if you learnt it really really well at the beginning then you forget a lot less but you're going to have a lot of time to do that. But having said that most people forget quite a lot in the initial period so just having that weekly revision tapers off how much we're forgetting which is going to save us a lot of time later on because we're not just relearning everything that we forgot. So having a weekly revision even a couple hours that you can spare on a weekend for going over the material for that week goes a long way and then same thing once a month going over everything that you've covered in that month. Now if you would have just do the monthly revision you would need to spend a really long time and it wouldn't actually be as effective when using these together as a pair. And that's because you'd have so much to do in the monthly revision because you didn't do the weekly revision that this would just take a really really long time. So we don't want that and you will have forgotten a lot already by that point so this monthly revision is a lot more doable when we actually do the weekly revision so it's important that we actually follow these steps that's the first thing. Just keeping a general schedule that you're following and a good rule of thumb before we start touching any of this in a more intricate individualized way is once a week and then once a month. So how do you actually do the revision well this is pretty well known sort of spectrum of revision that we know about which is often called recognition versus recall. And it's a little bit deeper than most people think so active recall is a term that gets thrown around a lot and you may have heard it yourself before but it's not the be all end all of techniques. And like I said these are all just basic general tips it's just the minimum gear required. Just because you're doing active recall doesn't mean that you're actually studying more efficiently and I've heard plenty of students say that they're planned for getting in that top 1% was to use active recall. So let me ask you this do you really think active recall is all it takes to get in that top 1% or top 0.1% now there is no active recall is something that basically any even slightly decent student and learner is going to do. So it's just the minimum requirement and actually there's another aspect that we're going to introduce much later in the course which is called reconstruction which is even better than active recall. And if you've already heard of active recall and you've heard your favorite YouTube and talk about it you'll be thinking wow I can't believe that there's something better than active recall but there is and we're going to be covering it much later on but you need to be able to do at least this first. So let me explain what each thing is so recognition is like being able to say take a $1 coin and look at the $1 coin and you're able to identify yes this is a $1 coin you look at a $2 coin you can say this is a $2 coin. fundamentals_2.name: Rote learning.txt
fundamentals_2.content: Now let's look at rote learning or just hard memorization. And it's a topic that is really under hot demand because a lot of people have problems with rote learning and memorization and they think that that's the main issue. But what you'll find when you go through the course is that you're probably over dependent on rote learning. And a lot of students think, well, there's no other way to actually learn this. But I've extremely rarely found that to be true. In almost all situations, especially in easier content, for example, high school level or early university level, there is not a lot of information that needs to be rote learned. So for the university level, maybe around about 20, 30% of the information ends up being rote learned. Whereas at the high school level, only around maybe 10% of the information actually needs to be rote learned. And common feedback is that students are very surprised once they learn these other approaches of learning how much rote learning they don't need to do. In fact, some students actually start getting insecure because they're not rote learning as much and they're so used to rote learning. But it just means that this technique is just that powerful. However, rote learning is necessary. However, it should be used as a last resort. You shouldn't be depending on rote learning to learn everything. You should be relying on these other types of techniques. However, I understand that as it is now, statistically speaking, the chances are you listening right now have a significant proportion of your learning through rote learning. Therefore, I'm going to teach you how to do it a little bit better so that when we teach you the later techniques, it's a little bit less stressful to swap these things out because you're not stressed so much and bogged down by the burden of having to rote learn so much. In the meantime, I do expect that you're going to have to rote learn a lot less as you learn other techniques throughout the course. However, appreciating that you do have a lot of rote learning, probably as part of your study system right now, I'll teach you some techniques about it. And one of the first techniques I'm going to teach you is the idea of basic association. And basic association is quite a simple thing to understand. It says that you can take something that you're trying to learn and you can associate that with something else and then if that something else in itself is more memorable, you can create this association between them. So here's an example. Let's say that you need to remember that a certain type of pigment is red in color when exposed to UV light. So instead of memorizing that and just saying this pigment is red under UV light exposure, you may think of something that's red. Okay, what do you think of that's red? For me, I might think of a Porsche. You know, a red Porsche. And then, the next thing I'll think of is UV light. Well, what do I think of when I think of UV light? Well, I might think of sunlight. And so in that case, I might imagine a Porsche, a red Porsche, under the baking hot sun. And I have to remember, baking hot sun, maybe in a desert, maybe the car is melting. Can you imagine that? And when you imagine that, now it's easier to associate that with thinking, okay, I remember this idea of like a melting red car under the baking hot sun in a desert. so what does that mean right sun uv light red carb red pigment and then you can remember there's a certain pigment that's red when exposed to uv light so that's how basic association works is you take a certain idea and you find a type of memorable imagery uh often something that's a little bit absurd something that is a bit crazy you don't want to think red pigment oh i'm going to imagine a red pigment under a UV light. Oh, I'm going to imagine UV light spectrum. That's just not memorable enough. You need to think of something that's a little bit outside of the ordinary, something that if you were to see that in real life, that would make you stop and stare because it's so, so crazy and out of this world. think of something that's a little bit absurd, and then associate a type of imagery to it. And you can use this for individual facts, you can use this for lists, you can use this for numbers even, by associating certain numbers with certain types of imagery. So for example, you might think of the number two, and the way that it looks, and think, oh, that looks kind of like a swan. And so you might think of a swan when you think of the number two. And you might think, there are two molecules of certain type of gas. Well, you might think of that type of gas and let's say it's sulfuric gas. Sulfur gas smells like rotten eggs, so you might imagine a swan that's carrying rotten eggs, or a rotting swan, or a really stinky swan, or a swan inside a giant rotting egg, or a swan inside a swamp that's just got all this bubbly geothermal activity. You can think of stuff like that, and all of this is very memorable. And you can actually draw these images into a notebook or mind map or on flashcards. And you can use that to make your memorization a little bit easier instead of just using words. Because remember, words are not very well processed. Our brain is honestly not very good at handling words much. worse than it is at handling images and visual information and imagery. So we want to leverage off that imagery as much as possible. And that's the idea of basic association is that we're associating something which is quite a specific fact to something else like imagery or some kind of crazy other thing. And we're actually going to take that and we're going to build on it much later and see how we can integrate that into another system. But for now, give that a go and you'll find that it's pretty fun and enjoyable and quite effective. Also, I'm sure you can still remember the swan and the rotting swamp, and I'm sure you can still remember the melting red Porsche under the sun. And when this video finishes, I'm sure you're going to remember exactly what those two things stood for as well. So that just goes to show, even without trying to remember it, You remembered it. That's the power of basic association. Other two little things is to use active recall. And I've been talking about active recall before, but if you don't know, active recall just means to not read something and then just like, you know, say it again or not just, you know, read it and then think, oh, yeah, I understand this because that's actually what's called recognition based learning. Recognition means that if I show you this pen, you know that this is a pen whereas recall means that you'd be able to draw the picture of that pen and know exactly what it looks like without having to look at it again. What you need is recall because you're tested on recall. Recognition is not really gonna get you anywhere, so you wanna make sure that you're revising through active recall. The active part of active recall is the bit that people get quite, misaligned on because they think that any type of recall is active the active is really talking about how much it activates your brain so active recall in terms of problem solving without referring to the information making mind maps or teaching based on your memory that is much more activating than for example just reading something closing it and writing it down again because that relies on only your short-term memory of that individual discrete thing And if you're gonna be using active recall, it's better to number one, have a long delay between the time you saw it and the time you need to recall it, usually at least around one or two days. And it's also better if you're able to recall it in a way that's different to how you remembered it. So for example, if you remember the facts, try to apply it to solve a problem based on recall from memory without looking at your notes. So those are ways that you can optimize active recall. Now I do talk about how active recall is not that great. It is good. It's just not something that you should over rely on because it is still about optimizing your memorization and the approaches we're going to talk about, you will start seeing that they just veer away from memorization altogether and it becomes less necessary and then the the final thing is the idea of using something to help you with spaced repetition with the study scheduling that i've already given you you've already got expanding gaps based repetition in there but the other thing that you can do is use digital flashcards and i say digital because digital flashcards is superior to physical flashcards for a multitude of reasons that's really, it's an incomparable, I find people that recommend physical flashcards laughable in how little they know about the neuropsychology of learning. It's just an atrocious idea. I would never recommend you use physical flashcards. And we're going to go into this a lot more in depth and explain why later on. But just for now, trust me, you definitely want to use digital flashcards, least of all because digital flashcards have a spaced repetition algorithm built into it. And if you use a flashcard app that's anything that's worth its space on your phone it will have the latener system built in by default or as a setting you can activate so if you're unsure just ask the developers or google it and see if the app supports that setting go into the settings and have a look if you can activate the latener system for spaced repetition and as long as you're following that you should be able to find the space repetition a little bit more manageable It's actually better not to do it in batches. So a lot of people will say, oh, Saturday is my flashcard day. I'll spend five hours doing flashcards. Or some people spend five hours a day doing flashcards, which is a whole other problem altogether, which again, we will talk about later. And right now, we're just not quite at the level where we can solve that. But a way to get over that is to just make sure that whatever flashcards you're using, you're trying to use basic association when you can to make it more memorable so that your ability to clear things is expedited. You're using all the other techniques I told you about in terms of in class and pre-study and study scheduling. That's going to allow you to retain things a little bit more easily and have less flashcards that you need and clear them more easily as well. And just manage that load. And again, don't stack them all together because it means that you have an overwhelming amount of flashcards to do in one day. It's very daunting. It's actually better to do them every now and again here and there throughout the day. For example, when you're on the toilet, You know, what else are you going to do? Pull out some flashcards, you know, do three, four, five flashcards. I don't know how many times you go to the toilet per day, but you can get a few flashcards done just on the bus waiting for a friend, you know, walking somewhere, you know, these little moments and pockets of time that you can be utilizing those actually really effective times to use flashcards. It's actually more effective psychologically speaking than doing it in batch and That's something called microlearning, which again, we will talk about and how you can systemize a process based on that later in the course as well. But for now, just a few guidelines in terms of using basic association when you can, using active recall and optimizing the type of active recall that you're doing so it's more activating and a little bit more challenging and applying the information differently, and using spaced repetition, not only through your study scheduling, but also through digital flashcards with Expanding Gap Space Repetition with the Leitner System, and a few tips on how to make sure that you're handling and managing your flashcards until the point comes where you don't even need to rely on most of them because your other approach to learning is more effective, which again, we will learn throughout the course. So remember, if it's overwhelming to do all of this at once, just do what you're comfortable with. Start small, baby steps. If it doesn't work, Give it another go, try to experiment, change a few things, try to make it work for you. If it still doesn't work, you let us know, we help you. And then as you progress through the course, you get better and better and better. You work, we work, we work, everything works. But it has to start from you diligently applying some of these and building that momentum which I think you've heard me say in every single one of these lessons, if you've been going in chronological order of what's on the page, but that's how important it is to just build that momentum. Don't dawdle. Don't just sit on your hands and just don't do anything about it. Really get in there. Start from tonight. Start using these things. Make a plan for yourself and really follow that schedule and make sure that you are just kicking off the first few weeks strong and you'll find that your results a month or two down the line, you'll thank yourself for it.
